practical_info.booking: Online ticket recommended in high season

activities.text: White Beach (Lefki Paralia), a few minutes by boat from Red Beach, is even more spectacular but less accessible. It's frequented mainly by small boats and organised excursions departing from Akrotiri port. If you have a vehicle, a rough track leads there but confirm conditions before you leave.

Hunt down a glass of Nykteri in a winery or taverna in the Akrotiri area. This Santorini white is produced by an old method: vintage happens at night (hence the name), then the wine undergoes slight controlled oxidation before bottling. It's rounder and more complex than classic dry Assyrtiko - if you've already tasted one, compare with the other to understand the island's vineyard range.

map_keywords: Museum Santorini of the Past Pyrgos

visits.text: Pyrgos - its full name is Pyrgos Kallistis, 'the most beautiful tower' - is Santorini's former capital, the island's chief town in the 18th century. Perched at 365 metres altitude on a hill at the island's centre, it offers 360° views over the caldeira, vineyards and the Aegean. It's one of Santorini's least touristy villages, which makes it one of the most pleasant. Built around a Venetian Kasteli in a defensive labyrinth against pirates, its narrow lanes are designed to disorient the enemy - you'll get lost too, and that's the charm. Allow 2 hours for a leisurely tour.

Climb to Profitis Ilias Monastery, which crowns Santorini's highest point at 567 metres. Founded in 1711 by brothers Gabriel and Joachim Belonias, it served as a refuge for revolutionaries during the 1821 Greek War of Independence. From the terrace, on clear days, you'll glimpse Crete to the south and the islands of Ios, Anafi and Amorgos - it's the only place from which you'll realise Santorini's crescent shape around the active volcano. Inside, a small museum displays icons, relics and ancient manuscripts. Ignore the antennas and military installations occupying part of the summit: they don't spoil the view.

The Church Theotokaki, at the Kasteli's heart, is one of Santorini's oldest, dating from the 10th century. The Museum of Icons and Ecclesiastical Artifacts, installed in the former Agia Triada chapel, displays remarkable religious relics. Pyrgos counts over 40 churches and chapels for a village of just hundreds of residents - some Catholic, the majority Orthodox. Don't hesitate to push open unlocked doors.

Tour the vineyards encircling Pyrgos to understand something unique worldwide: Assyrtiko vines are pruned into low baskets called kouloura, woven close to the ground to resist the island's violent winds and capture nocturnal moisture. The oldest vines are over 200 years old and ungrafted - phylloxera never reached Santorini thanks to its volcanic soil. Several nearby estates offer tastings with caldeira views: allow at least an hour.

activities.text: The climb to Profitis Ilias Monastery from Pyrgos takes roughly 30 to 40 minutes on foot. The road winds through vineyards and Mediterranean scrub. If you drive, parking is available nearby. Go late afternoon for the light - and to avoid the few tourist coaches that pass in the morning.

When tasting at a winery, ask to compare a classic dry Assyrtiko, a Nykteri and a Vinsanto side by side: these are three radically different expressions of the same base grape, and the contrast is striking. Specify that you want to understand the differences in method - most estates have guides or explanatory sheets. Allow at least 1h30 to avoid being rushed.

Dine in Pyrgos rather than heading back down to Fira or Oia: the central square's tavernas serve slow-cooked dishes - sfougato (courgette and cheese omelette), moussaka, stifado - at prices significantly lower than the caldeira. If you keep your eyes open, you'll find a few cafes run by year-round residents, where Greek coffee is served as it is everywhere in Greece except Santorini.

Orthodox Easter in Pyrgos is the most spectacular event in the local calendar: the village illuminates with thousands of candles arranged on walls and roofs to create a luminous procession. It's one of Greece's most beautiful Easter celebrations. If your trip coincides with this date, book accommodation months in advance.

visits.text: Kamari is the main beach resort on Santorini's east coast. Its black volcanic sand beach stretches nearly 2 km at the foot of the imposing Mesa Vouno mountain (369 metres). The sand, of volcanic origin, heats very quickly: bring sandals or water shoes if you're visiting in the height of summer. The pedestrian promenade running the beach's length is lined with tavernas and bars. The atmosphere is more family-friendly and affordable than Oia or Fira. Santorini airport sits 3 km to the north - for your final day, it's a practical base.

Ancient Thera, perched atop Mesa Vouno at 369 metres altitude, is an archaeological site founded in the 9th century BC by Dorian colonists led by King Theras. It was continuously inhabited until the Byzantine era. You'll find remains of an Apollo temple, a theatre, a gymnasium and numerous inscriptions. The climb from Kamari takes roughly 30 to 45 minutes on foot. There's also a winding road open to vehicles from 7:30am to 5:30pm, or a shuttle from the village. The combined ticket purchased in Fira is valid here.

On the path climbing to Ancient Thera from the Perissa side, don't miss the rock-cut chapel of Panagia Katefiani, carved directly into the cliff. The stop takes ten minutes and the view over the east coast and Kamari below is well worth the detour. Few visitors stop here - it's a calm and unexpected spot on an otherwise well-trodden path.

Perissa, on the far side of Mesa Vouno, is Kamari's sister beach - same black volcanic sand, slightly younger and more festive atmosphere. The two villages are separated by the mountain and linked only by the inland road (roughly 25 minutes' drive) or a paid boat shuttle. If you have time, make the round trip to compare the vibe.

activities.text: The hike to Ancient Thera from Kamari is the finest walk on the east coast. The path switchbacks through dry, aromatic vegetation, with progressive views over the bay. Leave early in the morning, bring water and take your time on site - the ruins are scattered across a windy ridge with panoramas of both the island's coasts.

If you haven't yet done the caldeira volcano excursion, now is the time: boats depart from Athinios port (12 km from Kamari) or Fira Skala port. You'll walk on the crater of Nea Kameni - still active, with visible fumaroles - then swim in the sulphurous hot springs of Palea Kameni, whose water takes on an ochre tint around the hydrothermal vents. Allow roughly 4 hours. Book in advance in July-August: spots go quickly.

Kamari's open-air cinema, operating since 1987, screens from May to September under the stars with blockbusters in original language subtitled in Greek. The place is convivial, with loungers and a snack bar. Arrive early for the best seats - it's a far more local way to end an evening than Fira's crowded bars.

Mavrotragano is Santorini's grand red, produced from an almost-extinct indigenous grape saved by a handful of passionate winemakers. It yields wines of deep colour, firm tannins and remarkable structure - very different from the light reds typical of the Cyclades. If you find it by the glass in a Kamari taverna, seize the chance.

Swimming at Kamari is a good option early or late in the day. The water is clear, supervised, and facilities (loungers, fresh-water showers) are well-organised. Heads up: black sand absorbs heat enormously - avoid walking barefoot between 11am and 4pm.

seo.h1_title: Santorini Road Trip: Caldeira, White Villages and Minoan Sites
seo.intro_paragraph: From Fira's cliffs plunging into the caldeira to the excavations of the Minoan city of Akrotiri buried 3,600 years ago, Santorini is far more than a postcard. Explore the medieval lanes of Pyrgos, the Venetian capital tourists forget, raise a glass of Assyrtiko in volcanic vineyards, and finish with your feet in the black sand of Kamari beneath Mount Mesa Vouno.
seo.seo_hook: Volcanic caldeira, buried Minoan city, legendary Oia sunset and white villages off the beaten path: Santorini reveals its secrets to those who take time to get lost.
featured_list: Fira: cliffside capital with direct caldeira views
Oia: 19th-century captains' village with Greece's most famous sunset
Akrotiri: Bronze Age Minoan city excavated since 1967
Pyrgos: medieval former capital surrounded by Assyrtiko vineyards
Kamari: black sand beach at the foot of Ancient Thera ruins

places_taglines.GR::fira: Volcanic capital suspended on the caldeira's edge since the 1956 earthquake
places_taglines.GR::oia: 19th-century captains' village with Greece's most photographed sunset
places_taglines.GR::akrotiri: Bronze Age Minoan city buried under ash 3,600 years ago
places_taglines.GR::pyrgos: Medieval former capital of Santorini perched among Assyrtiko vineyards
places_taglines.GR::kamari: Black volcanic sand beach resort at the foot of Ancient Thera ruins

highlights: Sunset from the ruins of Oia's Venetian castle
Akrotiri archaeological site, the Pompeii of the Aegean
Assyrtiko tasting in Pyrgos volcanic vineyards
Hike to Ancient Thera from Kamari's black sand beach

itineraries.title: Cyclades Island Hopping: Mykonos, Delos, Naxos, Paros & Antiparos
itineraries.subtitle: From cosmopolitan Mykonos to the marble islands—five days in the central Cyclades among ancient ruins, whitewashed villages, and quiet coves.
specialties: Kopanisti cheese - Mykonos's sharp, fermented PDO soft cheese
Louza - Spiced dried pork charcuterie from Mykonos
Amygdalota - Almond cookies coated in powdered sugar
Arseniko (Graviera) - Naxos's aged, sharp PDO cheese
Kitron - Unique citron liqueur distilled only on Naxos
Revythada - Slow-cooked chickpeas in olive oil, Paros specialty
Gouna - Sun-dried grilled mackerel, fishermen's mezze
Assyrtiko wine - Paros's dry, mineral white wine

visits.text: Chora, Mykonos's capital, is a whitewashed labyrinth said to have been designed deliberately to confuse marauding pirates. Wander its narrow lanes early in the morning before the cruise ship crowds descend on the port—after 10 a.m., the town belongs to the masses. The whitewashed houses with blue doors and balconies overflowing with bougainvillea set the quintessential Cycladic scene.

The seven windmills of Kato Mili overlook the port of Alefkandra—built in the 16th century by the Venetians to grind wheat shipped from neighboring islands, they turned thanks to the Meltemi, the north wind that blows nearly 300 days a year over Mykonos. Go in late afternoon when the orange light hits their whitewashed walls—it's the island's postcard shot. Watch out for gusts if you climb up to them; the wind can catch you off guard.

Little Venice gets its name from the 18th-century sea captains' houses built right at the water's edge, their colorful balconies practically dipping into the Aegean. It's one of the few places in the Cyclades where you can have sunset drinks with your feet almost in the waves. If you're here in the evening, book a table ahead in summer—the terraces fill up by 7 p.m.

Panagia Paraportiani is an oddity across the entire archipelago: not one church but five, built between 1425 and the 17th century, stacked and melded together to form a uniquely asymmetrical white mass. Listed as a Greek historical monument since 1936, it's usually closed inside—but it's the exterior you come to see. Visit at different times of day; the rounded forms change dramatically with the light.

The Archaeological Museum of Mykonos holds a selection of the ceramics and sculptures recovered from the island of Delos during excavations—notably amphorae, red-figure vases, and some funerary stelae. If you're planning to visit Delos the next day, this museum provides a good preview to help you understand what you'll see on-site.

activities.text: Stroll through Chora's lanes in the morning to catch the fish market near the old port—fishermen sell their dawn catch there at prices far more reasonable than in the tavernas. It's one of the last authentic moments of the town before mass tourism takes over.

The beaches of Paradise and Super Paradise, about ten kilometers south of Chora, are the island's party playgrounds: loud music, beach clubs, international vibe. Buses run there from Chora in summer. If you're after more peace, head to Agrari just next door—fine sand, fewer people, water just as clear.

In the evening, settle into Little Venice for aperitivo, then explore Chora on foot: luxury boutiques rub shoulders with jewelers and art galleries in a maze designed for getting lost—and that's intentional. Mykonos's nightlife is legendary across the Mediterranean; if that's your thing, the night here rarely starts before midnight.
Try kopanisti, Mykonos's sharp PDO cheese—a fermented soft paste with an intense flavor, spread on toasted bread with a drizzle of olive oil. You'll find it in local groceries and some tavernas.
Louza, Mykonos's spiced dried pork charcuterie, is eaten as mezze with cheese and olives. It's a Cycladic tradition you won't easily find elsewhere in Greece—ask for it in the old port tavernas.

visits.text: Delos, reachable in 30 minutes by boat from Mykonos's old port, is one of the Mediterranean's most important archaeological sites: the entire 3.5 km² island has been a UNESCO site since 1990, and no modern construction is permitted. According to Greek mythology, this is where Apollo and Artemis were born. The French School of Athens has been excavating here since 1873, and only a third of the site has been uncovered. Plan at least 3 hours on-site.

The Terrace of the Lions is Delos's most famous image: a row of marble lions from Naxos, offered by the Naxians in the 7th century BC to guard Apollo's sanctuary. Originally numbering 16, only 5 replicas remain on-site—the originals, worn by erosion, are preserved in the island's museum. One lion that ended up in Venice still sits outside the Arsenal, where it's been since the 17th century.

The Hellenistic houses in the theater quarter preserve some of the finest floor mosaics in the Greek world, notably the famous mosaic of Dionysus on a panther. These mansions belonging to wealthy 2nd-century BC merchants give a concrete sense of Delos at the height of its glory—one of the most active cosmopolitan ports in the Mediterranean.

The ancient theater of Delos, built between 314 and 250 BC, could seat between 5,000 and 6,500 spectators. Sit in the marble tiers and imagine the festivals honoring Apollo that took place here—the Delia, a panhellenic festival comparable to the Olympic Games, occurred every four years.

Mount Kynthos rises to 113 meters—not a major climb, but the view from the summit sweeps across the entire island and the surrounding Cyclades on clear days. This, according to tradition, is where Apollo was born. A sanctuary dedicated to Zeus and Athena once stood at the top. Bring good shoes—the path is rocky.

activities.text: Take the boat early in the morning from Mykonos's old port—first shuttles depart around 9 a.m. The site closes at 3 p.m. in low season, so check the hours before departing. There's no restaurant or water for sale on the island once the site opens: bring water and a snack, as the sun is merciless in summer.

practical_info.booking: Boat ticket + site entry purchased separately

activities.text: The Archaeological Museum of Delos, built in 1904 by the Archaeological Society of Athens and expanded twice since, houses the originals of the terrace lions, a 6th-century BC kouros torso, and a collection of mosaics, jewelry, and inscriptions. Allow 45 minutes for the nine rooms.

Amygdalota, small almond cookies coated in powdered sugar, are Mykonos's signature sweet, made in the monastic tradition. You'll find them in artisan bakeries in Chora when you return from Delos—they keep for several days.

visits.text: Naxos's Portara is the first thing you'll spot arriving by ferry: a massive marble doorway standing 6 meters tall on the islet of Palatia at the harbor entrance. Each of the four blocks composing it weighs about 20 tons—which explains why the Venetians, who pillaged all the other temple materials to build their Kastro, left it standing. The Temple of Apollo it belonged to dates to 530 BC and was never completed. Go at sunset when the sun descends exactly through the doorway's axis toward Delos, Apollo's birthplace.

Chora Naxos's Kastro was founded in 1207 by Marco Sanudo, the first Venetian duke of Naxos, on the town's highest point. Its outer walls are formed by the rear facades of Venetian nobles' palaces, many of which still bear their stone coats of arms. You enter through only two gates: Paraporti to the south and Trani to the north, topped by the Crispi tower—the only one still standing. Inside you'll find the 13th-century Catholic cathedral, the archaeological museum (housed in the former Jesuit school founded in 1627, where Nikos Kazantzakis, author of Zorba, was a student), and the della Rocca-Barozzi Venetian museum.

The Temple of Demeter at Sangri, about fifteen kilometers south of Chora, is a 6th-century BC Doric temple partially reconstructed with its own original materials. The small site museum preserves the sculpted fragments found during excavations.

The Kouros of Apollonas, at the island's northern tip, is a giant statue measuring 11 meters long and weighing 80 tons, abandoned in its quarry in the 7th century BC—probably due to a crack in the marble. It lies on its back, still attached to the rock. You'll feel tiny discovering it. Access is free, right by the road.

Apiranthos is one of Naxos's best-preserved villages: its lanes are entirely paved in marble, its houses built in dry-stone Cycladic construction, and its inhabitants largely descended from Cretan refugees who arrived in the 17th century. The village has four small museums. Take time for a taverna on the main square.

activities.text: The road between Chora and Apollonas crosses Naxos's mountainous interior: olive groves, vineyards, marble villages, and Aegean views unfold in succession. It's the best way to see the island in its daily reality, far from the beaches. Allow half a day for the round trip with stops.

Arseniko (or graviera of Naxos) is one of Greece's finest cheeses: hard, aged, with a powerful and slightly sharp flavor, produced under PDO on the island. You'll find it in all of Chora's cheese shops. Pair it with local honey—the combination is a Naxos classic.

Kitron is a liqueur unique in the world, distilled since 1896 in Halki from the leaves of a particular lemon tree that grows only on Naxos. The Vallindras distillery on Halki's main square offers tastings. Three versions exist—white, golden, and green—depending on the sugar level.

The beaches along Naxos's southwestern coast—Agios Prokopios, Agia Anna, Plaka—are among the Cyclades' finest: fine sand, shallow water, turquoise. Agios Prokopios is the most developed; Plaka is wilder and stretches for 5 km. All are accessible by bus from Chora.

visits.text: Panagia Ekatontapiliani—"the church of a hundred doors"—is the Cyclades' largest early Christian sanctuary and Greece's third largest. According to tradition, Saint Helen, mother of Emperor Constantine, vowed to build one during her stop at Paros in the 4th century. The current structure dates to the 6th century under Justinian. Its name comes from a legend: the church has 99 visible doors, and the hundredth will open the day Constantinople becomes Greek again. Don't miss the 4th-century baptistery attached to the church—it's the oldest and best-preserved in the Christian East.

Parikia's Kastro, built in the 13th century by Marco Sanudo, perches on the hill overlooking the port. Its walls are made from blocks of columns and lintels salvaged from ancient temples on the site—look carefully climbing the steps and you'll still see the column drums. The view from the top sweeps across the entire bay of Parikia.

Naoussa, in the island's north, is Paros's most charming fishing port: its white houses cascade down to the water, its tavernas spill onto the quay, and fishermen's caiques bob in the bay. A small 14th-century Venetian kastro guards the harbor entrance—it's half in the water, giving it a singular appearance.

Lefkes, the largest inland village, was Paros's capital during the Ottoman era. Its white marble lanes, blue-shuttered houses, and two grand churches make it one of the Cyclades' most beautiful villages. An 8th-century Byzantine marble path, partially restored, links Lefkes to neighboring villages.

The Valley of the Butterflies (Petaloudes), in the island's center, is a nature reserve where thousands of moths (Callimorpha quadripunctaria) gather each summer on the valley's damp trees. It's an unusual spectacle for the typically arid Cyclades.

activities.text: Kolymbithres beach, north of Naoussa, owes its charm to granite rock formations sculpted by erosion into coves and natural pools. The water is calm, deep blue, and the rocks create ideal underwater topography for snorkeling. Accessible by water taxi from Naoussa or by car.

Revythada is a signature Paros dish: chickpeas cooked very slowly in a potter's oven for hours, with no seasoning beyond olive oil and salt. The result is creamy, tender. Ask for it in Parikia or Naoussa tavernas—some only serve it on Sundays.

Gouna is a fishermen's specialty: mackerel split in half and sun-dried for several days, then grilled over charcoal. It's a typical Paros mezze, eaten with a glass of ouzo while gazing at the sea. Look for it in the port tavernas of Naoussa or Parikia.

Paros is one of the world's top kitesurfing destinations, with Golden Beach (Chryssi Akti) leading the way—the wind is consistent and predictable thanks to the Meltemi. Rental centers offer equipment and lessons for all levels.

visits.text: Antiparos Cave is one of Greece's most significant: 85 meters deep, accessible via 411 steps carved into the rock. It harbors an 8-meter-tall stalagmite that's 45 million years old, considered Europe's oldest. The cave was known since antiquity—geometric and classical pottery has been found inside—and it served as a cult site for Artemis. On December 24, 1673, the Marquis de Nointel, Louis XIV's ambassador to Constantinople, celebrated Christmas Mass here before 500 people, using the great stalagmite as an altar. It closes in late afternoon—plan to go in the morning.

Antiparos's Kastro was built in 1440 by Giovanni Lorendano, Venetian lord of the island, to protect inhabitants from pirate attacks. The fortress is unique in the Cyclades: the outer walls of the inhabitants' houses form the castle's ramparts. The single entrance is through a door next to Saint Nicholas Cathedral.

Antiparos's Chora is the antithesis of Mykonos: a quiet, authentic village where local life resumes its rhythm come September. Its lanes flowering with bougainvillea, family-run tavernas, and port facing Paros offer a welcome decompression after the more tourist-heavy islands.

The islet of Despotiko, accessible in 10 minutes by boat from Agios Georgios in southern Antiparos, harbors a 7th-century BC archaic sanctuary of Apollo that's been under excavation since 1997. The islet's deserted beaches are among the Aegean's finest—crystalline waters, white sand bottom, no tourist development whatsoever.

activities.text: The Paros-Antiparos crossing from Punta takes 10 minutes by ferry, with connections every 30 minutes from 6 a.m. to midnight in summer. On the Antiparos side, rent a scooter or bike at the port to explore the island at your own pace—roads are flat and distances short.

The beaches of Soros and Glyfa, on Antiparos's southern coast, are among the island's finest: fine sand, remarkably clear turquoise waters, very few people even in high season. Bring water and provisions—there are no tavernas on-site.

In the evening at Chora, traditional tavernas serve fish caught that very day, grilled octopus, and homemade mezzes in a relaxed, family atmosphere. Prices are well below those of Paros or Mykonos for equivalent quality.

Paros's assyrtiko, a dry, mineral white wine produced on the island's granite soils, pairs perfectly with seafood and grilled fish. Paros wines carry a PDO designation and can be found in the island's groceries and tavernas.

seo.h1_title: Cyclades Island Hopping: Mykonos, Delos, Naxos, Paros & Antiparos
seo.intro_paragraph: Start from Mykonos with its 16th-century Venetian windmills, then cross to Delos, a sacred uninhabited island and UNESCO site that's Apollo's mythical birthplace. Breathe the open sea at Naxos among giant kouroi and marble villages, then glide to Paros and its Byzantine hundred-door basilica. Finish at Antiparos, where an 85-meter-deep cave and deserted beaches close out this tour of the central Cyclades.
seo.seo_hook: Five islands, five personalities—from festive Mykonos to pristine Antiparos, via sacred Delos and mighty Naxos. The central Cyclades in one week.
featured_list: Mykonos: Venetian windmills, Little Venice, and legendary Mediterranean nightlife
Delos: open-air archaeological museum, UNESCO-listed birthplace of Apollo
Naxos: the largest Cyclades island, between giant kouroi and wild beaches
Paros and Antiparos: from Byzantine basilica to prehistoric caves

places_taglines.GR::mykonos: Where Venetian windmills spin above the cosmopolitan Aegean shore
places_taglines.GR::delos: Apollo's sacred birthplace, frozen in marble and myth
places_taglines.GR::naxos: The marble giant of the Cyclades, crowned by ancient kouroi
places_taglines.GR::paros: Where the oldest Byzantine basilica guards shores of legendary marble
places_taglines.GR::antiparos: A quiet escape harboring one of Greece's deepest caves

highlights: Delos archaeological site, UNESCO-listed, boat excursion from Mykonos
Antiparos Cave: 85 m deep, 45-million-year-old stalagmite
Naxos's Portara: sunset through the axis of the marble gateway
Panagia Ekatontapiliani of Paros: the Cyclades' largest early Christian sanctuary

itineraries.title: Cyclades Journey: Athens, Santorini, Mykonos & Naxos
itineraries.subtitle: From the Acropolis to volcanic cliffs - ten days between ancient Athens, spectacular Santorini, festive Mykonos and unspoiled Naxos.
specialties: Assyrtiko wine from Santorini's volcanic soils
Traditional souvlaki and gyros in Athens

visits.text: Start at Syntagma Square - literally 'Constitution' - the beating heart of modern Athens since 1843. From the café terraces, watch the changing of the Evzone Guard in front of Parliament: the ritual happens every hour, with the full ceremonial change on Sunday mornings at 11am. Then head up Ermou Street toward Monastiraki market, where stalls overflow with copper icons, ceramics and ancient replicas.

The Plaka neighborhood, wedged between the Acropolis and Monastiraki, is one of the oldest continuously inhabited districts in Europe - its alleyways date back to Ottoman times and beyond. You'll find neoclassical houses, tavernas that have been around for decades, and some of the city's rare pedestrian zones. Avoid restaurants with touts at the entrance - the best tavernas don't need hawkers.

The Ancient Agora, at the foot of the Acropolis, is the birthplace of Athenian democracy: this is where Socrates debated, political assemblies convened, and philosophers strolled beneath the colonnades. The Temple of Hephaestus on the site's edge is one of the best-preserved Greek temples in the world - converted to a church in the 7th century, then preserved as is.

The Temple of Olympian Zeus, south of the Acropolis, was ancient Greece's largest temple once completed. Construction began in 515 BC and wasn't finished until six centuries later, under Emperor Hadrian in 132 AD. Of the original 104 columns, only 15 remain standing - a sixteenth collapsed during a storm in 1852 and still lies on the ground.

activities.text: Explore Monastiraki flea market on Sunday morning - that's when it's most lively and well-stocked. Vendors spread out everything and anything from dawn: antiques, old books, vinyl records, jewelry. It's also the best place to grab a hot gyros, standing up, before starting your day.

Late afternoon, head up to the Psyrri and Gazi neighborhoods for drinks: these former industrial districts converted to bars and restaurants are today's hip Athens, far less touristy than Plaka. Locals dine late here - before 9pm, restaurants are half empty.

Try pork or lamb souvlaki at one of the artisan grill houses around Monastiraki - it's Athens' quintessential street food, far better than at tourist restaurants. Pair it with tzatziki and grilled pita.

visits.text: The Acropolis sits at 156 meters elevation on a rocky plateau measuring 300 by 150 meters. The Parthenon, built between 447 and 432 BC under Pericles by architects Ictinus and Callicrates under sculptor Phidias' supervision, required 22,000 tons of marble quarried from Mount Pentelicus 17 km away. In 1687, a Venetian bomb ignited gunpowder stored inside by the Ottomans - the roof collapsed. Arrive at opening (8am): crowds double after 10am. Book tickets online to skip the line.

The Erechtheion, north of the Parthenon, is the last monument erected on the Acropolis before the end of the 5th century BC. It's famous for its Porch of the Caryatids: six draped maidens serving as load-bearing columns. The five originals are in the Acropolis Museum - the statues on site are replicas. Lord Elgin took a sixth to London in 1803; it remains in the British Museum despite repeated restitution requests.

The Acropolis Museum, opened in 2009 at the hill's base, preserves the Parthenon's sculptures and friezes that weren't stolen or destroyed, as well as the original Erechtheion Caryatids. From its windows, you get a direct view of the Acropolis. Allow at least 90 minutes.

The Panathenaic Stadium is the world's only stadium built entirely of white marble. Inaugurated in 330 BC for the Panathenaic Games, it was renovated in 1896 to host the first modern Olympic Games. It can accommodate 50,000 spectators. You're allowed to run on the track.

activities.text: Climb Mount Lycabettus by funicular or on foot via the trail (about 45 minutes' walk from the Kolonaki neighborhood). At 277 meters, it's Athens' highest point - the view embraces the entire city from Piraeus port to the Acropolis. In the evening, the illuminated city and Acropolis views are striking.

In the evening, if programming permits, the Odeon of Herodes Atticus has hosted the Athens Festival since 1955 beneath its marble seats built in 161 AD. Concerts, operas and dance performances take place under the stars, with the illuminated Acropolis as backdrop. Check the program in advance.

visits.text: Fira, Santorini's capital, clings 250 meters above the caldera. The caldera itself resulted from one of the largest eruptions in human history: around 1600 BC, the island's central volcano exploded and collapsed, creating a 12 km diameter crater now filled with sea. The multicolored cliffs - black basalt, red, white pumice - tell the stratigraphy of this catastrophe. If you're afraid of heights, walk carefully along the edges.

The Museum of Prehistoric Thera in Fira displays objects discovered during Akrotiri excavations - a Minoan city buried under volcanic ash around 1600 BC, comparable to Pompeii. The original frescoes from the city are among the finest in the Aegean Antiquity: fishing scenes, navigation, young men in action. Allow an hour for the visit.

Oia is Greece's most photographed village - its cave houses carved into the cliff, cobalt domes and flowered alleyways make it a worldwide postcard. Arrive early morning, before 9am, to see it almost deserted. Oia's Byzantine castle is the best viewpoint for sunset - plan to arrive 90 minutes early to find a spot.

activities.text: In the evening, settle in Oia for sunset. It's the most famous in the Cyclades: in July-August, hundreds of people crowd the cliff edge. Go anyway, but arrive early and position yourself slightly back from the castle for an unobstructed view without fighting for space.

Santorini fava, yellow split pea purée grown on the island since antiquity and protected by PDO, has incomparable sweetness. Tomatokeftedes, fritters made from cherry tomatoes concentrated by volcanic sun, are worth trying as meze. You'll find both in all Fira and Oia tavernas.

Santorini assyrtiko, a white grape variety cultivated in ground-level baskets to protect it from wind, produces a mineral, dry and slightly saline wine - unique in the world. The vines have thrived on the island's volcanic soils for centuries. Several estates offer tastings with caldera views.

map_keywords: Fira Oia caldera trail hike Santorini

visits.text: The Akrotiri archaeological site is often compared to Pompeii: a Middle Bronze Age Minoan city, buried under meters of volcanic pumice around 1600 BC and preserved intact. Unlike Pompeii, no bodies were found here - the inhabitants had time to flee. The two-story alleyways, oil jars still in place, and house structures are protected by a massive covered structure. Allow 90 minutes.

Kamari beach, on the east coast, is Santorini's main black sand beach. This volcanic sand heats up very quickly in summer - wear shoes to walk to the water. The water is clear and quite deep quickly. The beach is long, well-equipped, with seaside tavernas.

Red Beach, west of Akrotiri, is one of Greece's most spectacular beaches: an enclosed cove between bright red lava cliffs plunging into deep blue sea. The footpath from the parking lot takes 10 to 15 minutes on rocky terrain - wear good shoes. The beach is small and packed in summer, arrive early.

Pyrgos is Santorini's best-preserved medieval village, perched on the island's highest point at 350 meters. Built around a 13th-century Venetian kastro, it has retained its labyrinthine alleyways and crenellated houses. 360° view over the entire island from the chapel at the summit. Much less crowded than Oia.

activities.text: The Fira-Oia hike along the caldera is one of the most beautiful in the Mediterranean: 10.5 km on the cliff edge, between 250 and 350 meters height, with constant views over the 12 km diameter crater and volcanic islets at the center. Allow 3h30 to 4h with breaks. Start early morning in summer - midday heat on the volcanic stones is crushing. The trail passes through Firostefani and Imerovigli before reaching Oia.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate - good equipment and water essential

activities.text: Amoudi Bay, at the foot of Oia's cliffs accessible by 300 steps, is the island's most authentic spot for fish. A few fishing tavernas serve dried octopus, sea urchins and grilled fish at the water's edge. You can also dive from the rocks directly into the sea. Avoid going between noon and 3pm - the return climb in the heat is exhausting.

visits.text: Megalochori is one of Santorini's most authentic villages, built in the 17th century by merchants and shipowners. Its main square with vaulted arches and small church is one of the island's most beautiful. Much less crowded than Fira or Oia, this is where you'll see the island as it was before mass tourism.

Imerovigli, between Fira and Oia, is nicknamed 'the balcony of the Aegean Sea'. Skaros Rock, a former 13th-century Venetian fortress partially collapsed into the sea during the 1956 earthquake, offers a 270° view over the Kameni islets and caldera - less crowded than Oia castle for sunset.

activities.text: Boat excursion to Nea Kameni volcano in the caldera's center: shuttles depart from Fira's old port (accessible by 580 steps or funicular). On the islet, a trail climbs to the volcanic cone's summit at 150 meters - sulfurous fumaroles remind you the volcano remains active, its last eruption dating from 1950. You can then swim in neighboring Palaia Kameni's natural hot springs, slightly ferruginous and sulfurous water.

Wine tasting in Santorini is a unique experience: vines are cultivated in ground-level basket coils to protect from wind, a millennial technique unique here. The mineral, dry assyrtiko white is most renowned - but vinsanto, sweet wine from sun-dried grapes, is also worth experiencing. Several estates offer tastings with caldera views.

visits.text: Chora Mykonos is a deliberate labyrinth: the alleyways are intentionally winding to confuse pirates. Its whitewashed houses, bougainvillea and blue domes form the quintessential Cycladic scene. Explore it early morning - after 10am, cruise groups invade the port and the alleys become highways.

The seven Kato Mili windmills were built in the 16th century by Venetians to grind wheat transported from neighboring islands, thanks to the Meltemi blowing 300 days a year. Their whitewashed cylindrical walls, topped with wooden and straw roofs, are Mykonos' most recognizable image. Go at sunset - the orange light on the immaculate white is photogenic, but beware the wind at the hilltop.

Little Venice - the Alefkandra district - owes its name to 18th-century captains' houses whose colorful balconies overhang the Aegean Sea directly. It's the island's only waterfront district. In the evening, the bar terraces fill up quickly - book if you want a waterfront spot.

Panagia Paraportiani is a complex of five chapels built between 1425 and the 17th century, merged into an asymmetrical white mass. It's the oldest of Mykonos' 400 churches and one of Greece's most photographed. Its name - 'Our Lady of the Side Gate' - comes from its position in the former medieval kastro gate. The interior is usually closed, but the exterior alone is worth the trip.

activities.text: In the evening at Mykonos port, bars and terraces come alive late. The island's nightlife is renowned throughout the Mediterranean. If clubs aren't your thing, settle for a drink at Little Venice watching the sun descend over the Aegean.

Try kopanisti, Mykonos PDO spicy cheese - a soft fermented paste with intense flavor. Louza, dried spiced pork charcuterie, is another local specialty unavailable elsewhere in Greece. Ask for both as meze in an old port taverna.

visits.text: The village of Ano Mera, 8 km from Chora, is authentic Mykonos - far from luxury boutiques and party beaches. Its Panagia Tourliani monastery, founded in 1542, houses a beautiful carved wooden iconostasis and flowered courtyard. Tavernas on the main square serve traditional family cooking at prices far below those at the port.

activities.text: Paradise and Super Paradise beaches, south of the island, are Mykonos' party playground: loud music, beach clubs, international atmosphere. Buses serve them from Chora. If you're seeking calm, neighboring Agrari is less developed and less crowded - same fine sand, same turquoise water.
Psarou beach, smaller and quieter than Paradise, is ideal for enjoying the sea without techno music. The water is particularly clear and surrounding rocks are good for snorkeling.

Late afternoon, head to Armenistis lighthouse at the island's northwest tip - built in 1891, it measures 19 meters. From the site, panoramic views over the Aegean Sea and Tinos island. Arrive before sunset: it's one of the island's best spots, and much less crowded than Oia or the windmills.

visits.text: Delos is 30 minutes by boat from Mykonos' old port. The entire 3.5 km² island has been a UNESCO site since 1990: no accommodation, no commerce, no modern construction. According to mythology, it's the birthplace of Apollo and Artemis. The French School at Athens has excavated here since 1873 and only a third of the site has been uncovered.

The Terrace of the Lions is Delos' emblematic image: Naxos marble lions, offered by the Naxians in the 7th century BC to guard Apollo's sanctuary. Of the 16 originals, only 5 survive as replicas on site - the originals are preserved in the island's museum. A sixth has stood outside the Venice Arsenal since the 17th century.

Delos' ancient theatre, built between 314 and 250 BC, could accommodate 5,000 to 6,500 spectators. This is where performances took place during the Delia, a Panhellenic festival held every four years in Apollo's honor. Sit in the stone seats to imagine the atmosphere.

Mount Cynthus, at 113 meters, is Delos' highest point. From its summit, the view embraces the entire Cyclades on clear days. This is where, according to tradition, Apollo was born. The rocky path takes 20 to 25 minutes - wear good shoes.

activities.text: Take the early boat from Mykonos' old port - the site closes at 3pm in low season. There's no water to buy or restaurant on the island after opening: bring water and snacks. The sun is merciless, with no trees for shelter.

practical_info.booking: Boat ticket + site entry purchased separately

activities.text: Don't leave without visiting the island's Archaeological Museum - built in 1904, it preserves the original terrace lions, a 6th-century BC kouros torso, and mosaics, jewelry and inscriptions found during excavations. Allow 45 minutes for the nine rooms.

visits.text: Naxos' Portara is visible from the ferry long before docking: a massive marble doorway perched on Palatia islet at the port entrance. Its four blocks each weigh 20 tons - too heavy for the Venetians to move, which explains why it's the only standing remnant of Apollo's temple begun around 530 BC under tyrant Lygdamis and never completed. According to mythology, this islet is where Theseus abandoned Ariadne. Go at sunset: the sun descends exactly through the doorway's axis.

Chora Naxos' Kastro, founded in 1207 by Marco Sanudo, first Duke of Naxos, dominates the port from a hill. Its outer walls are formed by the backs of Venetian palaces, many retaining their coats of arms. Inside: the 13th-century Catholic cathedral, an archaeological museum housed in the former 1627 Jesuit lyceum - where Nikos Kazantzakis, author of Zorba the Greek, was a student - and a Venetian museum.

The Temple of Demeter at Sangri, 15 km south of Chora, is a 6th-century BC Doric temple partially reconstructed with its original materials. Small site museum with carved fragments found. The place is quiet and non-touristy - worth seeing if you have a vehicle.

activities.text: The beaches on Naxos' southwest coast - Agios Prokopios, Agia Anna, Plaka - are among the Cyclades' finest: fine, pale sand, shallow turquoise water. Agios Prokopios is the most equipped; Plaka stretches 5 km and is wilder. All are accessible by bus from Chora.

Arseniko (Naxos graviera), a hard aged cheese with powerful flavor, is produced under PDO on the island. Taste it with local honey at Chora cheese shops. Naxos potatoes - small, flavorful, PDO - are renowned throughout Greece: roasted with olive oil and lemon, they accompany all taverna dishes.

visits.text: Halki, the island's former capital, is a marble village in the heart of Tragea plain, surrounded by olive groves and vineyards. Its main square is animated by a few tavernas and the Vallindras distillery, producing kitron since 1896 - a unique liqueur distilled from the leaves of a particular citrus tree that grows only in Naxos.

Apiranthos is one of Naxos' best-preserved villages: alleyways entirely paved with marble, Cycladic stone houses, four small museums. The inhabitants largely descend from Cretan refugees who arrived in the 17th century. Don't leave without stopping at the café on the main square - local life unfolds there effortlessly.

The Kouros of Apollonas, at the island's northern tip, is an 11-meter, 80-ton giant statue abandoned in its quarry in the 7th century BC - probably due to a crack in the marble. It's lain on its back in the rock for over 2,700 years. Access is free and open, roadside.

activities.text: The route between Chora, Halki, Apiranthos and Apollonas crosses the mountainous heart of Naxos - olive trees, vineyards, marble villages and Aegean Sea views. It's the best way to see the island in its reality. Allow half a day with stops.

Aliko beach, southwest of the island, is one of Naxos' most isolated and wild: fine sand, dunes covered with cedars, crystal-clear waters and very few people. A sand path leads through the dunes to the water's edge. Bring supplies - there's nothing on site.

Late afternoon, return to Chora for the Portara at sunset - it's even more beautiful than on arrival, from the pedestrian causeway crossing the port. Then settle into a waterfront taverna for your last evening in Naxos.

seo.h1_title: Cyclades Journey: Athens, Santorini, Mykonos & Naxos
seo.intro_paragraph: Begin in Athens with its Acropolis built between 447 and 432 BC under Pericles' orders. Fly to Santorini and its volcanic cliffs born from a cataclysmic eruption around 1600 BC. Ferry across to Mykonos, its 16th-century Venetian windmills and essential excursion to sacred Delos, a UNESCO site. End at Naxos - the largest Cyclades island - between giant kouroi, marble villages and deserted beaches.
seo.seo_hook: From the Acropolis to Santorini's volcanic cliffs, from festive Mykonos to Naxos' marble villages - ten days in the classic Cyclades.
featured_list: Athens: Acropolis, Parthenon and historic Plaka and Monastiraki neighborhoods
Santorini: volcanic caldera, Fira-Oia hike and Oia sunset
Mykonos: Venetian windmills, Little Venice and UNESCO-listed Delos excursion
Naxos: Portara, Venetian kastro, giant kouroi and wild beaches

places_taglines.GR::athenes: Where the 5th-century BC Acropolis still crowns the modern city
places_taglines.GR::fira: Santorini's cliffside capital perched 250 meters above the volcanic caldera
places_taglines.GR::mykonos: Cosmopolitan island with 16th-century windmills and gateway to sacred Delos
places_taglines.GR::naxos: The Cyclades' largest island, where ancient temples meet marble mountain villages

highlights: Athens Acropolis: Parthenon (447-432 BC) and Erechtheion with the Caryatids
Santorini: caldera born from a 1600 BC eruption and 10.5 km Fira-Oia cliff hike
Delos: deserted UNESCO sacred island, Apollo's mythical birthplace, 7th-century BC marble lions
Naxos: Portara with 20-ton blocks and 11-meter giant kouros abandoned in its quarry

itineraries.title: Dodecanese Journey: Rhodes, Symi & Kos
itineraries.subtitle: From Rhodes' medieval old town to the sponge-diver cliffs of Kalymnos, via Symi's pastel houses and Kos, birthplace of ancient medicine.

map_keywords: Palace of the Grand Master Rhodes

visits.text: Rhodes Old Town has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988, and it thoroughly deserves that recognition. The Knights of St. John occupied it from 1309 to 1522 and set about transforming it into an impregnable fortress—the 4 kilometers of ramparts you'll walk along bear witness to this. What makes the site exceptional is the layering: behind Gothic walls hide Ottoman mosques, Turkish baths, and Byzantine alleyways. Enter through the d'Amboise Gate late morning, before tour buses flood the narrow streets.

The Palace of the Grand Masters dominates the old town from the highest point of the citadel. Built in the 14th century, it contains 150 rooms, of which 24 are open to the public. What you absolutely can't miss inside: the mosaic floors, brought from Kos by the Italians during their controversial 1930s restoration. Worth knowing: in 1856, a gunpowder magazine explosion nearly destroyed the entire palace—what you see today is largely a Fascist reconstruction, intended to welcome Mussolini, who ultimately never visited. Look for the commemorative stele bearing his name near the ticket office.

The Street of the Knights (Odós Ippotón) is one of the best-preserved Gothic monuments in Europe. The Knights of the Order were organized into seven "tongues"—France, Italy, Spain, Provence, Auvergne, England, Germany—and each had its inn along this main thoroughfare. You'll see their facades at every turn. Take time to read the coats of arms carved above the doors: the Auvergne inn bears those of Guy de Blanchefort, Grand Master from 1512 to 1513.

The Archaeological Museum of Rhodes is housed in the former Great Hospital of the Knights, built between 1440 and 1489. The star of the collection? The Crouching Aphrodite, a Hellenistic marble statue of remarkable finesse. Allow 45 minutes to an hour for a complete tour. The combined ticket (valid 3 days) includes this museum, the Palace of the Grand Masters, and the church of Panagia tou Kastrou—a smart way to optimize your visits.

The Suleiman the Magnificent Mosque, built in 1522 just after the Ottoman conquest of Rhodes, visually marks the transition between two civilizations. Its pink minaret is visible from afar within the old town. It's closed to regular visits, but the exterior and surrounding square are worth seeing in the evening, when the light is soft and crowds thinner.

activities.text: Wander through the lanes of the lower town (Chora) following your instincts: craft shops, painted ceramic workshops, small tavernas tucked into courtyards. If you're there on a weekday morning, the covered market on Socratous Street offers a much more local atmosphere than the tourist boutiques along the waterfront.

Stroll along Mandraki harbor late in the day. At the harbor entrance stand two columns topped with bronze stag and doe—tradition claims the Colossus of Rhodes, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, stood at this spot (nothing proves it, but visitors love the story). Three Ottoman windmills and Fort Saint Nicholas complete the panorama. A café terrace facing the harbor at sunset—that's the best moment of the day.

Try pitaroudia, chickpea fritters typical of Rhodes, fried in olive oil with onion and dill. You'll find them in all the old town's tavernas, often served hot as an appetizer. Pair them with a glass of Athiri, the local white varietal: dry, light, slightly citrusy, perfect with mezze and fresh fish.

If you have energy after dinner, climb onto the ramparts from the Palace of the Grand Masters entrance (different hours from the palace, check before going). The walk offers plunging views over the old town and harbor you won't find anywhere else. Bring a flashlight—lighting is minimal.

visits.text: The Acropolis of Lindos perches on a cliff 116 meters above the sea—it's the third most-visited archaeological site in Greece, right after Athens and Delphi. The Temple of Athena Lindia, in its current form, dates from around 300 BC (replacing an earlier temple destroyed by fire in 392 BC). At the entrance, a rare detail: a warship's prow carved directly into the rock in the 2nd century BC, which served as a pedestal for a now-vanished bronze statue. Arrive before 9 AM—after that hour, the climb becomes a procession.

The village of Lindos, entirely pedestrian, is built in an amphitheater on a hillside. Its lanes paved with black-and-white pebble mosaics lead to the "captains' houses" from the 16th century, built by shipowners who had made fortunes in maritime trade. Push open the door of the Church of the Virgin (Panagia), founded in the 14th century and restored in 1489 by Grand Master d'Aubusson: its interior frescoes date from 1779 and are among the best-preserved on the island. Skip lunch on a terrace here—prices are noticeably higher than elsewhere on Rhodes.

St. Paul's Bay (Paralia Agios Pavlos), below Lindos, takes its name from the apostle Paul, who supposedly landed here during his journey to Rhodes. The water is a striking turquoise, enclosed on three sides by cliffs and the acropolis. It's also a popular wedding venue—if you're there on a weekday, you'll have better chances of finding the peace needed to enjoy it.

On the island's west coast, Kamiros is nicknamed the "Greek Pompeii" of Rhodes: a Doric city founded in the 5th century BC, abandoned after an earthquake, and rediscovered in 1852. You'll see a sophisticated water system—cisterns, pipes, fountains—that supplied the entire city. The stoa, terraced houses, and Temple of Athena are well-preserved enough to give you a precise idea of ancient urban planning. Allow one hour for the visit.

On the way back from Kamiros, the Valley of the Butterflies (Petaloudes) is worth a quick stop between June and September, when thousands of Panaxia quadripunctaria butterflies settle there in the coolness of plane trees and waterfalls. Off-season, the site remains pretty but the butterflies aren't there—check the timing before making the detour.

activities.text: The climb to Lindos acropolis is on foot from the village—bring good shoes and water, the slope is steep under the sun. Mules offer rides up, but several local sources discourage this option out of concern for the animals. The reward at the top: a 360° view over Lindos' two bays, the Aegean Sea, and on clear days, the Turkish coast.

After the acropolis, head down to swim in the main bay of Lindos (Main Beach). The water is clear, the beach organized with sunbeds and umbrellas. To avoid summer crowds, opt for neighboring St. Paul's Bay: smaller, less known, and just as beautiful.

Try souma before leaving: it's a traditional Rhodes fig eau-de-vie, sweet and fragrant, found in small grocers in Lindos village. Pair it with takakia, these little bites of fried octopus with lemon served by most waterfront tavernas. For wine, the local Mandilaria—fruity and light red—is perfect with grilled meats.

visits.text: Entering Gialos harbor, the spectacle hits you all at once: hundreds of neoclassical houses in shades of ochre, pink, blue, and yellow rising in tiers up the hillside, reflected in the harbor water. One hour by ferry from Rhodes, Symi owes this architecture to its 19th-century prosperity, when the island was a major shipbuilding center producing up to 500 vessels a year. The village of Gialos is a classified historic monument—facades are protected in their original state. Give yourself time on the waterfront before climbing toward Chorio.

Chorio, the upper village, is reached from Gialos via the Kali Strata—literally "the beautiful stairs"—500 steps lined with the island's finest manor houses. The climb is worth it as much for the sculpted facades as for the views over the harbor that open at each landing. At the top, the ruins of the 15th-century Knights' castle dominate everything, and Chorio's quiet lanes contrast with the bustle of Gialos below.

Panormitis Monastery, in the island's south, is one of the Dodecanese's most important pilgrimage sites. Founded in the 18th century, it's dedicated to the Archangel Michael, patron saint of sailors. Its baroque bell tower rises above a sheltered bay accessible only by sea or by winding road. Inside, the walls are covered with ex-votos offered by grateful sailors—bells, silver boats, objects recovered after shipwrecks. Several boat excursions from Gialos stop here.

The Symi Maritime Museum, housed in the old neoclassical shipyard on Kampos square, traces the history of this island of sailors and sponge divers. Diving suits, archival photos, 19th-century diving equipment: you understand here just how dangerous this fishing was—decompression accidents were frequent and left many men disabled. It's a short visit (30 to 45 minutes) but gives crucial context to what you see around you.

Symi's beaches are mostly pebbles rather than sand, accessible mainly by taxi-boat from Gialos. Agios Georgios Dyssalonas, Nanou, and Agia Marina are among the island's most beautiful coves—turquoise waters, cliff backdrops, no roads leading to them. Check at the harbor in the morning for departures and the last return boat.

activities.text: Position yourself on the ferry deck as you approach Symi—the entrance to Gialos harbor with its amphitheater of houses is one of the Dodecanese's most photographed moments. If you're spending the night on the island, enjoy the evening calm when day-trippers from Rhodes have caught their boat back: tavernas empty by half and lanes regain their silence.

Take a taxi-boat from Gialos to reach one of the isolated coves on the island's south or east coast—Nanou or Agia Marina are best for swimming. It's the only way to access these bays unreachable by road. Ask at the harbor about the last return boat before departing.

practical_info.booking: Departures from Gialos quay, frequent departures in summer

activities.text: Symi's specialty is tiny local shrimp (garides Symis)—minuscule, caught in the deep waters around the island, crispy and delicious eaten whole. They're served fried with a squeeze of lemon and a glass of ouzo. Every harbor taverna offers them—quality varies, but it's the dish you can't miss. Finish with galaktoboureko, that pastry of semolina cream wrapped in phyllo, soaked in lemon syrup.

map_keywords: Neratzia Castle of the Knights Kos

visits.text: The Asclepeion of Kos sits 4 km southwest of town, and it's the island's most important archaeological site. Founded in the 4th century BC, it was simultaneously a religious sanctuary, healing center, and medical school—one of history's first hospitals, capable of receiving up to 4,000 patients according to some ancient sources. Hippocrates, a native of Kos, supposedly taught here. The sanctuary spreads across three terraces connected by marble staircases: Roman baths and consultation rooms on the first level, temples of Apollo and Asclepius on the second, grand Doric temple and porticoes on the third. From the top, the view sweeps across Kos town, the sea, and the Turkish coast opposite. Bring a hat and water—there's little shade on site.

Opposite the Knights' castle, on Platanos square, stands the Hippocrates Plane Tree. The tree measures 4.7 meters in diameter and spreads more than 12 meters in radius—it's one of Europe's oldest and largest plane trees, even if botanists estimate its age at around 500 years rather than the legendary 2,400. No matter: the spot is pleasant, shaded, and tradition says Hippocrates taught medicine beneath its branches.

The Castle of the Knights of Kos (Neratzia), built from 1315 directly atop the ruins of an ancient fort, controls the harbor entrance from its promontory. The Knights of St. John reused ancient marble blocks for its walls—you can still clearly spot them in the lower courses. The interior houses a collection of ancient and medieval sculptures. Until the early 20th century, the castle was separated from town by a moat filled with seawater.

The ancient agora of Kos, south of the castle, is one of the region's best-preserved ancient urban centers. Founded in the 4th century BC on a geometric plan, it features a massive colonnaded stoa, ruins of a sanctuary to Aphrodite, a 2nd-century BC Temple of Hercules, and a 5th-century Christian basilica. The site is generally open and lightly monitored—ideal for peaceful exploration.

The Archaeological Museum of Kos, on Eleftherias square, houses the collection of discoveries made on the island since antiquity. The centerpiece: a marble statue of Hippocrates, and several remarkable mosaics from the Hellenistic and Roman periods. Combined ticket with the Asclepeion available.

activities.text: Kos is one of the Dodecanese's most bikeable Greek islands—flat, well-served by bike paths, and crossed end-to-end by a coastal road. Rent a bike in the morning to reach the Asclepeion (4 km), then head toward the beaches of Tigaki or Marmari on the north coast, with their fine sand and shallow waters. The total journey from Kos town represents about twenty kilometers round-trip—very accessible for any level.

In the evening, explore the lively center of Kos town with its blend of Ottoman architecture (mosques, fountains) and Greek and Roman remains around street corners. Apellou street and the areas around Eleftherias square concentrate tavernas, bars, and restaurants for ending the day on a terrace.

Try patatato, Kos's traditional dish: veal slowly braised with potatoes, white wine, and cumin. Simple, generous, rooted in the island's rural tradition. For dessert, sample the local baklava, more generous with walnuts and cinnamon than the neighboring Turkish version.

visits.text: Pothia, Kalymnos's capital, welcomes you with its amphitheater of colorful houses as soon as you leave the ferry. Along the waterfront, stalls displaying natural sponges of all shapes and sizes immediately remind you which island you're on. Homer already mentioned this tradition in the Iliad—Kalymnos was long the world's leading exporter of natural sponges. Wander through Pothia's lanes to see the neoclassical buildings inherited from this prosperity, and spot the coats of arms of the Knights Templar engraved on certain facades.

The Maritime Museum of Pothia (Vouvalio district) is the best introduction to the island's history. You'll see the archaic diving suits used by divers until the 1960s to descend to depths of several dozen meters, archival photos of seasonal expeditions to North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean, and sponge-processing equipment. Decompression accidents were so frequent that the island had an abnormally high number of disabled men—the museum doesn't hide this reality.

Chrysocheria Castle dominates Pothia from a promontory above town. Byzantine in origin, it was reinforced by the Knights of St. John. The climb on foot from the center takes 20 to 30 minutes—the view over the harbor and sea is well worth the effort. Two small churches with post-Byzantine murals stand within the walls.

Vathy, on the island's east side, is one of Kalymnos's most unusual sites: a narrow fjord of turquoise water cuts inland between limestone cliffs, opening onto a peaceful village surrounded by orange and mandarin groves. The road leading there is winding but passable. Avoid lunchtime when excursion boats from Kos and Bodrum discharge their groups—the village regains its calm by mid-afternoon.

activities.text: Since the early 2000s, Kalymnos has become one of the world's premier sport climbing destinations, with over 3,500 equipped routes across roughly eighty sectors. The Grande Grotta sector, near Myrties, is the most photographed: its stalactites overhang the Aegean directly. Even without experience, you can try it out—several local centers offer guided sessions on beginner sectors like "Afternoon," with sea views. French-speaking instructors are active on the island. A Climbing Festival is organized every autumn, attracting climbers from around the world. Book the day before in high season.

practical_info.difficulty: Beginner instruction available for all levels

activities.text: Take a taxi-boat from Myrties to reach the island of Telendos, directly opposite—10 minutes crossing. Since an earthquake in AD 554, Telendos has been separated from Kalymnos, and local legend has it that the vestiges of an ancient city still rest on the seabed between the two islands. On site: a few tavernas, a quiet beach, and rare peace. Ideal for sunset.

In Pothia, waterfront tavernas serve grilled octopus with vinegar, fresh squid, and fava (split pea purée) drizzled with olive oil and raw onion—fishermen's cuisine at its most authentic. As a souvenir, leave with a natural sponge bought from one of the harbor shops or a processing workshop.

seo.h1_title: Dodecanese Journey: Rhodes, Symi & Kos
seo.intro_paragraph: Explore Rhodes between UNESCO-listed medieval ramparts and Gothic lanes, then catch the ferry to Symi and its cascade of pastel neoclassical houses around the harbor. Continue to Kos to walk in Hippocrates' footsteps at the ancient Asclepeion, before finishing in Kalymnos, land of sponge divers reinvented as a world mecca for rock climbing.
seo.seo_hook: Knights of St. John fortresses, colorful houses cascading down to harbors, sponge-diving traditions, and limestone cliffs plunging into the Aegean: the Dodecanese enters your memory and never leaves.
featured_list: Rhodes: UNESCO-listed medieval old town and Palace of the Grand Masters
Symi: neoclassical harbor in amphitheater among Greece's most beautiful
Kos: ancient Asclepeion and bikeable island with fine sandy beaches
Kalymnos: sponge divers' island transformed into world-class climbing destination

places_taglines.GR::rhodes: Where medieval knights ruled for two centuries behind Europe's finest Gothic ramparts
places_taglines.GR::symi: A harbor where pastel mansions cascade down hillsides like a neoclassical waterfall
places_taglines.GR::kos: Where Hippocrates founded Western medicine beneath plane trees and marble colonnades
places_taglines.GR::kalymnos: From the depths where sponge divers risked their lives to cliffs where climbers chase the sky

highlights: Rhodes Old Town: one of Europe's best-preserved Gothic ensembles
Lindos Acropolis: Temple of Athena perched 116 m above the Aegean Sea
Symi: neoclassical harbor in amphitheater accessible in 1h by ferry from Rhodes
Asclepeion of Kos: medical sanctuary founded in the 4th century BC, birthplace of Hippocratic medicine
Kalymnos: over 3,500 equipped climbing routes on limestone cliffs facing the sea

itineraries.title: Dodecanese Circuit: Patmos, Leros & Karpathos
itineraries.subtitle: From the sacred island where Saint John penned Revelation to the smoking craters of Nisyros—a journey through the hidden Dodecanese, far from the crowds.
specialties: Traditional Doric costumes still worn daily in Olympos
Byzantine manuscript illumination at Patmos monastery

map_keywords: Monastery of Saint John the Theologian Patmos

visits.text: The Monastery of Saint John the Theologian has commanded the heights above Chora since 1088—the year monk Christodoulos, commissioned by Byzantine Emperor Alexios I Komnenos, laid the first stones atop the ruins of an Artemis temple. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1999, the complex resembles a fortress from the outside—high grey walls, corner towers—but the interior holds a revelation: a library housing 4,000 documents including the celebrated Codex Purpureus, Saint Mark's Gospel inscribed in silver letters on purple-dyed parchment, one of the world's oldest illuminated manuscripts. Allow at least 90 minutes to explore the museum and central courtyard. In high season, arrive at opening to beat the tour groups.

Halfway between Skala port and Chora, the Cave of the Apocalypse is carved into living rock—this is where Saint John, exiled to the island by Emperor Domitian around AD 95, supposedly dictated Revelation to his disciple Prochoros. Look closely at the wall: a three-lobed fissure in the stone symbolizes, according to tradition, the voice of the Trinity. The atmosphere is otherworldly—cool and silent even in peak crowds—contrasting sharply with the island's blazing white sunlight. Plan for 45 minutes on site.

Chora, the medieval village that grew around the monastery starting in 1138, is one of the rare Greek settlements to have evolved continuously since the 12th century—UNESCO cites this unbroken lineage as a criterion for World Heritage status in its own right. Lose yourself in its cobbled lanes between whitewashed houses with wooden balconies: several mansions once belonged to wealthy Aegean sea captains and bear witness to past prosperity. In the evening, this is one of the island's finest spots to watch golden light settle over the rooftops.

Skala, Patmos's main port, is the island's beating heart: fishing boats, inter-island catamarans, waterfront tavernas and colorful shops. This is where you'll take the pulse of daily life—far removed from the monastery's contemplative atmosphere. If you're here on a Sunday, you'll often encounter pilgrims in traditional dress disembarking for Holy Thursday Mass, when the abbot reenacts the washing of the feet in the monastery courtyard.

In Chora's lanes, push open the doors to the Zoodochos Pigi monastery, founded in 1607, and the Annunciation (Evangelismos), founded in 1937—two active convents of nuns that prove Patmos remains a living religious center, extending well beyond the main monastery alone. The sisters receive visitors discreetly but genuinely: dress respectfully.

activities.text: Walk from Skala to Chora on the flagstone path that winds between fig trees and dry-stone walls—about 40 minutes. It's the same route pilgrims have followed for centuries. The panorama over the island's bays widens at every turn. If you prefer to drive, note that Chora's parking is tiny and fills quickly in July-August.

Take a day trip to Patmos's southern beaches—Psili Ammos is accessible only by boat or via a 45-minute hiking trail from Diakofti. The sand is fine and the bay sheltered, yielding water of rare calm. Water taxis depart from Skala throughout the morning.

'Pouggia,' small cakes filled with almonds and cinnamon, are Patmos's signature sweet, tied to monastic tradition. Look for them in Skala bakeries—available year-round, but especially tender when they emerge from the oven in the morning. 'Revithia fournou,' chickpeas baked slowly overnight with olive oil and herbs, appear on most Chora taverna menus on weekends.

Patmos revived viticulture in 2011 after more than a century of abandoned vineyards. The local Fokiano grape yields a mineral, rare white reminiscent of Cycladic wines—ask for it in Chora tavernas. It doesn't always appear on the written menu, but proprietors nearly always have a bottle tucked away.

visits.text: The Kastro of Panagia, perched on Apityki hill 200 meters above the sea, is visible from the ferry long before you dock at Leros. Built by the Byzantines in the 11th century and reinforced by the Knights of Rhodes in the 15th, it shelters the miraculous icon of the Virgin who, according to legend, emerged from the sea of her own accord before being installed in the powder magazine chapel. Climb the 300 steps from Platanos—the view of Agia Marina and Panteli bays spreading on either side of the hill is one of the Dodecanese's finest. The 1745 iconostasis and the bronze-and-silver bell cast by Russian artisans are worth the ascent.

Platanos, Leros's former capital, is a quiet village squeezed between two hills, with sand-colored neoclassical houses and small squares shaded by plane trees. This is where the island's administrative heart beats, far from the port bustle. Take time to wander its lanes—several buildings date from the 19th century, when local shipowners were at the height of their prosperity.

Lakki is one of Greece's most bizarre architectural curiosities. Founded in the 1930s by the Italians under the name Porto Lago, this port was designed from scratch according to fascist rationalist principles: wide boulevards, buildings with rounded facades, a cinema and town hall resembling beached ocean liners. Lakki Bay is one of the Mediterranean's largest natural harbors. It's hard not to feel disoriented strolling these nearly deserted streets off-season—like wandering a forgotten 1930s film set.

The War Museum, installed in an authentic underground tunnel at Merikia, is unique in Greece. The Italians excavated it as a munitions depot; in 1943, the Battle of Leros left scars here when the Germans bombarded for 42 days before seizing the island from the British, Greeks and Italians. The museum, opened in 2005, preserves weapons, uniforms, munitions and maps from the 1912-1943 period. Allow 45 minutes.

Alinda's Belleni Tower, built between 1925 and 1927 by expatriate Parisi Belleni, is a neo-Gothic turreted stone building that now houses the island's folklore museum. Exhibits include the watch from the destroyer 'Queen Olga,' sunk in the harbor during the 1943 battle, and artwork painted by political prisoners interned on Leros under Greece's dictatorship (1967-1974).

activities.text: Leros's beaches are perfect for families: Alinda, the longest, is lined with tamarisks and its shallow waters suit children. Vromolithos offers a beach bar and crystalline water. Panteli, at the castle's foot, is the ideal spot in late afternoon when light settles on the ruined windmills along the ridge. For diving, Xirokampos in the south is renowned for its World War II wrecks—a good dozen ships and planes rest at 10 to 30 meters depth.

'Koukouvades,' fish marinated with rosemary, are Leros's signature seafood specialty. 'Pitakia,' pies stuffed with fava beans, and 'Kakavia,' bouillabaisse prepared with fish pulled from caïques that very morning, are essential at tavernas in Panteli or Agia Marina. For dessert, 'Melopita'—honey-and-mizithra-cheese cake—is the local sweet touch.

Ride a scooter (ideal for this reasonably sized island) up to the castle at sunset, when the last visitors have left and bats begin circling the battlements. The panorama of both bays in orange light is one of the Dodecanese's most memorable views. Scooter rentals are available at Agia Marina and Lakki ports.

visits.text: Olympos is arguably the Dodecanese's most spellbinding village. Founded between the 7th and 9th centuries on the heights of Mount Profitis Ilias to escape Saracen raiders, it remained cut off from the rest of the island for centuries—the paved road only opened in 2011. The result: women still wear traditional embroidered costumes daily (not for tourists), the local dialect preserves Doric Greek phrasings, and single-room houses are built in intact medieval architecture. The 75 windmills along the ridge, many still operational, are the island's symbol. Plan a full day.

Pigadia, Karpathos's capital and main port, is the island's logistical hub. Its natural semicircular harbor is one of the Dodecanese's most sheltered. Take time to visit the archaeological museum housed in the Prefecture Palace: Mycenaean ceramics, ancient coins, Roman statues—the collection documents 3,000 years of human presence on the island.

Aperi, Karpathos's former capital until the 19th century, clings to the hillside with stone houses and flower-filled courtyards. The village is famous for its grand 19th-century mansions built by wealthy shipowners—some feature neoclassical facades that seem transported from another Mediterranean entirely. It's also one of the island's best-maintained villages.

Menetes, at 350 meters elevation in the southern hinterland, is the birthplace of the Karpathos lyre—the traditional stringed instrument that has accompanied dances for centuries. The Church of the Dormition of the Mother of God is considered the island's most beautiful. The view across the sea from the village edge at sunset alone justifies the detour.

Apella Beach is one of the Dodecanese's most photographed: a fine-sand arc framed by red schist cliffs and bathed in turquoise water. Access is via a track from the main road (about 15 minutes on foot from the parking area). There are no tavernas or parasols for rent—bring water and a picnic.

activities.text: If you're in Olympos on August 15th, don't leave under any circumstances: the Feast of the Dormition of the Virgin is celebrated here with epic songs transmitted orally for generations, costumed dances, and music played on lyre and tsambouna (local bagpipe) until dawn. Diaspora residents return from abroad just for this.

The hike between Olympos and the port of Diafani descends about 8 km via a mountain trail that overlooks the sea. The view is vertiginous and the vegetation changes at every elevation. Count on 2.5 to 3 hours of walking. Boats connect Diafani to Pigadia for the return journey.

'Makarounes' are Olympos's signature handmade pasta: shaped by hand through rolling between the palms, they're served with melted butter, fried onions and grated mizithra. You'll find them in Olympos tavernas—one of the rare places where they're still made fresh on site every morning. 'Kserotigana,' ribbons of fried dough drizzled with honey, are served at festivals.

visits.text: Astypalea's Chora is a cascade of cubic white houses tumbling down a rocky hillside to the old port of Pera Gialos. At the summit looms Querini Castle, built of dark stone by the eponymous Venetian family after 1413—contrasting spectacularly with the village's white walls below. The ramparts once served as exterior walls for three-story houses built against them inside. The 1956 earthquake left ruins within, but the monumental entrance remains intact and the view from the top over the two bays forming the island's isthmus is extraordinary. Free admission.

The eight windmills aligned along the ridge of Chora, between the village and the castle, are Astypalea's symbol. Their position allowed them to capture north and south winds interchangeably according to season. One has been converted into the municipal library, another into a shop. In the evening, this is the locals' meeting point before descending to the tavernas.

Pera Gialos, the old port at Chora's foot, is Astypalea's most authentic spot: painted wooden fishing boats, tavernas with plastic tables facing the sea, fish market in the morning. This is where the island truly lives, far from the tourist animation of Chora. Stroll the quay in late afternoon when fishermen return.

The Church of Panagia Portaïtissa (18th century), just below the castle, is considered one of the Dodecanese's most beautiful: its carved and gilded wooden iconostasis was built over nine years by Agios Anthimos of Cephalonia, who was blind according to tradition. A copy of the Panagia Portaïtissa icon was brought from Mount Athos.

Pera Gialos's archaeological museum sits directly on the old port waterfront. It houses finds ranging from prehistory to the medieval era, including pieces from the Kylindra necropolis—a unique burial site where 2,700 infant skeletons interred in jars were discovered on the castle hill's flank, spanning a thousand-year period. A local cult whose significance archaeologists still debate. The museum is small but dense: allow 30 to 40 minutes.

activities.text: Astypalea has the shape of a butterfly—two islands connected by an isthmus less than 100 meters wide. Explore the eastern part (Exo Nisi) by scooter or quad: the wild beaches of Kaminakia, Vatses and Agios Konstantinos are accessible via dirt tracks, with electric-blue water and zero tourist infrastructure. Bring water and provisions.

Reserve half a day to reach Vathy by water taxi from Pera Gialos. This natural fjord on the island's east—accessible only by sea or via a poor-condition track—is one of the Dodecanese's most timeless atmospheres: a silent fishing village at the end of a narrow sea arm surrounded by arid hills. A single taverna serves the morning's catch. Boats generally depart late morning.

Have a drink in front of Chora's windmills at sunset—it's the locals' gathering spot. Illuminated Chora gives the impression the village is either climbing the mountain or suspended in the void, depending on the viewing angle. The atmosphere shifts dramatically once night falls.

Astypalea's 'Kopanisti' is a pungent, fermented soft-paste cheese served as mezze. The local 'Pouggia' are filled with almonds and mastiha (mastic resin), a perfumed variation on the specialty you may have tasted in Patmos. Ask for them in Pera Gialos pastry shops.

visits.text: Stefanos Crater, at the center of Nisyros's caldera, is one of the Aegean's most striking geological experiences. The caldera measures 4 km in diameter and Stefanos itself spans 300 meters across and 30 meters deep. The last phreatic eruption occurred in 1888, but hydrothermal activity is very present: sulfur fumaroles, ground hot and yellow underfoot, bubbling mud pots—all with a frank rotten-egg odor. Arrive before 9 am: in peak season, up to 1,000 day-trippers from Kos land in the morning. Closed shoes mandatory—the ground burns in places.

Mandraki, Nisyros's capital, is a compact village with white houses and lanes paved with pebble mosaics—a technique you'll find in squares and even private courtyards. The archaeological museum covers more than 3,000 years of island history, from the Neolithic to the Byzantine era.
