Acropolis of Athens: the Parthenon, built between 447 and 432 BC from 22,000 tonnes of Pentelic marble
Epidaurus: an ancient theatre seating 12,000 whose perfect acoustics still baffle modern engineers
Mystras: a ghost Byzantine citadel where Constantine XI was crowned the last Emperor of Byzantium in 1449
Cape Tenaro: the southernmost point of mainland Greece, once considered a gateway to the Underworld
Olympia: site of the Olympic Games from 776 BC to 393 AD — 1,169 unbroken years
Corfu: a Venetian old town UNESCO-listed, the only Greek city to withstand the Ottomans

itineraries.title: Central Greece & Gulf Coast
itineraries.subtitle: From Athens to the Messolonghi Lagoon via mythical Thebes, Osios Loukas, Delphi and its oracle, the Venetian port of Naupacte and the forgotten sanctuary of Thermos—this route reveals the least-known mainland Greece to travellers.
itineraries.notes: Car rental mandatory—none of the secondary sites are accessible otherwise. Check museum closing days (usually Mondays). Distomo, on the road to Osios Loukas, houses a memorial to the Nazi massacre of June 10, 1944 (218 civilians)—an optional stop for those wishing to pay their respects.

visits.text: Thebes (Thiva in modern Greek) lies 50 km from Athens—roughly an hour's drive. The modern city is built directly atop the ancient Cadmea—the acropolis of the mythical city—which explains why ruins emerge randomly among ordinary streets. According to mythology, this is where Heracles and Dionysus were born, where Oedipus ruled, and where in 335 BC Alexander the Great destroyed the city after a rebellion, either massacring or enslaving all 30,000 inhabitants. The renovated Archaeological Museum is remarkable: the painted funeral steles from ancient Demetrias are exceptionally rare in Greece, and Linear B tablets (Mycenaean script from the 15th century BC) testify to the site's antiquity. Plan for 1h30.

About 500 meters east of the museum, Megalo Kastelli Park contains Mycenaean burial chambers from the 14th-13th centuries BC, dubbed the 'Tomb of Oedipus's Children' by local tradition—a legendary attribution, but the architecture is genuine. Along Pindarou Street (named after the Theban poet Pindar, 518-438 BC), remnants of ancient Cadmea peek out between houses. The medieval tower visible at the museum entrance dates from 13th-century Frankish occupation.

activities.text: Thebes is little-touristed—precisely what makes it an authentic stop. The central square's cafés are frequented exclusively by locals. Lunch at a downtown taverna before heading toward Distomo in the afternoon: the detour to Osios Loukas takes 45 minutes from Thebes.

practical_info.tip: Free parking in front of the archaeological museum. Thebes isn't an ideal place to sleep—it serves better as a morning stop before heading to the Delphi region

visits.text: Osios Loukas Monastery is one of the finest throughout the Mediterranean—and one of the least known to Anglophone travellers. Perched on the flank of Mount Helicon in a cirque of olive and almond trees, it was founded by the hermit Luke of Steiris, born in 896 in Delphi and died in 953 after prophesying the reconquest of Crete from the Arabs (a prediction fulfilled eight years after his death). UNESCO-listed since 1990, its katholikon (completed around 1011) houses the most complete 11th-century gold-ground mosaics in Greece: Pantocrator, Crucifixion, Descent to Hades—an iconographic program of rare coherence and beauty. The polychrome marble, jasper and porphyry pavements are equally exceptional. In the crypt, the saint's relics remain in place. Budget at least 1h30.

Between Thebes and Osios Loukas, a stop in Levadia (Livadia) merits a detour. The town organizes around the spring of Trophonios, an ancient oracle contemporary with Delphi that made consultants enter a terrifying cave—the 2nd-century AD Greek traveller Pausanias left a vivid account. The springs still gush at the foot of a rocky cliff, now developed as a pleasant promenade with water mills and cafés. Levadia's medieval castle (14th century) overlooks the town from the hillside—30 minutes of climbing.

activities.text: In the monastery shop, monks sell their honey, olive oil and preserves. These are exceptional-quality products at very reasonable prices. Before leaving the site, grab a coffee on the small terrace facing the valley—the view of olives descending toward the sea, the silence and mountain air combine into something you won't find anywhere else on this circuit.

practical_info.tip: Strict dress code: shoulders and knees must be covered. The monastery is uncrowded on weekdays—arrive early morning for an almost solitary visit. Entrance €3

visits.text: Arachova, at 960 meters on the slopes of Parnassus, dominates the Delphi-Athens corridor. Its grey-stone houses with red-tile roofs cling to the mountain in multiple levels. On the central square, centuries-old cafés serve local tsipouro and formaela PDO cheese from Arachova—a semi-hard goat cheese whose production has been regulated since 1996. Shops sell hand-woven carpets and woollen textiles, a living craft tradition. In winter, Arachova is a ski resort favored by Athenians—Parnassus lifts are 14 km away.

Just 1 km below Arachova on the Delphi road, the Sanctuary of Athena Pronaia is the first monument you encounter before entering the main site. The Tholos—a 4th-century BC circular temple with three Doric columns still standing on a 15-meter-diameter drum—is Delphi's most photographed image. No one knows its exact purpose. The Castalian Spring gushes right opposite: here the Pythia and pilgrims purified themselves before consulting the oracle. The spring water still flows into the two ancient basins.

activities.text: The winding Arachova-Delphi road offers spectacular views over the olive valley descending toward the Gulf of Corinth—according to some sources, Greece's largest olive grove. Dinner at Arachova's central square with its tavernas is one of the most pleasant evenings on the circuit: valley views, mountain air, and excellent local cuisine centered on roasted lamb and Parnassus cheeses.

practical_info.tip: Arachova is crowded on winter weekends (Athens skiers). On weekdays in off-season, it's calm and charming

visits.text: Delphi was 'the navel of the world' according to ancient Greeks—Zeus released two eagles at Earth's extremes and they met here. On Parnassus's slopes, between two red cliffs of the Phaedriades looming vertically, the archaeological site stretches in terraces. Climb the Sacred Way—the path pilgrims from across the Mediterranean followed to consult the oracle. The Temple of Apollo (6th century BC, rebuilt in the 4th) is now only a rectangle of columns, but the view from its foundations—over the valley, olives and Gulf of Corinth—ranks among Greece's finest. The Pythia delivered oracles from an underground chamber in a trance, likely induced by ethylene vapors rising from a geological fissure—archaeologists have confirmed these emanations.

Above the temple, Delphi's theatre (4th century BC, 5,000 seats) is in remarkable condition. From its upper rows, you dominate the entire sanctuary with Parnassus behind—the site's best photograph. Higher still, the Pythian Games stadium (4th century BC, 7,000 spectators) is Greece's best-preserved after Epidaurus: the 177-meter track with its stone starting blocks is intact. Budget 30 additional minutes for the climb.

Delphi's Archaeological Museum is among Greece's finest. Don't miss the Charioteer of Delphi—a bronze statue from 478 BC commemorating a Pythian Games victory, extraordinarily well-preserved with enamel eyes and bronze lashes. The Sphinx of the Naxians (570 BC) and friezes from the Treasury of the Siphnians (525 BC) illustrate the scale of donations made to the sanctuary by Greek city-states. The omphalos—the marble 'navel of the world'—is here too. Plan 1h30.

activities.text: Late afternoon, descend to Amphissa (15 min) or Itea (25 min) for dinner facing the Gulf of Corinth. Amphissa is a pleasant town with a medieval castle of Frankish lords (13th century) commanding views over the olive plain—30 minutes of climbing. Amphissa's Saturday market is among the region's most authentic.

practical_info.tip: Buy your combined site+museum ticket at the entrance. Full exploration (site + museum + stadium) takes 3-4 hours. Arrive before 9am to avoid tour groups

map_keywords: Port of Galaxidi Gulf of Corinth

visits.text: Galaxidi appears around a coastal bend amid olives and Gulf of Corinth like a 19th-century print. This port of 1,700 inhabitants possesses two natural harbors and a collection of neoclassical mansions built by ship captains who made their fortunes in the Mediterranean. In the 18th-19th centuries, Galaxidi commanded a fleet of 350 vessels trading as far as Marseille and the Black Sea—maritime power disproportionate for such a small town. The 1821 War of Independence largely ended this prosperity. Today its cobbled alleys and softly-colored façades make it one of mainland Greece's most photogenic villages.

Galaxidi's Maritime Museum occupies a renovated former shipyard—excellent for understanding the port's history through well-organized galleries. The collection includes 19th-century sailing-ship paintings (Greece's largest selection), navigation instruments, travel journals and marine charts. Outside the museum, the PeraPanta coastal promenade extends to the statue of a sailor's wife waving her handkerchief toward the sea—a melancholic image that encapsulates the village's history.

activities.text: The Gulf of Corinth coves at Galaxidi's foot are calm and the water is clean. The coastal walk between the village's two ports (Hiriolakas and the main harbor) skirts small pebble beaches with views of the Byzantine islets opposite. If you're here on Saturday, Amphissa's market 30 km away merits an early-morning detour before heading down to Galaxidi.

practical_info.tip: Galaxidi is 25 km from Delphi via the coastal road through Itea—one of the circuit's most pleasant drives, between olives and sea

visits.text: Naupacte's port is among Greece's most picturesque—a circular basin ringed by 15th-century Venetian ramparts, with a lighthouse, century-old plane trees and cafés spilling onto the quays. Here, under its Venetian name of Lepanto, occurred on October 7, 1571 the greatest galley-battle in history: the Holy League (Spain, Venice, Rome) versus the Ottoman fleet. 300 ships on one side, 330 on the other. The Ottoman fleet was destroyed—187 of its 251 vessels sunk, over 20,000 dead. Among the Holy League soldiers was a 23-year-old Spanish youth named Miguel de Cervantes, who took two arquebus shots to the chest and lost use of his left hand. His statue now stands on the port.

The climb to the Venetian citadel is steep but rewarding: walls descend from the hilltop in several concentric rings to the sea, a defensive system among Greece's best-preserved. From the summit, views encompass the Gulf of Corinth, the cable-stayed Rio-Antirio Bridge (2004, one of the world's longest with 2,252 meters of suspended span and 2,880 meters total) and on clear days, the Peloponnese mountains. The Botsaris Tower on the ascent is a 15th-century structure housing a museum dedicated to the Battle of Lepanto.

activities.text: The coastal road from Galaxidi to Naupacte hugs the Gulf of Corinth for 70 km—one of mainland Greece's finest drives, between sea and wooded cliffs. Psani and Gribovo beaches at Naupacte hold Blue Flags and the water is excellent. For evening, the port terraces beneath plane trees are ideal for seafood dinner—dried octopi hanging on washing lines above fishing boats epitomize the port.

practical_info.tip: The GR-48 coastal road from Galaxidi to Naupacte is spectacular—take time to stop at viewpoints

visits.text: Messolonghi is the city of heroes. In 1826, after two successive sieges, the exhausted Greek garrison attempted a desperate breakout against Ottoman besiegers—the Exodus of Messolonghi. More than 5,000 people, soldiers and civilians, perished. The Garden of Heroes, created in 1830 by Governor Kapodistrias, holds their graves and that of poet Lord Byron, who died here in April 1824 of fever, age 36, after financing and supporting the Greek cause. His heart is buried here; his body was returned to England. It has become a pilgrimage site for Greeks.

Just outside town, Kleisova Lagoon is a remarkable ecosystem: 320 km² of lagoons and salt marshes hosting nearly 270 bird species and constituting one of Greece's largest salt reserves. The 'pelades'—small fishermen's houses on stilts in the lagoon—are a unique Mediterranean landscape. The Salt Museum, housed in a restored former salt works, recounts this salt economy that has structured the region since antiquity.

Messolonghi's Museum of Art and History, in a well-restored 1932 neoclassical building, displays archaeological objects from the region (prehistory to Roman era) and a complete section on the 1821-1826 War of Independence with documents, weapons and portraits of combatants. Four Greek prime ministers born in this town signal Messolonghi's importance in modern Greece.

activities.text: A boat ride in Kleisova Lagoon, arranged by local fishermen, is among the circuit's most singular experiences—stilt fisheries, pink flamingos in season and the silence of this water expanse at sunset provide total contrast to the previous days' archaeological sites.

practical_info.tip: Messolonghi is also known for smoked lagoon fish (red mullet, eel, grey mullet) sold at the local market—a specialty not to miss

visits.text: The archaeological site of Thermos is among this circuit's least-known destinations—and one of the most important for Greek architectural history. Federal capital of the Aetolian League, the sanctuary was the Western Greek equivalent of Delphi. Its Temple of Apollo Thermos, dated 630-610 BC, is one of the oldest known Doric temples—38 meters long, 12 meters wide, 15 columns per long side. The site reveals in visible layers the evolution of Greek sacred architecture: from the Mycenaean megaron (Megaron A, Mycenaean period) to the archaic Doric temple. The painted terracotta metopes, among Greece's oldest, are displayed in the National Museum of Athens—the small local museum preserves some fragments.

Lake Trichonida, 6 km southeast of Agrinio, is Greece's largest natural lake at 98.6 km². A Natura 2000 zone, it hosts nearly 200 bird species including grey herons, storks and pink flamingos during migration season. The road along its northern shore is pleasant, with fish-tavernas directly on the water serving freshwater fish. From Agrinio, it's 15 minutes' drive.

activities.text: Agrinio is the capital of Aetolia-Acarnania—a lively city with little inherent tourist appeal, but marking the logical circuit end before reaching Aktion Airport (Preveza, 70 km, 55 min) for connections to the Ionian Islands, or resuming toward Athens (240 km, 2h30 by highway). Lunch on Lake Trichonida's shores before choosing your direction.

practical_info.tip: Aktion Airport (Preveza) serves Athens and some European summer flights. Otherwise, Agrinio is 2h30 from Athens via the A5 (Ionia highway)

seo.h1_title: Central Greece & Gulf Coast - 8 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Between Athens and the Ionian Sea, central Greece concentrates extraordinary sites that most travellers barely skim. Delphi and Osios Loukas Monastery are UNESCO-listed; Thebes remains one of the most myth-laden ancient cities (Oedipus, Heracles, Alexander the Great); and Naupacte hosted the Battle of Lepanto in 1571. This 8-day circuit is among the rare ones covering the Athens-Messolonghi corridor continuously, without backtracking, from Boeotian plains to Aetolian lagoons.
seo.seo_hook: Delphi + Osios Loukas (2× UNESCO) + Naupacte (Lepanto) + Messolonghi (Lord Byron): forgotten central Greece, no queues.
featured_list: Delphi: Apollo's oracle, Sacred Way, ancient theatre—navel of the world in ancient eyes
Osios Loukas: 11th-century golden Byzantine mosaics, UNESCO alongside Daphni Monastery and Nea Moni
Naupacte: fortified Venetian port, site of the Battle of Lepanto (1571), Cervantes statue
Messolonghi: Garden of Heroes, Kleisova Lagoon, town where Lord Byron died in 1824

places_taglines.GR::thebes: Mythic Boeotian city, birthplace of Oedipus and Heracles, destroyed by Alexander the Great in 335 BC
places_taglines.GR::distomo: Boeotian village, gateway to the Byzantine monastery of Osios Loukas, UNESCO-listed since 1990
places_taglines.GR::arachova: Mountain village on Parnassus at 960 meters altitude, 10 km from Delphi, renowned for formaela cheese
places_taglines.GR::delphi: Pan-Hellenic sanctuary of Apollo, seat of the Pythia's oracle, UNESCO site, ancient world's navel
places_taglines.GR::galaxidi: Neoclassical maritime port on the Gulf of Corinth, once commanded a fleet of 350 ships in the 18th-19th centuries
places_taglines.GR::naupacte: Venetian port on the Gulf of Corinth, theatre of the Battle of Lepanto (1571) where Cervantes was wounded
places_taglines.GR::messolonghi: City of Greek independence heroes (1821-1826), Kleisova Lagoon, site of Lord Byron's death
places_taglines.GR::agrinio: Capital of Aetolia-Acarnania, gateway to Lake Trichonida and the ancient sanctuary of Thermos

seo_keywords: mainland greece off the beaten path

highlights: Delphi: 'navel of the world' in antiquity, Temple of Apollo and Pythia's oracle, UNESCO site
Osios Loukas: finest Byzantine monastery on mainland Greece, 11th-century golden mosaics, UNESCO-listed
Naupacte: Venetian port encircled by ramparts where the Battle of Lepanto unfolded (1571)—Cervantes lost use of one hand
Messolonghi: city of Greek independence heroes (1821-1826), Kleisova Lagoon and Garden of Heroes
Thermos: sanctuary of the Aetolian League with one of Greece's oldest Doric temples (630-610 BC)

itineraries.title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves
itineraries.subtitle: 19 sailing stages in a loop from Paros, from the Lesser Cyclades to the volcanic southern islands
itineraries.notes: Itinerary designed for a loop sailing cruise from Paros. All legs are by boat. Actual duration: 19 sailing days.

visits.text: Parikia's old harbor greets you on arrival with its whitewashed windmills standing sentinel on the breakwater—Cycladic postcard perfection in the flesh. Behind the ferries and bustling quays, the old town weaves through pebble-paved lanes around a Venetian kastro built in 1260 from marble blocks salvaged from a nearby Temple of Athena: look carefully and you'll still spot ancient columns embedded in the walls. The old market follows the main street to the kastro and brims with tavernas, bakeries, and little boutiques. Get lost in this maze—the late afternoon light washing over the white facades is worth more than any guidebook.

The Panagia Ekatontapiliani basilica—'the church of a hundred doors'—ranks as Greece's third-largest early Christian sanctuary. Legend traces its foundation to the 4th century by Saint Helena, mother of Constantine the Great; the present building dates from the 6th century and was restored under Justinian. The complex sits within a 252-meter perimeter wall and shelters a main church measuring 40 by 25 meters, flanked by the oldest baptistery in the Christian East. Tradition holds that the hundredth door remains invisible and will open only when Constantinople is reclaimed. Visit early in the morning—the filtered light in the nave, the Doric columns salvaged from a Roman gymnasium, and the 15th-century iconostasis are worth the stop. It's also a major pilgrimage site for the Assumption, rivaling even Tinos.

activities.text: Swimming at Livadia Beach, the easiest beach to reach from the harbor—10 minutes on foot heading west. Fine sand, shallow water, perfect for a first plunge after the crossing.
Sheltered anchorage available at Krios Beach depending on conditions, just across from Parikia harbor—dinghy shuttle, good holding on sandy bottom.

visits.text: Naoussa concentrates all of Paros's charm in one village. The fishing harbor is ringed by whitewashed lanes climbing toward the Kastro—a 15th-century Venetian fortification partially submerged, its round tower still standing guard at the harbor entrance. The old Venetian warehouses have transformed into elegant restaurants serving the morning catch. Naoussa actually boasts the largest fishing fleet in all the Cyclades: watch fishermen mending their nets early in the morning before the terraces fill. Come evening, the kastro lanes morph into an open-air lounge.

Kolymbithres, 4 km northwest of Naoussa, is Paros's most photographed spot. Giant granite boulders, polished by millennia of wind and sea spray, have carved out natural basins filled with translucent saltwater—somewhere between swimming pools and stone sculptures. The water often sits less than a meter deep and takes on milky-blue reflections against the pearl-gray stone. In July–August, the pools fill by 11 a.m.: arrive before 10 for first dibs or come late in the day. Caïques make the run from Naoussa harbor.

activities.text: Anchorage at Naoussa marina or Ormos Plastira depending on conditions—good holding on sandy bottom, sheltered from the north.
Snorkeling in the coves of Kolymbithres: the granite formations create tunnels and underwater cavities accessible with just a mask. Colorful fish guaranteed.

visits.text: The Portara greets you at the entrance to Naxos harbor—this monumental white marble gateway standing 6 meters tall is all that remains of a temple dedicated to Apollo, whose construction began around 530 BC under the tyrant Lygdamis. The building was never completed, and the Venetians carted off all the blocks to build the kastro, except these four 20-ton giants, too heavy to budge. The Portara faces toward Delos, Apollo's birthplace, and legend places Theseus's abandonment of Ariadne here. Sunset from the causeway is a popular classic—for crowd-free photos, go early in the morning.

The Kastro of Naxos Chora was erected in 1207 by Marco Sanudo, the Doge of Venice's nephew, after the Fourth Crusade. He moved the island's capital from the heights down to the coast. The inner precinct, accessible only through two gates—one still sporting its original 13th-century wooden door—shelters noble residences bearing carved Venetian coats of arms, the Catholic cathedral, the archaeological museum housed in the former Jesuit school (Nikos Kazantzákis studied here), and vaulted lanes nearly deserted. A time warp 15 minutes on foot from the harbor.

The Bourgos quarter, between the harbor and the Kastro, packs in artisan workshops, marble studios, tavernas, and galleries. Naxos has been a marble island since antiquity—the unfinished kouros statues abandoned in the quarries during the Archaic period still bear witness. Push open the workshop doors: sculptors still work the local white stone.

activities.text: Sunset facing the Portara from anchorage or the islet causeway—the white marble turns amber, the sea turns gold. One of the Aegean's most photographed spectacles.

Waterfront stroll and provisioning at the harbor market: Naxos is the largest island in the Cyclades and its hinterland produces renowned cheeses, honey, citron, and potatoes. The market is well stocked.

visits.text: Welcome to the Lesser Cyclades. Iraklia counts just 150 permanent residents across 18 km² and electricity only arrived in 1982. The port of Agios Georgios is endearing: the entire village descends to greet the ferry, collect deliveries, and chat under the tamarisks. Livadi beach, 1.5 km from the port, is the island's finest—a long crescent of blonde sand lapped by turquoise water where you'll find space even in high August. No umbrellas for rent on site, bring your own.

activities.text: Snorkeling and swimming in Livadi bay: the crystalline waters let you spot fish, sea urchins, and if you're lucky, sea turtles that regularly visit the bay.

Hike to the village of Panagia (Chora), 4 km from the port via a marked trail. This inland hamlet is the island's traditional capital, with its Cycladic stone houses and whitewashed church. Those with energy left can continue to the Agios Ioannis cave, considered the largest in the Cyclades with 2 km of stalactite-adorned galleries—bring a flashlight and closed-toe shoes.

practical_info.duration: ~1h30 round trip to Panagia, 3h to the cave

visits.text: Schinoussa is even smaller than Iraklia—8 km², fewer than 300 residents. Its main village, Chora (or Panagia), perches 1.5 km from Mersini port: a single street you can walk down in 5 minutes, lined with family tavernas and geranium-draped houses. Here, everyone knows everyone and newcomers quickly blend in. Come evening, Chora comes alive on the central square: locals and the Greek vacationers who make up most of the island's clientele gather over a warm rakomelo. Push open the door of kafeneio Xara—an institution.

Tsigouri beach is Schinoussa's most accessible, 600 meters from Chora via a paved road. Golden sand, shallow water, and tamarisks for shade—it's the only spot on the island offering this comfort. A beach bar serves drinks and mezedes. For a wilder experience, Psili Ammos beach is worth the 45-minute walk from Chora: a pristine bay with water of surreal clarity, no infrastructure whatsoever. Arrive early in summer.

activities.text: Swimming and lounging at Tsigouri—or swim out to the small islet 200 meters offshore for absolute solitude.
Short coastal hike to Livadi or Aligaria beach: Schinoussa's trails link the beaches one to another, all within 40 minutes on foot from Chora. No roads between them, just hillside paths.

visits.text: Koufonissia—actually two islands, Ano and Kato Koufonissi—is the rising star of the Lesser Cyclades. Ano Koufonissi's Chora, the only inhabited village, sprawls around a colorful harbor dominated by the church of Agios Georgios. But what makes the island unique is its southeastern coast: a 45-minute coastal path from Chora links the beaches of Finikas, Fanos, and Italida—surreal turquoise waters on golden sand—before reaching Pori, the archipelago's finest beach. At Pori's northern tip, the Ksilobatis caves offer limestone arches bathed in emerald water. Budget 3 hours round trip for the full circuit from Chora, no shade along the way: leave early and pack water.

Gala Beach, between Pori and Italida, is a hidden cove whose Greek name means 'milk'—the cave's wave foam does indeed give the water a unique milky tint. Just beyond lies the Eye of the Devil, a natural rock pool with a diving platform. These two curiosities sit 5 minutes' walk from Pori.

activities.text: Sea kayaking from Ammos beach toward the cliffs of Kato Koufonissi: paddling along the sheer walls reveals arches and coves invisible from land. Kayaks are for rent at the main beach. Check the wind (meltemi) before setting out.
Anchorage in Pori bay: sandy bottom, good holding depending on conditions. Water taxis from Chora make 4 runs per day—handy for alternating between swimming and exploring Chora.

visits.text: Katapola, one of Amorgos's two ports, opens onto spectacular mountain scenery. Amorgos Chora, at 320 meters elevation, is linked to the port by a winding road—take the municipal bus or hike 45 minutes up the switchback trail. At the top, white windmills keep watch over the village from a wind-lashed ridge, and Venetian coats of arms still adorn old doorways.

The Panagia Hozoviotissa monastery is one of the Aegean's marvels. Founded in the 11th century by Byzantine Emperor Alexios I Komnenos to shelter a miraculous icon from Palestine, it's built literally into a cliff face 300 meters above the sea. The building stands 40 meters tall yet only 5 meters wide in places—8 floors carved into rock, a labyrinth of small rooms and narrow staircases. You'll need to climb about 300 steps to reach it from the road. The monks (3 or 4 remain) welcome visitors with Amorgian raki, loukoumi, and Greek coffee. Modest dress required—garments are loaned at the entrance. Luc Besson filmed scenes from The Big Blue here in 1987.

Agia Anna beach lies below the monastery at the foot of the cliffs. Turquoise water, pebbly bottom, access via stairs from the road. This is where the monastery's icon supposedly washed ashore mysteriously on a boat from Palestine. Open sea, sometimes with swell.

activities.text: Waterfront stroll at Katapola and provisioning: Amorgos is known for its honey, local wine, and sun-dried octopus. The harbor market is modest but authentic.
Easy hike between Chora and the windmills for panoramas over the archipelago—on clear days, the view reaches Naxos and Paros.
Swimming at Agia Anna (open sea, possible swell)—cliff diving is an island classic.

visits.text: Ios has a dual identity you'll grasp on arrival: Chora perched on its hill is an authentic Cycladic maze of whitewashed lanes adorned with bougainvillea and 12 windmills—but come nightfall, the bars take over and the island has been one of the Aegean's party hotspots for over 40 years. If you're seeking tranquility, climb to Chora early and head down before noon. For history buffs, the prehistoric site of Skarkos, 10 minutes from Chora, is one of the best-preserved Minoan settlements in the Aegean. The hill said to hold Homer's tomb is accessible via trails north of the island.

Mylopotas is Ios's largest beach—a sand crescent over 1 km long facing due west, Blue Flag and crystalline waters. It's also the liveliest: beach clubs, wakeboarding, diving, evening concerts. The eastern part of the beach is calmer than the western end where nightlife concentrates. The bay offers well-held anchorage on sandy bottom, but very busy in July–August.

activities.text: Swimming and paddleboarding in Mylopotas bay—SUP and canoe rental on the beach. Waters stay shallow for 5 to 10 meters from shore.
Anchorage in the bay (good holding on sand, high seasonal crowding). In season, better to drop anchor arriving early morning.

visits.text: Santorini—the caldera and its perched villages seen from the sea offer one of the Mediterranean's most spectacular maritime entrances. Vlychada harbor on the south coast is calmer than the crowded caldera and ringed by lunar-white cliffs. It's the best overnight anchorage. The volcanic sand beach of Perissa and Perivolos stretches 7 km: black sand burning hot (bring sandals), crystalline water, and gently sloping sandy bottom. In high season, it's one of the island's liveliest.

The Akrotiri archaeological site is Santorini's equivalent of Pompeii—a Minoan city buried under meters of volcanic ash during the catastrophic eruption around 1500 BC. Excavations began in 1967 under Spyridon Marinatos. Beneath a massive protective dome, you wander the ancient city streets: two-story houses with sewer systems, murals depicting daily life, pottery, tools. No bodies were found, suggesting inhabitants fled before the eruption.

Red Beach sits 5 minutes from Akrotiri on foot: a blood-red cliff plunges vertically into the sea, creating a striking contrast with the blue water. The beach is narrow and crowded; access is sometimes restricted due to rockfall risk in summer. Go early or enjoy it from the sea.

activities.text: Swimming on the volcanic beach of Perissa or Perivolos—the black sand absorbs heat, consider water shoes. Water is deep and clear.

Wine tasting in Santorini—the island's vineyards produce assyrtiko, a mineral white unique in the world, cultivated in basket-shaped (kouloura) vines on volcanic soil. Several estates are accessible from anchorage or by road.

visits.text: Folegandros is an island of character—lashed by the meltemi, with 300-meter cliffs plunging into intense blue sea, and a Chora perched on a vertiginous ledge that long served as a place of political exile (until 1969). The main village is entirely pedestrian: three beautiful squares lined up and shaded by plane trees, stone-paved lanes, an inhabited Venetian kastro, and the persistent feeling that time passes differently here. From Chora, the switchback trail (20-30 min) leads to the Panagia church on its promontory—the view over the Aegean and neighboring islands at sunset ranks among the Cyclades' most spectacular.

Agkali (or Angali) beach on the south coast is accessible by car or bus from Chora. Pebble bottom sheltered by a cliff, deep blue water, two tavernas under the tamarisks. No natural shade except at the cliff base—bring an umbrella. From there, a 30-minute coastal trail leads to the wild cove of Agios Nikolaos, a favorite among island regulars.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling at Agkali: the rocky seabed shelters varied marine life. The cove is sheltered enough to be pleasant even in moderate wind.
Sunset from anchorage or Panagia church—the golden light on Folegandros's white cliffs is a spectacle that regulars return for every summer.

visits.text: Sikinos is the least visited of the inhabited Cyclades—300 residents, minimal infrastructure, no hotel chains. Alopronia port is a peaceful fishing village with a small, calm sandy beach. Then take the road (or better, the paved mule path) climbing 4 km to the Kastro and Chora, perched at 280 meters—two quarters facing each other, the first lively with cafés and the town hall, the second a labyrinth of white lanes bordered by bougainvillea. Three windmills stand watch over the ensemble from the cliff edge, facing the sea.

activities.text: Climb to Zoodochou Pigi monastery, built in 1690 atop the Kastro: panoramic view over the archipelago and beyond. The island's most astonishing monument, however, is Episkopi—a 3rd-century Roman mausoleum converted to a Christian temple in the 17th century, accessible via trail from the Kastro. The place radiates a particular energy.
Swimming on Alopronia beach at the foot of the port—fine sand, calm water, and tamarisks. It's the island's most accessible beach, perfect for a pause before setting sail again.

visits.text: Milos is the volcanic island par excellence—over 70 beaches, each different in sand color and cliff shape. Kleftiko, on the southwest coast, is the showstopper: ancient white limestone formations riddled with sea caves, accessible only by water. It was a Saracen pirate lair, and the name recalls that (kleftiko = bandits' place). Today, boat excursions depart each morning from Adamas. The caves are swim-accessible from the boat—filtered underwater light creates mesmerizing blue and green hues. Dolphins, turtles, and seals frequent the area.

Adamas, Milos's main port, sits on one of the Mediterranean's largest natural bays. The waterfront is lively and well supplied. From the port, boat excursions connect Kleftiko, Sarakiniko (the lunar scenery of the north), and the sirmata—these colorful rock-carved boat garages on the shore, Milos's symbol.

activities.text: Snorkeling at Kleftiko: the sea caves are swim-accessible from boat excursions. Bring mask and fins. Don't land from your sailboat as access is regulated—daytime anchorage only.

visits.text: Kimolos takes its name from the Greek 'kimolia'—chalk—and its houses are indeed whiter than white. The island covers just 36 km² and is easily explored on foot or by scooter from Psathi port. The village of Chorio, perched on a hill 2 km from the port, is dominated by a 14th-century Venetian kastro, some of whose houses are still inhabited. The metropolitan church of Panagia Odigitria (1592) is its religious heart. Wandering Chorio, look for the syrmata at tiny Goupa beach: these are former boat garages carved into rock, intact maritime heritage.

Prassa beach (also called Agios Georgios) is unanimously considered Kimolos's finest—some say of all the Cyclades. Almost surreal white sand, turquoise water of exceptional clarity. A beach bar, sun loungers, and sea caves within flipper range. Near Prassa, natural hot springs with reddish waters supposedly have healing properties according to locals. Spotting a monk seal in the neighboring waters isn't rare.

Skiadi rock is worth the 20-30 minute walk from the main road: erosion has sculpted a formation shaped like a giant mushroom, unique in the Mediterranean. Look for the marked path between Chorio and the west coast.

activities.text: Swimming at Prassa and snorkeling around the rock formations: corals, sea urchins, and colorful reef fish in remarkably clear water. The nearby Vromolini sea caves will please freedivers.

visits.text: Sifnos is the Cyclades' island of ceramics and gastronomy. Since at least the 9th century BC, the island has exported utilitarian pottery throughout the Mediterranean—Sifnos clay is rich in feldspars that speed vitrification. In the 18th and 19th centuries, about a hundred workshops ran at full tilt. Today a handful of potters perpetuate the tradition: at Platys Gialos and Vathy, you'll still see the old pottery buildings recognizable by their curved roof and elongated shape. Several workshops welcome visitors.

Platys Gialos is Sifnos's largest sandy beach—a long bay well exposed with Blue Flag, waterfront tavernas, and practical anchorage to the left of the bay. The White Tower sits 1.5 km northeast. A 2 km walk southeast leads to Chryssopigi beach, the island's most emblematic, with its little white monastery perched on a rocky spur—one of Sifnos's most photographed images.

activities.text: Swimming and paddleboarding at Platys Gialos: wide sheltered bay, shallow water, fine sand. SUP rental on the beach.

visits.text: Ormos Vathy is one of the Cyclades' finest anchorages—a perfectly sheltered horseshoe bay with a small fishing village at the back. On nearby Tsopos beach, the Atsonios pottery has operated for 4 generations: it's one of the last workshops set up the old way, right on the beach, with wood-fired kilns and drying tables. Watch the potter work before choosing from the utilitarian ceramics and artisan pieces on display.

Apollonia, Sifnos's capital, perches in the hills at the island's center. It's a maze of paved lanes where you'll happily get lost among artisan shops, traditional tavernas, and small flower-filled squares. It was founded in the 6th century BC and bears the god Apollo's name. Come evening, it's the island's liveliest spot—open terraces, Greek music, warm ambiance without Mykonos excess.

activities.text: Walk in the island's south from Vathy to Faros—a fishing hamlet with its small sheltered beach in a former pottery port converted to a restaurant. The coastal trail skirts wild coves accessible on foot or by swim.

visits.text: Megalo Livadi is a village out of time in southwest Serifos, 30 minutes of curves from the port. This is where the island's grand industrial history played out—until 1963, Serifos's iron mines (exploited since antiquity, reactivated at large scale from 1880 by a Franco-German company) loaded their ore from this quay onto cargo ships bound for Europe. The rusted metal loading bridge still stands, cumbersome and poetic. In 1916, a miners' strike led to a bloody confrontation with gendarmes—4 miners killed. It's considered the first bloody labor strike in modern Greek history. A memorial on the quay commemorates this episode.

activities.text: Sunset from anchorage or the surrounding hills—Megalo Livadi bay is well sheltered and faces west. A small waterfront taverna serves good fresh fish.

visits.text: Kolona is one of the Cyclades' great surprises. A slender sand tombolo—a natural tongue barely a few meters wide—connects the main island to the islet of Agios Loukas, creating two back-to-back beaches with different water temperatures: the right one is slightly warmer because hot springs surface nearby. It's rare to find such a formation in the Mediterranean. Access via dirt track (prefer the water taxi from Merichas, 15 min)—no shade on the beach, bring sun protection.

Dryopida, Kythnos's second village, deserves a 20-minute detour from Kolona. Its red-tiled lanes break from the usual Cycladic white—this is where the island's characteristic tiles have been made for centuries. Katafyki cave, beneath the village, sheltered residents during World War II. It's open in season.

activities.text: Swimming between two seas: swim on one side then the other of the tombolo to feel the temperature difference. Snorkeling possible around the islet's rocks.

visits.text: Despotiko is an uninhabited islet 700 meters southwest of Antiparos, and it's one of recent decades' archaeological revelations in the Cyclades. Systematic excavations led since 2001 by archaeologist Yannos Kouragios have unearthed a sanctuary of Apollo dating from the 8th century BC—20 buildings, over 70 fragments of archaic marble kouroi, inscribed pottery shards bearing Apollo's name. Like Delos, the island was a pilgrimage site attracting visitors from across the Aegean. The temple's Doric columns are under restoration. Access only by sea from Antiparos.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling around Despotiko: the crystalline, lightly visited waters let you observe preserved marine life. Anchorage on sandy bottom in the small Livadi bay.

visits.text: Return along Paros's west coast from Antiparos—a discreet passage between the two islands, with the white marble quarries from antiquity visible on the slopes. Parikia rediscovered feels almost a relief after the wild anchorages of recent days: wifi, fresh water, provisions, and the lively Chora for a final dinner facing the sea.

activities.text: Final quick swim depending on timing and weather—Krios beach, dinghy-accessible from anchorage, is perfect for a farewell plunge in still-turquoise water.

seo.h1_title: Cyclades Sailing Odyssey: Islands & Hidden Coves
seo.intro_paragraph: Sail in a loop from Paros, from the Byzantine hundred-door church to the granite formations of Kolymbithres, before diving into the unspoiled Lesser Cyclades of Iraklia, Schinoussa, and Koufonissia. Cross to Amorgos and its cliff monastery, contrast with Ios's energy, then head north to Milos and its pirate caves before closing the loop via Sifnos and Antiparos.
seo.seo_hook: A 21-day sailing cruise through the Cyclades: from wild coves to cliffside monasteries, from preserved villages to volcanic lunar beaches.
featured_list: Paros: harbor and 4th-century early Christian basilica
Lesser Cyclades: timeless Iraklia, Schinoussa, Koufonissia
Amorgos: monastery clinging to a 300-meter cliff
Santorini: Minoan city of Akrotiri and volcanic beaches
Milos: Kleftiko and its pirate caves
Despotiko: archaic sanctuary of Apollo under excavation

places_taglines.GR::parikia-paros: Where Byzantine domes meet Venetian ramparts above a working fishing harbor
places_taglines.GR::naousa-paros: A Cycladic fishing village commanding the archipelago's largest fleet
places_taglines.GR::naxos-chora: Where a temple to Apollo was never finished and marble still gleams at sunset
places_taglines.GR::iraklia-livadi: An island where electricity arrived in 1982 and solitude remains guaranteed
places_taglines.GR::schinoussa-tsigouri: Where every beach lies within a forty-minute walk and not a road connects them
places_taglines.GR::koufonisia-pori: Twin islands whose coastal path threads turquoise coves no photograph captures honestly
places_taglines.GR::amorgos-katapola: Where Byzantine monks carved eight floors into a sheer cliff three hundred meters above the sea
places_taglines.GR::ios-mylopotas: An island split between moonlit revelry and a kilometer of pristine golden sand
places_taglines.GR::santorini-vlychada: Where lunar-white cliffs shelter a harbor beneath the caldera's volcanic drama
places_taglines.GR::folegandros-agkali: A wind-lashed rock crowned by a village that time and exile couldn't humble
places_taglines.GR::sikinos-alopronia: The Cyclades' quietest inhabited island, where a Roman tomb became a church
places_taglines.GR::milos-adamas: Seventy beaches, one volcanic bay, and caves that once hid Saracen pirates
places_taglines.GR::kimolos-rema: Where the houses glow whiter than anywhere else and the sand rivals the Caribbean
places_taglines.GR::sifnos-platys-gialos: An island that's fed the Aegean pottery and cooking for three thousand years
places_taglines.GR::sifnos-vathy: A horseshoe bay where potters still fire kilns on the beach as their ancestors did
places_taglines.GR::serifos-megalo-livadi: Where iron miners struck in 1916 and a rusted loading bridge remembers
places_taglines.GR::kythnos-kolona: A sand tongue connecting two seas at different temperatures
places_taglines.GR::antiparos: Gateway to an uninhabited sanctuary where Apollo's temple is rising from the earth again

highlights: Loop sailing cruise from Paros with no return constraint
Unspoiled Lesser Cyclades: Iraklia, Schinoussa, Koufonissia
Hozoviotissa Monastery clinging to a 300-meter cliff on Amorgos
Akrotiri archaeological site, Greece's Pompeii, on Santorini

itineraries.title: Ionian Islands Road Trip: Corfu, Paxos & Lefkada
itineraries.subtitle: From Corfu's UNESCO-listed Venetian old town to the white cliffs of Lefkada, a journey through history, turquoise coves and the Ionian Sea.
specialties: olive oil from centuries-old Venetian groves

map_keywords: Palace of St Michael and St George Corfu

visits.text: Corfu's old town has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2007 — a maze of narrow cobblestone lanes known as kantounia, lined with neoclassical facades in pastel hues inherited from centuries of Venetian rule. Venice held the island almost uninterrupted from 1386 to 1797, and it's the Republic that shaped this unique urban fabric, which neither the French nor the British managed to erase. Get up early: the lanes are stunning before 9am, empty and gilded by the morning light.

The Old Fortress (Palaio Frourio) sits on a rocky headland separated from the town by a sea moat — the Contrafossa — which the Venetians dug to turn the citadel into an artificial island. It successfully repelled three major Ottoman sieges: 1537, 1571 and 1716. Inside, don't miss the Church of St George, whose golden facade resembles an ancient temple — the contrast is striking. Climb to the lighthouse at the top for the best panoramic view over the sea and the town.

The New Fortress (Fortezza Nuova), built by the Venetians from 1577 on the hill overlooking the harbour, draws fewer visitors than the Old one — take advantage of that. Its underground galleries, bastions and tunnels carved into the rock form a fascinating labyrinth. The view over the harbour and the Ionian Sea from the battlements alone makes it worth the visit.

The Palace of St Michael and St George, built by the British between 1819 and 1824 in neoclassical style, now houses the Museum of Asian Art — a collection of 11,000 pieces from China, Japan and India, bequeathed by a Greek diplomat. An unexpected combination for a Greek island. The entrance from the People's Garden also offers a fine angle on the Spianada.

The Spianada Esplanade is the largest green public square in Greece. The arcaded Liston that lines its western edge was built during the French occupation, directly inspired by the Rue de Rivoli in Paris. This is where the Corfiots still play cricket, a tradition inherited from the British — one of the few places in Greece where the sport is practised. Late in the afternoon, grab a table on the terrace and watch local life unfold.

activities.text: Lose yourself in the Campiello, the oldest neighbourhood in the old town, north of the Old Fortress. It's a tangle of kantounia so narrow that two people can barely pass, with carved Venetian wells popping up in tiny squares. Laundry dries between buildings, bougainvillea spills over ochre walls — the atmosphere is light-years away from mass tourism.

On Orthodox Easter evening (in April), the Corfiots celebrate the Resurrection by hurling ceramic jars from their windows at 11pm sharp — a loud, festive spectacle unique to Greece. If you're not here for Easter, the religious processions around the relics of Saint Spyridon, the island's patron saint, are still well worth stopping for.

Pastitsada is Corfu's signature dish: rooster (or veal) braised in a tomato sauce with Venetian spices, served over thick macaroni. The recipe is a direct trace of four centuries of Italian influence. Try it in an old-town taverna — the spots around the Campiello are the most authentic.

Bourdeto is Corfu's other pride: fish (scorpionfish by preference) cooked in a tomato sauce fired up with hot paprika. It's a fisherman's recipe — rustic and bold. Pair it with a glass of Robola, the dry, mineral white from neighbouring Cephalonia, which cuts through the acidity of the sauce.

visits.text: The Achilleion is one of the most romantic — and most tragic — palaces in the Mediterranean. Empress Sisi had it built between 1889 and 1891 in a neo-Pompeian style, 10 km south of the capital, on a 200,000 m² plot overlooking the Ionian Sea. She had taught herself Ancient Greek to read Homer in the original and wanted to pay tribute to Achilles, whom she saw as the embodiment of the Greek soul. After her assassination by an anarchist in 1898, Kaiser Wilhelm II bought the palace in 1907 and spent every spring there until World War I. In 1981, the first floor served as the casino set for the James Bond film For Your Eyes Only. Allow at least 1h30 for the gardens and the apartments.

The peninsula of Kanoni, 4 km south of the capital, offers Corfu's most iconic view: in the foreground, Vlacherna Monastery perched on its tiny islet connected to the shore by a causeway, and behind it, the islet of Pontikonisi ('Mouse Island') crowned with dark cypresses. Homeric legend says it's the Phaeacian ship turned to stone by Poseidon after bringing Odysseus home to Ithaca. Arrive in the late afternoon for the best light.

Paleokastritsa, 25 km to the northwest, is one of the most beautiful natural sites in the Ionian Islands: six interlocking bays, crystal-clear turquoise waters and limestone cliffs rising 30 to 50 metres. The Monastery of Panagia Theotokos, founded in 1225 on a rocky headland above the main bay, was according to tradition established after the discovery of an icon of the Virgin. The present buildings date from the 18th century. Modest dress is required to enter — cover-up garments are provided at the door. The view from the monastery gardens over the sea is among the finest on the island.

The hilltop villages of Lakones and Makrades, above Paleokastritsa, offer plunging views over the six coves of the bay — a panorama the locals call the 'Balcony of Corfu'. These olive-grove villages have kept their traditional architecture and their unpretentious tavernas. Stop for a coffee or a simple terrace lunch.

Corfu's Archaeological Museum, in town, houses the pediment of the Artemision, a 6th-century BC temple of Artemis discovered on the island: the central Gorgon stands nearly 3 metres tall and ranks among the best-preserved archaic sculptures in Greece. Allow about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Book a boat trip from Paleokastritsa to explore the sea caves along the northwest coast. Small boats slip into limestone cavities where sunlight creates blue and green reflections on the water — local guides call one of them the 'Blue Light Cave'. The experience is at its best in the morning, when the sea is calm.

Take a dip in Agios Spyridon bay at Paleokastritsa — it's the most accessible beach in the resort, a mix of sand and pebbles with shallow waters. Several outfitters rent sea kayaks to explore the coves that can't be reached on foot. Expect to pay around €25–35 for a half-day kayak rental.

Sofrito is Corfu's quintessential Venetian dish: thin slices of veal pan-fried in a sauce of garlic, parsley, white wine and vinegar. The recipe dates back to 16th-century Venetian cooks. Pair it with a glass of Verdea, the dry white from Zakynthos — slightly oxidised and full-bodied, it holds its own against the bold flavours of the sauce.

Mantolato, a hard nougat made with roasted almonds and thyme honey, is Corfu's traditional confection — old-town delicatessens have stocked it since the 19th century. It's the ideal edible souvenir to bring home: it travels well and keeps for several weeks.

visits.text: Gaios, the capital of Paxos, stretches along a natural channel sheltered by two islets: Agios Nikolaos, which bears the ruins of a Venetian fortress built between 1424 and 1510, and Panagia, home to a small monastery. The pastel-coloured waterfront with its Venetian-influenced buildings is one of the most charming in the Ionian Islands. According to Greek mythology, Poseidon struck off the southern tip of Corfu with his trident to create Paxos as a love retreat for himself and Amphitrite. Standing here, you can believe it.

The blue caves along Paxos' west coast are accessible only by sea — no road, no stairway. Local boats depart from Gaios or Lakka to cruise along the limestone cliffs, where sunlight filtered through the water casts blue and green reflections inside the cavities. Set aside half a day for the excursion and book ahead in July–August.

Lakka, at the northern tip of the island, nestles at the head of a horseshoe-shaped bay with perfectly still waters — sailing boats moor here by the dozen in summer. The village is tiny (a handful of tavernas, flower-lined lanes) but the waterfront atmosphere at sunset is hard to beat. If you have access to a small boat, rent one and explore the pebble beaches along the north coast, unreachable any other way.

Loggos is the most discreet of Paxos' three villages — a fishing port of a few dozen houses, with tavernas right on the water. It's the best address for a lunch of grilled fish in an authentic setting, well away from the organised excursions that flood Lakka in high season.

The Tripitos Arch, on the west coast of Paxos, is a natural limestone arch several metres high jutting out above the sea. It's reachable on foot from the village of Magazia (about 20 minutes). Few tourists make this detour — it's one of the most dramatic landscapes on the island.

activities.text: Book a boat trip to Antipaxos as soon as you arrive — the little sister island sits just 15 minutes to the south. Its beaches of Vrika and Voutoumi regularly rank among the finest in the Mediterranean — fine white sand and turquoise water that could pass for the Caribbean. The excursion typically takes a full day and includes a stop at the blue caves.

Set off on the footpaths that wind through the centuries-old olive groves in the island's interior. The Republic of Venice actively promoted olive cultivation on Paxos from the 15th century to secure its oil supply — the gnarled trees you'll see are often several hundred years old. The coastal trail from Loggos to Lakka (roughly 2 hours) offers magnificent views.

Paxos' bourdeto is a local twist on the Corfu classic: fish (scorpionfish or gurnard) simmered in a spicy tomato sauce, with a more pronounced kick than the Corfu version. Enjoy it at a harbour-side taverna in Gaios with a glass of tsipouro, the anise-scented grape marc spirit of the Ionians, served ice-cold.

Amygdalota from Paxos are small, soft almond-and-icing-sugar biscuits traditionally baked in the island's monasteries for religious feast days. You'll find them in the artisanal grocery shops of Gaios — pick up a box for the ferry ride the next day.

visits.text: The castle of Agia Mavra (Santa Maura), standing at the edge of the channel that separates the island from the mainland, is the first thing you'll see as you cross the bridge. Built in the 14th century by the Orsini counts, it gave its medieval name to the entire island — Lefkada was known as Santa Maura until the 19th century. The Turks held it from 1479 to 1684, before the Venetians took it back. The grounds are freely accessible and the view over the lagoon and salt flats is beautiful in the late afternoon.

Cape Lefkatas, at the island's south-western tip, is one of the most myth-laden sites in all of Greece. The white cliff drops 72 metres straight into the Ionian Sea. In antiquity, tradition held that those suffering from unrequited love would leap from this promontory into the sea — surviving the jump meant being cured of their passion. The poet Sappho (c. 630–580 BC) is said to have died here for the love of Phaon, according to legend. A lighthouse built in 1913 now stands on the site of the ancient temple of Apollo. On a clear day, you can see the coast of Epirus.

Nydri, on the east coast, is the launching pad for boat trips to the small islands of the archipelago: Meganisi, Madouri, Sparti and, most famously, Skorpios — formerly owned by Aristotle Onassis, where he married Jackie Kennedy in 1968. Water taxis and organised excursions leave from the harbour throughout the day in summer. The marina is lively, with plenty of waterfront tavernas.

Faneromeni Monastery, perched on a wooded hill 3 km north of the capital, offers the finest bird's-eye view of Lefkada town and the channel separating it from the mainland. The present buildings date from the 17th–18th centuries. Allow 20 minutes on foot from the car park.

The town of Lefkada itself is worth an hour's wander through its lanes: the facades are built of painted corrugated iron on wooden frames — an earthquake-resistant technique honed over centuries of seismic experience, which gives the houses a Caribbean feel unique in the Ionians. The archaeological museum, in town, displays finds from the excavations of Dörpfeld, the German archaeologist who believed he had found the homeland of Odysseus here.

activities.text: Rent a boat from Nydri and explore the islets off the east coast at your own pace. Skorpios remains private property, but you can anchor nearby; Meganisi has several superb coves accessible only by sea. The east coast of Lefkada is sheltered from the prevailing winds — ideal for a relaxed day on the water, even without sailing experience.

Eglouvi lentils are Lefkada's most prized agricultural product — this small mountain village at 700 metres altitude grows tiny, flavourful lentils that carry a Protected Designation of Origin (PDO). In soup with local olive oil or as a cold salad, they're simple and outstanding. Look for them in the grocery shops of the capital.

Ladopita is a rustic Lefkadian cake made with olive oil, flour and raisins — no butter, no milk. It's a Lenten recipe handed down from the Venetian centuries. Dense and mildly sweet, it pairs perfectly with a Greek coffee on a terrace. You'll find it in the traditional bakeries of the old town.

Agios Nikitas, 12 km northwest of the capital, is the most charming seaside village on the coast: its pedestrian lanes lead straight down to a small sandy beach, the tavernas sit at the water's edge and the crowds are more manageable than in Nydri. It's a good option for dinner or a late-afternoon swim.

visits.text: Porto Katsiki ('Goat Port') is one of the most photographed beaches in the Mediterranean. A concave limestone cliff drops sheer onto a strip of white pebbles and sand, lapped by water of an almost unreal blue-turquoise hue — locals attribute the colour to the geological composition of the seabed. From the car park at the top of the cliff, roughly a hundred steps cut into the rock lead down to the beach. Arrive before 10am in July–August: after that, the beach fills quickly and the staircase becomes a queue.

Egremni, a few kilometres north of Porto Katsiki, stretches for nearly 2.5 km of golden pebbles at the foot of 150-metre cliffs. The earthquake of 17 November 2015 destroyed the old stairway of 347 steps and buried part of the beach under a landslide. Since 2022, a new stairway has been rebuilt, but the beach is still mainly reached by boat from Vassiliki — which keeps it wilder and less crowded than Porto Katsiki. The waves can be powerful: exercise caution with children.

Vassiliki is a world-class windsurfing destination — the reliable afternoon thermals that blow across the bay from the open sea draw competitors and enthusiasts from around the globe. In the morning, the bay is a mirror-flat playground for beginners; from 2pm, the wind picks up and sails multiply. Dozens of windsurf and kitesurf schools cater to all levels.

Karya, a mountain village in the centre of the island at 550 metres altitude, is famous throughout Greece for its handmade embroidery — the village women carry on a tradition dating back to the 19th century, producing intricate floral patterns. The local folklore museum tells the story. In summer, an artisan market livens up the central square. The view from the heights takes in both coasts of the island.

Kathisma beach, halfway between the capital and Porto Katsiki on the west coast, is the largest organised beach on the island: golden sand, sunbeds, beach bars and occasionally paragliding sessions from the neighbouring hill. If Porto Katsiki feels too chaotic, Kathisma offers the same turquoise water with a little more comfort.

activities.text: Take a windsurfing lesson in Vassiliki — it's one of Europe's most renowned spots to learn or improve. Conditions are close to ideal: calm mornings for the basics, steady thermal winds in the afternoon for practice. Book your slot the day before — schools fill up fast in high season.

Head out early by boat from Vassiliki to Egremni and Porto Katsiki — the perspective from the sea on those white cliffs plunging straight down is completely different from the view at the top. Water taxis and small excursion boats leave from the harbour from 9am.

Savoro is a traditional preservation dish of Corfiot and Lefkadian fishermen: fish (sardines, mackerel) is fried, then marinated in vinegar, raisins and rosemary. The result is a surprisingly tangy-sweet preparation served cold — perfect for a light lunch after the beach.

Pasteli, a sesame-and-honey bar, is the traditional beach snack of the Ionian Islands — people have been eating it since ancient Greece. Simple, energising and utterly unprocessed. Pick up a few from a grocery shop in Vassiliki before heading down to Porto Katsiki: you'll need the fuel for the climb back up.

seo.h1_title: Ionian Islands Road Trip: Corfu, Paxos & Lefkada
seo.intro_paragraph: Corfu unfolds four centuries of Venetian architecture through its labyrinthine UNESCO-listed lanes, before the ferries carry you to Paxos — a 25 km² island blanketed in olive groves and carved into coves accessible only by sea. Lefkada rounds off the journey with its 72-metre white cliffs plunging into impossibly blue water, and Vassiliki where windsurfing sails fill the bay every afternoon.
seo.seo_hook: UNESCO-listed Venetian lanes, sea caves on Paxos accessible only by boat, Lefkada's white cliffs and their otherworldly water. An island-hopping circuit of a different kind of beauty.
featured_list: Corfu: UNESCO-listed Venetian old town since 2007
Paxos: turquoise coves and blue caves reachable only by boat
Lefkada: limestone cliffs and the legend of Sappho
Vassiliki: world-renowned windsurfing spot

places_taglines.GR::corfou: Where Venetian kantounia, British cricket and Ottoman-defying fortresses share one spellbinding UNESCO old town
places_taglines.GR::paxos: A pocket-sized island of twisted olive groves and turquoise coves that no road can reach
places_taglines.GR::leucade: Where Sappho leapt and white cliffs drop 72 metres into impossible shades of blue
places_taglines.GR::vassiliki: Morning glass, afternoon thermals: the bay where Europe comes to windsurf beneath Lefkada's cliffs

highlights: Corfu's UNESCO-listed old town with its Venetian fortresses and labyrinthine lanes
Blue caves of Paxos and Antipaxos beaches accessible only by boat
Porto Katsiki and Egremni cliffs, ranked among the most beautiful beaches in the Mediterranean
Vassiliki, a European benchmark for windsurfing and kitesurfing

itineraries.title: Kefalonia & Zakynthos: Caves, Myths and Turquoise Shores
itineraries.subtitle: From the underground lakes of Kefalonia to the towering cliffs of Zakynthos, with a stop on Odysseus's legendary island — five days across the wildest Ionians.
specialties: Ladotyri cheese aged in olive oil (Zakynthos PDO)

visits.text: Argostoli has been the capital of Kefalonia since 1757 — before that, the population sheltered inside the Venetian fortress of San Giorgio as protection against pirates. The town thrived as one of the busiest ports in the Greek islands, until the earthquake of 12 August 1953 levelled it entirely. What you see today is therefore a rebuilt city, but it has held on to an authentic Mediterranean character with its palm-lined waterfront and the lively Vallianos Square, which comes alive every evening.

The De Bosset Bridge — also called the Drapano Bridge — is the longest stone bridge over the sea in the world, stretching 700 metres. Originally built in wood in 1813 by Swiss engineer Charles-Philippe de Bosset under British command, it was entirely reconstructed in stone a few decades later. Roughly halfway across, you'll come upon an obelisk erected by the British to commemorate their presence — the original plaque reading 'To the glory of the British Empire' was destroyed in 1865, one year after the island was united with Greece. The bridge marks the edge of the Koutavos Lagoon, where you can spot Caretta caretta turtles swimming freely.

The Archaeological Museum of Argostoli, opened in 1960 (architect Patroklos Karantinos), houses finds from across the island spanning prehistory to the Roman era. The Mycenaean collection is especially impressive — highlights include geometric ceramics, gold jewellery and bronzes unearthed during excavations at the island's four ancient cities: Same, Kranioi, Pronnoi and Paleis. Allow about an hour if you want to see it properly.

The Korgialenios Historical and Folklore Museum, founded in 1963 on the ground floor of the library bearing the same name, traces the history of Kefalonia from the Venetian period through to the 1953 earthquake. You'll find antique furniture, silverware, household tools, traditional costumes and close to 3,000 photographs — a tangible way to understand what the town looked like before it was completely destroyed. The entrance is through the library, which also holds 50,000 rare books and manuscripts.

The Lighthouse of Saints Theodore — locally known as Fanari ('the lighthouse') — is a neoclassical circular structure supported by 20 Doric columns, built in 1829 during the British occupation to plans drawn by J.P. Kennedy. Its lantern sits 11 metres above the Bay of Argostoli. Destroyed in the 1953 earthquake, it was faithfully rebuilt by 1960. Come here in the late afternoon: the sun sinking behind the Paliki peninsula on the far side of the bay paints the sea orange — one of the finest sunsets on the island.

The Katavothres are one of the strangest geological phenomena in the Mediterranean: seawater rushes into cracks in the limestone, vanishes underground, crosses the entire island — roughly 15 km — and resurfaces in the Bay of Sami, on the eastern coast, fourteen days later. In 1963, Austrian hydrogeologists Maurin and Zoetl poured 160 kg of fluorescent green dye (uranine) into the sinkholes — and detected it again in the underground lake at Melissani and the springs at Karavomilos. The process remains partly unexplained. As far back as 1835, a British tax collector had the idea of harnessing the current to power corn mills — the ruins of those mills are still visible on site.

activities.text: Lithostroto, the main pedestrian street of Argostoli, is where locals gather every evening for the ritual stroll. Shops, cafés, the Catholic church, the clock tower rebuilt in 1985 — everything is packed into a few hundred metres. Keep walking to Vallianos Square, the large central plaza, for a Greek coffee under the palm trees. Quiet during the day, buzzing and full of life at night.

The Koutavos Lagoon, once a mosquito-infested swamp and a hotbed of malaria, is now a bird sanctuary where migratory species nest. Caretta caretta turtles feed here regularly. You can walk the coastal path or rent an electric boat to get a closer look at the wildlife. Morning is the best time — calm water, soft light, and usually a few fishermen by the harbour.

Kreatopita is the quintessential Kefallonian pie: flaky pastry stuffed with a mixture of meats (lamb, pork, beef), rice and spices, baked until golden. It's a festive dish that families still prepare for special occasions. Track it down in the bakeries and tavernas around the market — it's the best quick lunch in town.

Robola of Kefalonia is one of Greece's most acclaimed white wines. This ancient grape variety grows on the Omala plateau at 700 metres elevation and carries a PDO label. Dry, mineral, taut, with crisp acidity — nothing like the soft, rounded whites from the rest of Greece. Ask for it at any taverna on the waterfront: it pairs perfectly with grilled fish and mezze.

visits.text: Melissani Cave — also known as the 'Cave of the Nymphs' — is a limestone cenote whose ceiling collapsed during the 1953 earthquake, allowing sunlight to pour directly onto the underground lake. The lake stretches roughly 160 metres long and reaches depths of 30 to 40 metres; its surface sits one metre above sea level, fed by water that has travelled 15 km underground from the Katavothres in Argostoli. Archaeological excavations in 1962 uncovered a sanctuary dedicated to the god Pan on a small central islet, along with clay figurines and oil lamps dating from the 3rd–4th centuries BC. Plan your visit between 11 am and 1 pm: that's when the sun strikes the opening directly and transforms the water into a kaleidoscope of blues and violets. The tour is by rowboat and lasts 10 to 15 minutes.

Drogarati Cave, 5 km from Sami, was discovered around 300 years ago when an earthquake caused its entrance to collapse inward. It measures 65 metres long, 45 metres wide and 20 metres high, with a steady temperature of 18°C — a welcome relief in summer. The stalactites and stalagmites, some of which resemble dolphins according to speleologists, have formed over roughly 100 million years. The main chamber, known as the 'Hall of Apotheosis,' has such remarkable acoustics that it has hosted concerts — including, in 2014, a performance by the Bavarian Philharmonic who sailed in on a cruise ship docked at Argostoli. Bring a jumper even in summer, and wear sturdy shoes for the slippery steps.

Sami's harbour is Kefalonia's main ferry port, with connections to Patras and Ithaca. The town itself is modest but pleasant, with a handful of tavernas and cafés along the waterfront. Just next door lies Karavomylos, the village where the resurgent springs from the Katavothres emerge — the seawater that entered the ground in Argostoli reappears here, slightly brackish, after its 14-day underground journey.

Antisamos Beach, 4 km from Sami, is one of the most beautiful on Kefalonia: a long sweep of white pebbles framed by pine forest that tumbles down to the shore, with deep turquoise water. Several scenes from the 2001 film 'Captain Corelli's Mandolin' were shot right here. The beach is lifeguarded in summer but never overcrowded — the winding access road from the main highway puts off the lazier crowd. Bring your snorkelling mask: the seabed is rich.

activities.text: A combined visit to both caves (Melissani + Drogarati) works best as a half-day outing. Start early in the morning with Drogarati to beat the cruise-ship coaches, then head to Melissani around 11:30 am to catch the peak sunlight. Allow about an hour at each cave, including queuing time in high season.

After the caves, head to Antisamos for a swim. The water is crystal-clear and far less crowded than the beaches in the south. If you have a mask, dip your head below the surface and look around — the visibility is exceptional.

Riganada is a rustic dried bread rubbed with fresh tomato, drizzled with olive oil, topped with feta and scattered with dried oregano — a summer classic across the Ionian Islands. Dead simple, dead good. Order one with a chilled glass of Robola at a taverna on the Sami waterfront at the end of the day.

map_keywords: Cave of the Nymphs Vathy Ithaca

visits.text: Vathy, the capital of Ithaca, is a small town of 1,700 people tucked at the end of a horseshoe-shaped gulf — one of the best natural anchorages in the Ionian Sea. Most of the Venetian-style buildings were reconstructed faithfully after the 1953 earthquake; it is said that one of El Greco's earliest works hangs in the local cathedral. Stroll along the harbour, stop at one of the quayside tavernas — the atmosphere is gentle, worlds away from mass tourism.

The Archaeological Museum of Vathy holds over 1,000 objects unearthed on the island, covering the Mycenaean through Roman periods. The star exhibit is a pottery fragment inscribed with the name 'Odysseus,' found during excavations at the Alalkomenes site. There is also a small bronze statuette that some scholars identify as a portrait of Odysseus himself, along with Mycenaean votive tripods. Admission is free. The museum is closed on Tuesdays.

Aetos Hill, 4 km from Vathy, rises to 380 metres. From the church of Ai Giorgios, a steep trail climbs for 20 minutes to the ruins of the ancient city of Alalkomenes — this is where Heinrich Schliemann dug in 1868 and 1878, convinced he had found the palace of Odysseus. Modern archaeologists are more cautious: the fortifications date from the 7th century BC, well after the events described in the Odyssey. But the view from the top — spanning both coasts of the island and Kefalonia across the water — is worth the effort on its own.

Kathara Monastery sits perched at roughly 600 metres elevation on the mountain that Homer called Neriton. Founded in the late 17th century, it offers a full 360° panorama over Vathy, the southern half of the island and Kefalonia beyond. Even if the monastery itself doesn't interest you, make the drive for the view — it ranks among the most beautiful in all the Ionian Islands.

The Cave of the Nymphs, locally known as Marmarospilia, lies a few kilometres north of Vathy. According to the Odyssey, this is where Odysseus hid the gifts from the Phaeacians upon his return home. The cave contains stalactites and a small ancient altar, with a natural opening in the ceiling — the smoke from sacrificial offerings supposedly rose through it. It is not always open to visitors due to ongoing excavations — check before making the trip.

activities.text: Ithaca is tailor-made for gentle hiking. Signposted trails connect the villages through olive groves and scrubland of kermes oak. The island is home to just 3,000 people, and the paths are often deserted — one of the most authentic experiences in the Ionians. Ask in Vathy for details on the marked routes.

Revithokeftedes are chickpea fritters laced with fresh herbs, typical of the Ionian Islands. In Ithaca, they're usually served with yoghurt or skordalia (garlic mash). A simple, delicious vegetarian starter — one of the best things to order in a harbourside taverna in Vathy.

The village of Stavros, in northern Ithaca, deserves a stop: the central square features a bust of Odysseus and a model of his 'idealised' palace, and a small archaeological museum displays finds from excavations led by the British School of Archaeology in the 1930s. Not to be confused with the museum in Vathy — this one focuses on the northern part of the island and its ties to the Odyssey.

visits.text: Zakynthos Town — dubbed 'Il Fiore di Levante' (the Flower of the East) by the Venetians — was completely destroyed in the 1953 earthquake and rebuilt in a neo-Venetian style with arcaded streets and colonnaded façades. Solomos Square, the largest in town, opens directly onto the Ionian Sea. Here you'll find the Byzantine Museum, a statue of the poet Dionysios Solomos (who penned the Greek national anthem in 1823), and the town hall. This is where the action is in the morning; in the evening, head up to Bohali.

The Church of Agios Dionysios is dedicated to the island's patron saint. Founded in 1708, it houses his relics in a silver reliquary and features a gilded carved iconostasis along with fine Byzantine frescoes restored after 1953. The bell tower overlooking the harbour is one of the defining landmarks of Zakynthos. Every year on 24 August and 17 December, thousands of worshippers gather here for the saint's feast days.

The Byzantine Museum of Zakynthos, on Solomos Square, is one of the richest in the Ionians. It brings together carved wooden Byzantine icons, original frescoes from the 15th to 17th centuries rescued from churches destroyed in 1953, and 18th-century paintings by artists of the Ionian School such as Doxaras, Koutouzis and Kandounis. Some canvases depict the town before the earthquake — a striking contrast with what it looks like today. Allow about 1 hour 30 minutes.

The Venetian Castle of Bohali, set on the hillside above the town, was built in the 15th century by the Venetians on even older foundations. The pine-shaded enclosure overlooks the harbour from 150 metres — from the crumbling ramparts you can take in the entire bay, the port and the mountains of the interior in a single sweep. Drive up or walk (25 minutes from Solomos Square). At sunset, locals from the village of Bohali settle on café terraces with a glass in hand — join them.

The National Marine Park of Zakynthos (NMPZ) was established to protect the Caretta caretta sea turtles. It encompasses Laganas Bay and the nesting beaches on the island's south coast. Turtles lay their eggs between June and August on these shores — access is regulated, but glass-bottom boat excursions let you observe them in their natural habitat without disturbing them. If you're visiting in summer, don't miss a trip out on the water.

activities.text: Roma Street, the main pedestrian thoroughfare leading off Saint Mark's Square, is lined with local shops where you can pick up mandolato nougat (almonds, honey, egg whites — the island's signature sweet), ladotyri (a hard cheese aged in olive oil, Zakynthos PDO) and olive-oil products. It's also the place for evening outings — the tavernas kick into gear around 8 pm.

Facing Saint Mark's Square stands the Solomos and Kalvos Museum, housed in the island's former casino and open since 1968. On the ground floor lie the tombs of the two poets — Dionysios Solomos, author of the national anthem, and Andreas Kalvos. Upstairs: manuscripts, personal belongings and period documents. A must for anyone interested in Greek literature.

Fritoura is the local evening tradition: small fish — mackerel, sardines, anchovies — fried in olive oil and served with a squeeze of lemon. Simple, no-nonsense, excellent. The Zakynthos waterfront is lined with tavernas serving fritoura as a starter alongside Verdea — the local white wine, dry and lively, made from grape varieties found nowhere else.

Verdea is the PDO white wine of Zakynthos, made from indigenous varieties (Pavlos, Goustolidi) grown nowhere else in Greece. Dry, with bright acidity and a faint bitterness on the finish — a world apart from the soft whites produced across most of the islands. Order it at the taverna with the fritoura or local cheeses.

visits.text: Navagio Bay — also known as 'Smuggler's Cove' — is the most photographed spot in all of Greece. It owes its name to the wreck of the cargo ship Panagiotis, which ran aground here on the night of 2 October 1980 after being chased by the Greek navy during a storm. The vessel had left Argostoli loaded with contraband goods. White limestone cliffs rise over 200 metres above the beach of white sand and turquoise water. Note: since 2022, landing on the beach has been banned due to rockfall hazards — but you can approach by boat and swim in the bay.
