For the view from above, take the road from Anafonitria or Volimes to the free clifftop viewing platform.

The Blue Caves are a series of arches and tunnels carved by the sea into the white limestone cliffs on the island's north coast, between the port of Agios Nikolaos and Cape Skinari. Their name comes from the electric blue colour the water takes on inside, the result of sunlight reflecting off the submerged limestone — a phenomenon strikingly similar to the Blue Grotto on Capri. The rock walls and even your body underwater glow vivid blue. They are only accessible by small boat (50 passengers maximum to enter the arches). Depart from the port of Agios Nikolaos or from Cape Skinari.

Cape Skinari is the northernmost tip of Zakynthos, facing Kefalonia — on a clear day, Mount Ainos on the neighbouring island is perfectly visible. The lighthouse dates from 1897 and stands 9.5 metres tall. This is the departure point for boat excursions to the Blue Caves and Navagio Bay. The traditional windmills on the cape have become one of the island's most recognisable symbols.

Anafonitria Monastery, set inland in the northwest, dates from the 15th century. It is one of the oldest on the island and is famous for having sheltered Saint Dionysios as a monk before he became the patron saint of Zakynthos. It is surrounded by centuries-old olive groves and a medieval defence tower that still stands. The atmosphere here is peaceful, far removed from the beaches.

Xigia Beach is a natural curiosity: sulphur springs rise directly into the sea from the rocks, giving the water a milky tint and a faint but distinctive smell. The therapeutic properties of these sulphurous waters have been known locally for a long time. The beach is small, wild and rarely crowded — a fine alternative to Navagio for anyone looking for a different kind of swim.

activities.text: The ideal plan for this last day: leave early in the morning for Cape Skinari and board a small boat to the Blue Caves (allow 2 hours). Then head inland to Volimes and Anafonitria, winding through olive groves and mountain villages. In the afternoon, make the detour to the Navagio viewpoint for the photo, then finish at Xigia Beach for a dip in the sulphurous waters before driving back to Zakynthos Town.

The village of Volimes, up in the hills, is known for its textile artisans — rugs, embroidery and lace are displayed in the small shops lining the main street. It's also where you'll find the best local produce: mountain honey, olive oil and handmade mandolato nougat. The vendors are often the producers themselves.

The combined boat trip covering the Blue Caves and Navagio Bay (seen from the sea) takes 3 to 4 hours depending on the option chosen. Boats depart from the port of Agios Nikolaos (10 minutes from Navagio) or from the main port of Zakynthos Town (90 minutes). Book the evening before in summer — spots go fast. Small boats carrying 12 to 50 passengers can enter the caves; the larger vessels can only circle them from outside.

practical_info.depart: Port of Agios Nikolaos or Zakynthos Town port

activities.text: Ladotyri is the PDO cheese of Zakynthos: a hard cheese made from sheep's and goat's milk, aged in olive oil — hence the name (lado = oil, tyri = cheese). It has a firm texture, a dark rind and a powerful, slightly fruity flavour. Eat it at the end of a meal with local honey, or diced as an appetiser.

seo.h1_title: Kefalonia & Zakynthos: Caves, Cliffs and Turquoise Shores
seo.intro_paragraph: Step inside Melissani Cave on Kefalonia, where an underground lake glows an unearthly blue beneath a skylight opened by an earthquake, then cross to Ithaca and the echoes of the Odyssey. Continue to Zakynthos to watch Caretta caretta turtles and stand above the dizzying cliffs of Navagio. Five days woven through mythology, geology and unspoiled coastline.
seo.seo_hook: Underground lakes lit by shafts of blue light, shores steeped in the Odyssey, protected sea turtles and a smuggler's shipwreck — the southern Ionians pack all of this into five days.
featured_list: Melissani: underground lake and midday light show
Ithaca: the island of Odysseus and its wild trails
Zakynthos: a paradise for Caretta caretta turtles
Navagio: a shipwreck beneath 200-metre cliffs

places_taglines.GR::argostoli: A capital reborn after 1953, where a 700-metre stone bridge spans a lagoon full of sea turtles
places_taglines.GR::sami: Ferry port and launchpad for the two most spectacular caves on Kefalonia
places_taglines.GR::ithaque: A 96 km² island forever tied to Odysseus's homecoming in Homer's epic
places_taglines.GR::zakynthos: Venetian grace, nesting sea turtles, the Navagio shipwreck and glowing Blue Caves

highlights: Melissani underground lake: visit between 11 am and 1 pm for the light show
Drogarati Cave: outstanding acoustics, steady temperature of 18°C all year round
Navagio: view from the clifftop platform or boat trip from Agios Nikolaos
Caretta caretta turtles: glass-bottom boat observation in the Zakynthos Marine Park

seo.h1_title: Greek Macedonia: Mount Olympus, Ancient Capitals & Byzantine Treasures
seo.intro_paragraph: Let Thessaloniki unfurl its Byzantine and Ottoman soul along the waterfront, then reach Vergina where royal treasures glitter beneath the earth. Raise your eyes toward Mount Olympus and its myths, then follow the Edessa falls into emerald freshness. Macedonia blends imperial power and vibrant nature.
seo.seo_hook: Thessaloniki, crossroads vibrant with history, royal treasures buried in stone, misty peaks touching the divine. Northern Greece, where Olympus reigns.
featured_list: Thessaloniki: harbor cosmopolitan cultural hub
Mount Olympus: cloud-piercing divine threshold
Vergina: royal tomb golden splendor
Edessa: waterfall emerald natural theater

itineraries.title: Thrace Road Trip: Kavala, Thasos & Philippi
itineraries.subtitle: From the Ottoman fortress of Kavala to the megalithic dragon houses of Thasos, through Europe's first Christian site and the colourful alleyways of Xanthi.
itineraries.notes: Thasos is only accessible by ferry from Keramoti (35 min) or Kavala (75 min). Visiting the Evros Delta requires a military permit that must be requested in advance.

visits.text: The fortress of Kavala commands the entire peninsula from the summit of Panagia hill. Its current form dates from the early 15th century, though it rests on Byzantine foundations subsequently reworked by the Venetians and the Ottomans. Climb up to the outer walls for the view: at your feet, the red-tiled roofs of the old town, the turquoise harbour, and in the distance the silhouette of Thasos on the horizon. The open-air theatre nestled within the walls hosts performances in summer — check the programme if you're here in July or August.

The Kamares Aqueduct is the most photographed monument in the city, and it deserves every shot. This double-tiered structure — 11 large arches below, narrower arches above — stretches 270 metres long and stands 25 metres high. It was built between 1520 and 1536 on the orders of Ibrahim Pasha, Grand Vizier to Suleiman the Magnificent, after the city was destroyed in 1391. Made of local granite with Ottoman brick detailing, it supplied Kavala with water from Mount Pangaion until 1911. The best angle for a photo: from Lahana Street in the late afternoon, when the golden light brings out the stone.

In the old quarter of Panagia, look for the house of Mehmet Ali (1769–1849) — the founder of the last dynasty to rule Egypt, born right here in this modest cantilevered stone building. The house is preserved as a museum and gives a good sense of Ottoman domestic architecture of the era. Across from it, the square bearing his name features an equestrian statue of the pasha — one of the rare depictions of a human figure tolerated within Islamic tradition.

The Panagia quarter, perched on the peninsula between the fortress and the sea, is the historic heart of Kavala. Its cobbled alleyways, colourful houses with cantilevered balconies (sachnisia), and hidden courtyards make it one of the best-preserved Ottoman neighbourhoods in northern Greece. Don't miss the Halil Bey Mosque, built on the foundations of an early Christian basilica — during the population exchange of 1922–1923, it served as temporary shelter for Greek refugee families.

The Imaret, on the western flank of the Panagia peninsula, is one of the largest Ottoman-era Islamic complexes still standing in Europe. Built in the early 19th century by Mehmet Ali to serve as a soup kitchen, madrasa and mosque, it blends Ottoman arches with slender columns around a remarkable inner courtyard. The interior has been privatised, but the exterior and gardens can be explored freely from the lane.

activities.text: Stroll along the Kavala waterfront down to the fishing harbour: the blue-and-white boats, nets drying on the quay, and fish tavernas lined up along the water give the city the feel of an authentic Greek port, well away from the mass-tourism circuit. This is where Saint Paul landed on his first journey to Europe — a commemorative plaque near the harbour marks the event.

Sample sardeles pastes: salted, dried and pressed sardines are Kavala's signature culinary speciality, going back centuries. They're eaten on toasted bread, simply drizzled with lemon juice, alongside a glass of Macedonian tsipouro without anise — the local spirit served chilled with olives and feta. The tavernas along the harbour serve both without ceremony.

In the evening, order a saganaki: a slab of graviera cheese, breaded and pan-fried in olive oil, served still crackling with a wedge of lemon. Simple, no-nonsense, and far better than it sounds — it's the go-to appetiser across the entire region.

visits.text: The site of Philippi has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2016 — the 18th Greek site to join the list. Founded in 360 BC by colonists from Thasos under the name Krenides, the city was conquered in 356 BC by Philip II of Macedon, father of Alexander the Great, who gave it his name. Allow at least two hours to cross the entire site — it covers 67 hectares, slightly larger than Pompeii.

The theatre of Philippi was built by Philip II in the mid-4th century BC and remodelled under the Romans in the 2nd century AD into a theatre with a monumental three-storey stage building. In the 3rd century it was converted into an arena for gladiatorial combat: the wild animal pit beneath the orchestra is still visible. Since 1957 it has hosted the Philippi Festival every summer — one of the oldest ancient-theatre festivals in Greece.

The Roman forum of Philippi is one of the best-documented in the eastern provinces of the Empire: a vast square surrounded by temples, shops, the orators' tribune, and a meeting hall for the decurions. A monumental inscription at least 20 metres long, with metal letters set into the pavement at a height of 62 cm, honoured the flamen of the divine Augustus. Look for the ruts left by chariots on the Via Egnatia — they're still legible in the stone.

The early Christian basilicas of Philippi are among the most impressive in Greece. Basilica A (late 5th century) measured 130 by 50 metres, with a transept, galleries and atrium. The Octagon, an episcopal complex built on the presumed site of Saint Paul's house of prayer, went through three construction phases from the 4th to the 6th century. It was here, in AD 49–50, that the Apostle Paul founded Europe's first Christian community — and was briefly imprisoned in a Roman cistern traditionally identified as the 'Prison of Paul'.

The Archaeological Museum of Philippi, spread over two floors, presents the site's collections chronologically: sculptures, vases, coins and gold jewellery from prehistoric through Roman times on the ground floor, Christian heritage and the city's decline upstairs. Allow 45 minutes to an hour — the Macedonian jewellery and coins from the era of Philip II are particularly worth your time.

activities.text: Side trip to the Baptistery of Saint Lydia, a few hundred metres from the archaeological site, on the bank of the Zygaktis River. Lydia, a wealthy purple-cloth merchant from Thyatira, was the first European baptised by Paul in AD 49–50 in this river. The modern octagonal baptistery dates from 1974, but an open-air baptism ceremony takes place every 20 May — if you happen to be here on that date, you can attend.

Try kavourmas: pork or veal slow-cooked in its own fat with spices — a traditional Macedonian winter preservation method you'll find in tavernas around the Kavala area, served as a hot or cold meze. Pair it with an assyrtiko from Drama: this mineral white wine, produced in the highland vineyards to the west, is dry, bright and sharply acidic.

The evening drive back to Kavala follows the Philippi plain, dominated by Mount Pangaion to the south-west — it was from its gold and silver mines that Philip II bankrolled his conquests. If you have time, stop at Krinides, the small modern village on the site of ancient Krenides, to see the remains of the Hellenistic city walls on the outskirts of the UNESCO site.

visits.text: To reach Thasos, take the ferry from Keramoti, 35 km south-east of Kavala — the crossing takes just 35 minutes and departures run every 30 minutes in season. It's by far the quickest and cheapest option. Once in Limenas, the capital, head down to the ancient agora: it sits right in the heart of the modern town, surrounded by the ruins of sanctuaries dedicated to Heracles, Dionysus, Artemis and Poseidon, with a medieval tower occupying one corner. Information panels at the entrance show a reconstruction of what the square once looked like — helpful for grasping the scale.

The ancient theatre of Thasos, built into a natural hollow in the hillside north-east of town, dates in its earliest phases from the late 4th century BC. In the 1st century AD it was converted into an arena for gladiatorial combat. It has been fully restored in recent years using white marble from Thasos — the same stone used in antiquity, quarried on the island. The view from the upper tiers over the harbour of Limenas and the Aegean is one of the finest on the island.

The Archaeological Museum of Thasos, founded in 1935 and renovated between 1989 and 2000, is the oldest museum in the Eastern Macedonia and Thrace region. Its centrepiece is the Kouros of Thasos: an archaic statue standing 3.5 metres tall, depicting a young man carrying a ram — a masterpiece of the 6th century BC discovered in 1911 in five pieces inside a Byzantine wall, where it had been reused as building material. The museum also holds a fine head of Alexander the Great and an Aphrodite with a Dolphin regularly lent to major international exhibitions.

The acropolis of Thasos dominates the town from its rocky spur. Climb up from the theatre along the signposted path: you'll pass the sanctuaries of Apollo and Athena, then the Sanctuary of Pan carved directly into the rock face. Along the way, look for the Cyclopean marble ramparts — some blocks are fitted so precisely that you still can't slide a credit card between them. The view from the top sweeps across the entire northern coastline of the island.

activities.text: Swim at Makryammos, the closest beach to Limenas, just 2 km to the east: a crescent-shaped cove with fine sand, emerald-green water and maritime pines reaching right down to the sea. It's the postcard image of Thasos — the blend of dense vegetation and crystal-clear water that earned the island its nickname of 'the Emerald Isle'.

In the evening, settle into a café on the harbour at Limenas and order a local retsina — the pine-resinated white wine produced on the island, slightly bitter, best drunk chilled with olives and goat's cheese. It's here that you begin to understand why ancient Greece regulated wine production by law: Thasian amphorae stamped with quality-control seals have been found across the entire Mediterranean basin.

visits.text: The village of Panagia, perched in the mountains 10 km south of Limenas, is one of the most beautiful on the island: stone fountains, centuries-old plane trees, tile-roofed houses and cobbled lanes winding up the hillside. It's the birthplace of the local dance tradition and one of the best-preserved villages in Eastern Macedonia. Arrive on a Sunday morning to see the locals in traditional dress after church.

The village of Theologos, 25 km south-west of Limenas, was the island's medieval capital during Genoese and Turkish rule. It was where the population retreated to escape pirates. Its narrow lanes, stone houses with walled gardens and old fountains set it apart from the modernised coastal villages. Allow 45 minutes for a full circuit.

The marble quarries of Alyki, on the cape of the same name 32 km south-east of Limenas, are an exceptional archaeological site: the extraction of Thasian white marble — one of the most prized in antiquity — is documented here from the Archaic period through to Byzantine times. Half-cut blocks have been waiting in the rock for twenty centuries. This white marble with its glittering crystals was used, among other things, to build the Arch of Galerius in Thessaloniki. From the beach at Alyki, submerged blocks are visible beneath the water.

activities.text: Drive around the island: the coastal road covers 95 km and can be comfortably done in a day with stops. Don't miss Golden Beach (Chrisi Amoudia) on the east coast — a long stretch of golden sand lapped by deep-blue water, widely considered one of the finest beaches in the northern Aegean. Behind the beach, archaeologists uncovered a well dating from the 3rd century BC, and on the headland above, the ruins of a 4th-century BC lighthouse.

Tuck into spit-roasted goat with mountain herbs at a taverna in Theologos or Panagia: it's the island's traditional feast dish, oven-baked or spit-roasted with wild herbs gathered from the slopes of Mount Ipsarion. The mountain-village tavernas are cheaper and more authentic than those on the beaches.

visits.text: The old town of Xanthi (Palia Poli) is one of the largest preserved traditional settlements in Greece: 380,000 m² declared a protected zone in 1976, with over 1,200 listed buildings. It was rebuilt after two devastating earthquakes in 1829 and reached its zenith between 1880 and 1925 thanks to basma tobacco — an exceptionally aromatic variety that supplied imperial courts and European markets. The Epirote and Macedonian craftsmen who built it blended Baroque, Neoclassical, Romanesque and Ottoman elements into a result the Greeks nicknamed 'Little Paris'.

Walk up the main street to Demokratias Square to see the clock tower, erected in 1870 by the Pomak notable Hadji Emin Aga. The Greek authorities wanted to demolish it in 1972 — protests by Muslim residents forced a reversal. It stands as a symbol of Xanthi's particular brand of coexistence, where Greek Orthodox Christians, Turks and Pomaks have shared the same streets for centuries.

The History and Folklore Museum of Xanthi occupies the Kougioumtzoglou House, a former double residence built in the 1860s by the Kougioumtzoglou brothers, major tobacco merchants. It's a rare example of a three-storey twin house in the local style. The collections feature traditional costumes, embroidered textiles, craft tools and archives from the tobacco era. Allow an hour to do it justice.

Near the train station, the former tobacco warehouses (the Mandra) bear witness to Xanthi's economic might at the turn of the 20th century. The most notable, shaped like the Greek letter Pi (Π), housed the Ottoman tobacco monopoly's operations until 1912. It's currently being converted into a cultural venue. If you drive or bus through the neighbourhood, its imposing red-brick outline is visible from the road.

activities.text: Wander through Xanthi's traditional bazaar, in the lanes around Antika Square: this is where you'll find the open-air cafés, spice-scented groceries, leather workshops and Turkish-coffee shopfronts. The atmosphere in the morning is particularly authentic — locals gather here before heading to work. A thick Greek coffee, served with a glass of cool water, is the ritual opener.

Day trip into the Rhodope Mountains to the Pomak villages, about an hour's drive north: Sminthi with its white mosque and stone lanes, Echinos set amid deep forest, Ottoman bridges over mountain torrents. These villages, tucked into the folds of the Rhodopes, have preserved an architecture and way of life virtually unchanged since the 19th century. The road itself is spectacular — forested gorges, pristine rivers, not a tourist in sight.

Try soutzouk loukoum, Xanthi's signature sweet: Turkish delight flavoured with walnuts or rose, sometimes coated in a layer of toasted hazelnuts. It's the Thracian version of a widespread Ottoman confection, but Xanthi's sweet shops have a well-established regional reputation. Pair it with a lightly anise-flavoured Thracian tsipouro — milder than ouzo.

visits.text: The lighthouse of Alexandroupoli has been the city's emblem since 1880: 27 metres tall, six flights of stone steps, and its beam visible from 44 kilometres out. It's still listed under the city's former name, Dede-Agats, on nautical charts — the town was only renamed Alexandroupoli in 1920 during a visit by King Alexander of Greece, and definitively incorporated into Greece by the Treaty of Lausanne in 1923. Grab a terrace seat facing the lighthouse for your morning coffee — it's the best introduction to the city.

The Ethnological Museum of Thrace occupies a Neoclassical house dating from 1899. Its collections of embroidered costumes, jewellery, craft tools and photographic archives offer a dense snapshot of Thracian culture — a region that was successively Bulgarian, Greek and Turkish in the 20th century before reaching its present configuration. Allow an hour: it's small but thoroughly documented.

Some 28 km west of Alexandroupoli, in the village of Feres, the Monastery of Panagia Kosmosotira is worth the detour. Founded in 1152 by Isaac Comnenus, son of Emperor Alexios III, it's one of the oldest churches still standing in northern Greece. Its layout echoes Hagia Sophia in miniature, with 12th-century frescoes from the Constantinopolitan school. It became a mosque in 1357 and was returned to Orthodox worship in 1920.

activities.text: Excursion to the Evros Delta, designated a national park in 2007 and a Ramsar wetland since 1974: 188 km² of wetlands on the Turkish border where 300 of Greece's 400 bird species pass through or nest. Dalmatian pelicans, white storks, purple herons and greater flamingos are regularly spotted. Start at the Traianoupoli Information Centre (village of Loutra) to obtain your military-zone entry permits and book a boat ride on the Drana Lagoon. The trip lasts about 1 h 30.

practical_info.booking: Permit required — contact the information centre in advance

activities.text: Evening walk along the Alexandroupoli seafront: the fish tavernas line up facing the Aegean and the illuminated lighthouse provides the backdrop. Order shrimp saganaki — baked in a tomato sauce with melted feta and ouzo — it's the unmissable local speciality, far better here than in Athens because the prawns come straight from the gulf.

For your last meal, try kagianas: scrambled eggs with fresh tomato and feta, served on village bread. Technically a breakfast dish or an evening meze, though the tavernas in this region serve it at any hour. Simple, filling, and deeply rooted in everyday Thracian cooking.

seo.h1_title: Thrace Road Trip: Kavala, Thasos & Philippi
seo.intro_paragraph: Start in Kavala, where a 270-metre Ottoman aqueduct has straddled the city since 1530, then cross to Philippi, where Brutus and Cassius lost Rome in 42 BC and Saint Paul founded Europe's first Christian community. Board a ferry to Thasos, the white-marble island of emerald waters, before diving into the colourful alleyways of Xanthi — once the world capital of basma tobacco — and finishing in Alexandroupoli, gateway to the Evros Delta and its 300 bird species.
seo.seo_hook: Ottoman aqueduct, Europe's first Christian site, an emerald marble island, an old town with 1,200 colourful façades, a delta alive with flamingos. Greek Thrace, at last.
featured_list: Kavala: Suleiman the Magnificent's Ottoman aqueduct and the old town of Panagia
Philippi UNESCO: Philip II's theatre, the Roman forum and Europe's first church
Thasos: the Emerald Island with ancient marble quarries, a giant kouros and sandy beaches
Xanthi: 1,200 listed buildings, basma tobacco and a preserved Ottoman quarter
Alexandroupoli: the 1880 lighthouse and the Evros Delta, an international wetland

places_taglines.GR::kavala: A Macedonian port where an Ottoman aqueduct soars above Venetian rooftops and the ghost of Mehmet Ali lingers
places_taglines.GR::thasos: An emerald island of white marble, colonised by Parian Greeks in the 7th century BC and barely touched since
places_taglines.GR::xanthi: Where 1,200 pastel façades tell the story of basma tobacco, Pomak minarets and a Greek town they once called Little Paris
places_taglines.GR::alexandroupoli: A frontier city whose 1880 lighthouse still guides ships across the Aegean to the flamingo-filled Evros Delta

highlights: The Kamares Aqueduct in Kavala, built by Ibrahim Pasha between 1520 and 1536
The UNESCO site of Philippi, where Saint Paul founded Europe's first Christian community in AD 49
Thasos and its Archaeological Museum with the 3.5-metre Kouros from the 6th century BC
The Evros Delta, an international Ramsar wetland home to 300 bird species

itineraries.title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace
itineraries.subtitle: From Thessaloniki to the wild beaches of Halkidiki, from Mount Athos to the Macedonian tombs of Amphipolis: a circuit between the Aegean, ancient history and monastic serenity.

visits.text: The White Tower is the first thing you'll see as you arrive along the waterfront — a cylindrical 15th-century fortification rebuilt under Suleiman the Magnificent, which initially served as an Ottoman prison and mass-execution site before being renamed the 'White Tower' in 1912 during the Greek reconquest. It now houses a museum on the history of Thessaloniki and offers a panoramic view from its summit over the Thermaic Gulf and, on a clear day, Mount Olympus. Allow 45 minutes for the visit.

A few minutes' walk away, the Basilica of Agios Dimitrios is the largest church in Greece dedicated to the city's patron saint. Built between 629 and 634 on the ruins of a Roman bath where Demetrios was imprisoned and executed under Diocletian, it measures 53.6 metres long by 33 metres wide and has five naves. It was converted into a mosque from 1493 to 1912 — known then as the Kasimiye Camii — though Christian pilgrims were tolerated at certain times. Don't miss the 7th-century mosaics on the pillars and the underground crypt where the ancient Roman baths once stood. Free entry.

The Galerius complex brings together two UNESCO-listed Roman monuments best seen together. The Arch of Galerius, inaugurated in 303 to celebrate victory over the Sassanid Persians at the Battle of Satala in 298, stands in the middle of Egnatia Street — the central arch is 9.7 metres wide and 12.5 metres high, with marble bas-reliefs still visible. 125 metres to the northeast, the Rotunda, begun in 306 as a mausoleum for Galerius, has a diameter of 24.5 metres and walls 6 metres thick — Galerius was ultimately buried elsewhere, and the building became in turn a temple, a church and then a mosque, whose last minaret in the city is still standing.

Climb to the Ano Poli, the old upper town and one of the few areas to survive the devastating fire of 1917. Its narrow lanes of Ottoman houses with wooden balconies offer plunging views over the Thermaic Gulf. Don't miss the Heptapyrgion, the seven-towered Byzantine fortress that commands the city from the hilltop, nor the Church of Hosios David (Latomos Monastery), whose 5th-century mosaics are UNESCO-listed.

activities.text: Head down in the late afternoon to Aristotelous Square, the city's nerve centre designed by a French architect after the 1917 fire in a style blending Art Deco with classical references. The café terraces fill up fast and the sea breeze blows in from the waterfront. Order a café frappé — Thessaloniki claims to have invented this iced drink — and watch local life unfold.

For dinner, head to the Modiano Market, built in the 1920s and recently renovated: 75 shops, delicatessens, local cheeses and a handful of tavernas inside. The Ladadika neighbourhood just next door, in colourful former warehouses, is the heart of the nightlife with its rebetiko music and Macedonian cooking. Try the bougatsa, a cream or cheese pastry that's a Thessaloniki staple.

visits.text: Potamos beach at Epanomi, 35 km south of Thessaloniki, is a pointed sandy spit jutting into the Thermaic Gulf — its shape recalls Croatia's Zlatni Rat, but with far fewer people. On a clear day, Mount Olympus is visible across the gulf. What makes the site truly unusual is the wreck of a cargo ship that ran aground more than 50 years ago just off the shore: locals say Saint Antonios himself sank the vessel to protect the adjacent 550-hectare wetland, a refuge for migratory birds. Arrive in the morning for the calm before the Thessaloniki families descend.

The Epanomi wetland stretches across 550 hectares between dunes and marshes — a sanctuary for migratory birds, with herons, passing flamingos and numerous aquatic species. A trail runs along the lagoon from the east car park of the beach. The site is especially active in spring and autumn during the migrations.

activities.text: The wreck offshore is reachable by swimming or snorkelling from the beach — the water is clear and shallow here, making it an excellent snorkelling spot suitable for all levels. The west side of the bay, exposed to the wind, is popular with kitesurfers.

The Epanomi area is known for its vineyards: the soil and microclimate produce characterful dry whites. A short drive through the surroundings will take you past several wineries open for visits in summer. End the day at sunset facing the gulf — Olympus turns orange.

visits.text: Entering the Kassandra peninsula, stop at Nea Fokea to see the Byzantine tower by the harbour. This 28-metre stone tower, built in 1407 to protect the metochion of the Saint Paul Monastery on Mount Athos, is the best preserved in all of Halkidiki — intact right up to the battlements. In 1821, it served as the headquarters of Emmanouil Papas, leader of the Greek uprising in Halkidiki. At its base lies the cave sanctuary of Saint Paul, a chapel hewn into the rock.

Cape Possidi, at the south-western tip of Kassandra, is a shifting tongue of sand that juts into the sea — its shape changes with the tides and the seasons. The 19th-century lighthouse at its end is one of the most photographed sunset spots in Halkidiki. The village of Afitos, perched on the ancient acropolis a few kilometres away, is worth a detour for its stone houses, cobbled lanes and views over the Toroneos Gulf.

activities.text: The beaches of Kassandra are among the most accessible in Halkidiki — several hold the Blue Flag, the sand is fine and the shallow waters are ideal for families. The coast around Possidi alternates between organised beaches with sunbeds and wild coves fringed with pines. Swim in the morning, before the buses from Thessaloniki arrive.

visits.text: Sithonia, Halkidiki's middle peninsula, is a different world from Kassandra. Here, pine forests plunge straight into emerald coves, winding roads reveal a new bay at every turn and the fishing villages haven't been swallowed by hotels. Drive the loop starting from the east coast — the views across to Mount Athos are breathtaking.

Karydi Beach, near Vourvourou, regularly ranks among the finest beaches in northern Greece: powdery white sand, turquoise water barely knee-deep, set against pines and sculpted rocks. It's a wild beach — no sunbeds, no umbrellas — so bring everything you need. Arrive before 10am in summer; it fills quickly.

Kavourotripes, also known as Orange Beach, is a chain of small coves separated by white cliffs tinged with orange. The water shifts from azure blue to emerald green. It's the most famous organised beach on Sithonia — sunbeds are available at the main cove, the others remain wild. If you're worried about the parking situation (it's chaos in August), arrive early.

Neos Marmaras is Sithonia's liveliest port — dozens of waterfront tavernas, excursion boats and a marina. It's a practical base for exploring the peninsula, but also the place to enjoy fresh seafood in the evening under the harbour lights.

activities.text: Rent a boat (no licence required) from Neos Marmaras or Vourvourou to explore the coves that can't be reached by road — particularly the beaches of Platanitsi and Armenistis, which look even wilder from the sea. Organised excursions from these ports also offer views of the Mount Athos coastline opposite.

Porto Koufo, at Sithonia's southern tip, is an almost enclosed natural harbour: two sheer cliffs leave a 300-metre opening onto the sea. The effect is dramatic and the waters inside are perfectly still. Stop here for a lunch of grilled fish at a waterside taverna.

visits.text: The Prosphorion Tower dominates the entrance to Ouranoupoli's harbour — a 14th-century Byzantine fortification built to protect the metochion of Vatopedi Monastery, and one of the best-preserved medieval monuments in the region. It houses a small museum on the history of the Athos peninsula. It's also the rallying point for every cruise to Mount Athos: you'll recognise it by the quay packed with pilgrims and tourists in the morning.

Ouranoupoli — 'City of Heaven' in Greek — is the last town before the border of the Monastic Republic of Mount Athos, an autonomous theocratic state home to some 1,500 monks in 20 monasteries. Women and non-Orthodox laypeople are not permitted to enter (10 non-Orthodox per day, men only, by special permit). From the harbour, though, you can see the foothills of the peninsula rising from the sea.

activities.text: Take the coastal cruise departing from the harbour — boats leave daily at 10:30am to sail along the western coast of the Mount Athos peninsula and observe the monasteries clinging to the cliffs from the sea. The cruise lasts about 3 hours 45 minutes and passes monasteries such as Xenophontos and Simonos Petras. It's open to everyone, men and women, and is the only way for the vast majority of visitors to get close to the site. Book ahead in summer.

In the late afternoon, catch the small ferry to Ammouliani Island, 10 minutes from Ouranoupoli. This island, largely untouched by mass tourism, has crystal-clear waters and secluded beaches — notably Alikes beach, a spectacular crescent of golden sand. The return trip in the late afternoon leaves time for sunset over the harbour.

visits.text: The Lion of Amphipolis has stood beside the Strymon since its reconstruction in 1936 — a colossal 8-metre-tall statue (including its base) in Thasian marble, dated to the 4th century BC. Recent excavations suggest it crowned the Kasta burial mound. It is thought to have been erected in honour of Laomedon of Mytilene, one of Alexander the Great's generals. From the road, the lion's silhouette is clearly visible above the river — stop for the photo.

The Kasta tumulus, 4.5 km away, is the largest funerary monument ever discovered in Greece. Excavations since 2012 have revealed a 497-metre-long, 158-metre-diameter peribolos in Thasian marble, dated to the last quarter of the 4th century BC. In 2014, archaeologists uncovered inside a three-chambered Macedonian tomb with two sphinxes, two caryatids, a pebble mosaic depicting the abduction of Persephone by Hades and, in the third chamber, skeletal remains of at least five individuals. The identity of the deceased remains unknown. A protective building has been erected over the site — check opening times before visiting.

The Archaeological Museum of Amphipolis, in the village, displays finds from local excavations: marble reliefs, gold jewellery, vases, sculptures and liturgical objects testifying to the intense commercial activity of Amphipolis, founded by the Athenians in 437 BC on the Thracian site of Ennea Hodoi. The city was in turn Athenian, Spartan and then Macedonian — Philip II seized it in 357 BC.

activities.text: Stroll along the Strymon in the afternoon — the river was navigable in antiquity and Amphipolis' strategic position at its mouth explains why so many empires fought over it. The banks are quiet and shaded, with views over the surrounding Macedonian hills. The two Byzantine towers flanking the river, the better-preserved of which dates from 1367, are visible from the bridge.

visits.text: On the way to Dikella, make a detour through Kavala — a port city built into a hillside descending to the Aegean, facing the island of Thasos. Its old Ottoman quarter of Panagia is best explored on foot from the 270-metre Kamares aqueduct, built in the 16th century under Suleiman the Magnificent. Climb to the citadel for the sea view. In the quarter's streets, look for the birthplace of Mehmet Ali, an Ottoman officer born here in the 18th century and considered the founder of modern Egypt.

Twenty-five minutes north of Kavala, the site of Philippi is an essential stop. Founded in 360 BC by settlers from Thasos, fortified by Philip II of Macedon in 356, it witnessed the Battle of Philippi in 42 BC where Octavian (the future Augustus) and Mark Antony crushed Brutus and Cassius. In AD 50, the Apostle Paul founded the first Christian church in Europe here. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, the grounds include an ancient theatre still used in summer, early Christian basilicas, a Roman forum and a museum. Allow at least 2 hours.

Dikella beach, on the northern Aegean coast, is a long stretch of golden sand and smooth pebbles with good facilities — sunbeds, showers, a gentle slope into the water — popular with families from the Alexandroupoli area. It's quiet, rarely visited by foreign tourists and makes for a peaceful end to the circuit before the return via Kavala (airport less than 30 km away).

activities.text: Spend the afternoon at Dikella beach — the water is clean and shallow, the Aegean sunset is serene and crowd-free. Mesi beach, a few kilometres along, is even wilder if you're looking for total solitude.

Finish with a seafood dinner at one of the waterfront restaurants in the nearby village of Agia Paraskevi — octopus, Thracian mussels and grilled fish have been served here for decades by local fishing families. The cuisine of Eastern Macedonia blends Greek, Turkish and Balkan influences in a way quite different from the islands.

seo.h1_title: Northern Greece's Finest: Thessaloniki to Thrace
seo.intro_paragraph: Thessaloniki opens onto two millennia of layered history — Roman triumphal arches, UNESCO-listed Byzantine basilicas, preserved Ottoman quarters — then Halkidiki unfurls its three peninsulas of wild coves and emerald waters. Ouranoupoli marks the frontier of a monastic state unlike any other, Mount Athos, approached by cruise. The road ends with Amphipolis and its majestic archaeological discoveries, before the open sea of Thrace.
seo.seo_hook: Thessaloniki and its 15 UNESCO monuments, Sithonia's wild coves, Mount Athos monasteries seen from the sea, Amphipolis' Macedonian tomb. Northern Greece, the road less travelled.
featured_list: Thessaloniki: 2,000 years of history in a single city
Sithonia: wild coves with emerald waters
Mount Athos: monasteries observed from the sea
Amphipolis: the largest Macedonian tomb ever discovered

places_taglines.GR::thessalonique: A port metropolis where fifteen UNESCO-listed Byzantine monuments stand shoulder to shoulder
places_taglines.GR::epanomi: A wild sandy spit with a shipwreck offshore and Mount Olympus on the horizon
places_taglines.GR::possidi: Kassandra's shifting sand cape with a 19th-century lighthouse and a Byzantine tower from 1407
places_taglines.GR::kalamitsi: A tranquil Sithonia bay ringed by turquoise coves among the most beautiful in Greece
places_taglines.GR::ouranoupoli: The last village before the Monastic Republic of Mount Athos, a UNESCO-listed world apart
places_taglines.GR::amphipolis: Home to the largest Macedonian tomb ever unearthed, dating from the 4th century BC
places_taglines.GR::dikella: Golden-pebble beach on the Aegean coast, a short drive from the UNESCO ruins of Philippi

highlights: Thessaloniki and its UNESCO-listed early Christian and Byzantine monuments
Sithonia's wild beaches, among the finest in the Aegean
Coastal cruise past the monasteries of Mount Athos from Ouranoupoli
The Kasta tumulus at Amphipolis, the largest Macedonian tomb ever discovered

itineraries.title: Classical Greece Road Trip: Athens To Olympia
itineraries.subtitle: From the Acropolis of Athens to the stadiums of Olympia, through the Mycenaean citadels, the Byzantine frescoes of Mystras and the Oracle of Delphi — seven days across the founding sites of Western civilization.
specialties: Moussaka and souvlaki – Greek comfort food staples
Kalamata olives – PDO olives from the Peloponnese
Formaela cheese – grilled PDO cheese from Arachova
Tsipouro – Peloponnesian grape spirit served with meze
Handwoven textiles from the mountain villages of Parnassus

visits.text: Start at Syntagma Square, the modern heart of Athens. Facing Parliament — housed in the former royal palace — the Evzones guard change takes place every hour at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. These soldiers in pleated kilts and pom-pom shoes are equal parts kitsch and photogenic, and locals love watching tourists break into applause.

Head down to the Olympieion, or Temple of Olympian Zeus, whose construction began in the 6th century BC and wasn't completed until Emperor Hadrian finished the job in 131 AD — over six centuries of building work. Originally, 104 Corinthian columns standing 17 meters tall framed a chryselephantine statue of Zeus. Only 15 remain upright today: enough for the silhouette to stop you in your tracks from the street. Right next to it, walk through Hadrian's Arch, which once marked the boundary between classical Athens and the Roman city. The inscription carved on each side neatly sums up the ambition of the era.

Climb up to the Acropolis early in the morning — since 2023, a daily cap of 20,000 visitors is enforced, and tickets sell out fast in peak season. Book online the day before. The hilltop measures 170 meters long by 350 meters wide and rises to 156 meters above sea level. Once through the Propylaea, you emerge face-to-face with the Parthenon, built between 447 and 432 BC on the orders of Pericles by architects Ictinus and Callicrates, under the supervision of the sculptor Phidias. It measures 69.5 meters long by 30.88 meters wide, surrounded by 8 Doric columns on each façade and 17 along the sides — 22,000 tonnes of white marble quarried from Mount Pentelicus. The 12-meter-tall gold and ivory statue of Athena it once housed is long gone. Wear non-slip shoes: the paving stones are polished smooth and treacherously slippery.

On the way down, don't miss the Acropolis Museum, opened in 2009 at the foot of the hill. This is where the real Caryatids from the Erechtheion are kept (the ones outside are copies), along with the original Parthenon friezes and polychrome archaic pediments. The glass building is designed so that you can see the Parthenon in the background throughout the entire visit — a deliberate and strikingly effective touch. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

Spend some time at the Ancient Agora, at the foot of the hill: this is where merchants, philosophers and citizens gathered. Socrates held debates here, and Athens' earliest judges presided over trials on this ground. The Temple of Hephaestus, built around 450 BC, is one of the best-preserved classical Greek temples in the world — its roof is still intact. The museum housed in the Stoa of Attalos displays ostraka, the clay shards on which Athenians voted to banish citizens who had grown too powerful.

The Panathenaic Stadium — the Kallimarmaro, meaning "beautiful marble" in Greek — was first built in 330 BC and entirely refaced in white marble to host the first modern Olympic Games in 1896. It stretches 200 meters long and could seat 50,000 spectators in antiquity. In 2004, it served as the finish line for the Olympic marathon. You can run on the track yourself: the guards let you, as long as it's outside peak hours.

activities.text: As evening sets in, lose yourself in the Plaka district, wedged between the Acropolis and Syntagma. It's the oldest continuously inhabited part of Athens, a tangle of narrow lanes, neoclassical houses and open-air tavernas. Skip the restaurants on the main street (tourist prices) and seek out the ones tucked into the side alleys — you'll find proper Greek cooking at half the cost. Climb up to Anafiotika, the tiny Cycladic-style neighborhood clinging to the rock face, built in the 19th century by workers from the island of Anafi who came to construct the royal palace.

The mezedopolia is the art of sharing a spread of small plates: taramosalata (cod roe dip), tirokafteri (spicy cheese spread), grilled octopus, dill-braised broad beans, lamb meatballs. In Athens, the ritual of drinks and meze on a terrace kicks off around 7 PM and can easily stretch past midnight. It's the best way to eat like a local.

visits.text: First stop on the road: the Corinth Canal. Dug between 1882 and 1893 (plans date back to antiquity — Nero himself launched construction in 67 AD before giving up), it connects the Gulf of Corinth to the Aegean Sea over a length of 6.4 km. What really strikes you are the sheer vertical walls: 79 meters of limestone sliced clean. The width is only 21 meters — enough for small cruise ships, but not for supertankers. Park on the road bridge and look straight down: vertigo guaranteed.

The Theatre of Epidaurus is often described as the most perfect in antiquity, and for once, that's not an exaggeration. Built around 330 BC by the Argive architect Polykleitos the Younger, it originally had 34 rows of seats (6,200 capacity), with 21 additional rows added in Roman times to bring the total to 12,000 spectators. Climb to the very top row and ask someone at the bottom to whisper or crumple a sheet of paper: you'll hear it perfectly from 22 meters above. Researchers still debate the exact acoustic explanation — the limestone may filter out low frequencies while letting high ones through. Arrive early in the morning to beat the cruise ship coaches from Athens.

The Sanctuary of Asclepius, god of medicine, is considered the first major healing center of antiquity — the "Lourdes" of the ancient Greek world. The sick traveled from across Greece to practice incubation: they slept in the abaton (a 70-meter-long portico) hoping that Asclepius would appear in their dreams to reveal a cure. The complex also included a gymnasium, baths, a 180-meter stadium and a library — embodying the idea that body and soul heal together. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988.

The small Archaeological Museum of Epidaurus, at the entrance to the site, displays ancient medical instruments, stelae carved with accounts of miraculous healings, and a remarkable statue of Hygieia dating from the 4th century BC. The inscriptions on the stelae are particularly gripping: they describe cures, visions and resurrections — a blend of real medicine and faith. Allow 30 to 45 minutes.

End the day in Nafplio, the first capital of independent Greece in 1829, nestled at the head of a sheltered gulf. Its Venetian center is one of the most beautiful in the Peloponnese: cobblestone lanes, ochre and burgundy façades, cafés lining the local Syntagma Square. If you still have energy, climb up to Palamidi Fortress — 999 steps according to some, 216 according to others. The view over the bay and the floating fort of Bourtzi is worth every step.

activities.text: In summer, the Athens & Epidaurus Festival programs performances of ancient tragedies and comedies on Friday and Saturday evenings at the Theatre of Epidaurus. Aeschylus, Sophocles and Euripides performed in their original setting, under the open sky, with the Argolid mountains as a backdrop — an experience unlike anything else. Check the program before your trip and book well in advance.

The bay of Tolo, a small seaside resort a few kilometers from Nafplio, makes the ideal base for the night. The waterfront tavernas serve grilled fish straight off the morning boats. Order some psarosoupa (fish soup) and saganaki (fried cheese) with a glass of local white wine.

visits.text: Tiryns is often overlooked in favor of Mycenae — unfairly so. This Mycenaean citadel from the 14th century BC boasts the best-preserved Cyclopean walls in the ancient world: up to 14 meters thick, with vaulted galleries carved into the walls themselves. The ancient Greeks believed only the Cyclopes could have assembled such massive blocks. Homer called it "Tiryns of the mighty walls." It's a UNESCO site and far less crowded than Mycenae: chances are you'll have the place to yourself.

Mycenae is the city of Agamemnon, the king who led the Greeks against Troy. Founded, according to legend, by Perseus, it reached its peak between 1400 and 1200 BC and influenced the entire eastern Mediterranean. Entering through the Lion Gate, erected around 1250 BC, you pass beneath the oldest monumental relief sculpture in Europe: two lionesses (their metal heads are lost) flanking a Minoan-style column. The Cyclopean rampart stones weigh several tonnes each and are fitted together without mortar. Arrive early — in peak season, the parking lot fills up by 9 AM.

Don't miss the Treasury of Atreus, 300 meters from the main gate — it's actually a royal tholos tomb dating from 1250 BC. You enter through a 36-meter open-air corridor (the dromos), flanked by walls 6 meters high. The circular chamber measures 14.50 meters in diameter and 13.20 meters in height: it was the largest dome in the Western world until the Pantheon in Rome was built five centuries later. The acoustics inside are eerie and mesmerizing — try clapping your hands.

Head back down to Nafplio to explore its Venetian old town. Palamidi Fortress, perched 216 meters above the harbor, was built by the Venetians between 1711 and 1714 in a record time of three years. From its upper bastion, the view sweeps across the entire Gulf of Argolis. If you're only going to climb one fortress at sunset on this trip, make it this one: the golden light on the gulf is stunning.

activities.text: Nafplio deserves a proper on-foot exploration. Cross Syntagma Square, lined with Venetian and Ottoman houses, pass the Vourliotis Mosque (now a cinema), then walk along the harbor to the Bourtzi Fort, built on a tiny island in the middle of the bay. Water taxis shuttle you there in just a few minutes.

Argolid cuisine is generous: lamb kleftiko (slow-braised lamb), crispy spanakopita, local cheeses drizzled with Peloponnesian honey. The tavernas along Nafplio's harbor are among the best in the region — look for the ones without a laminated menu posted by the door.

visits.text: Mystras is one of the great surprises of the Peloponnese. This ghostly Byzantine city, terraced along the slopes of Mount Taygetus at 620 meters altitude, is often called the "Wonder of the Morea." Founded in 1249 by the Frankish prince William II of Villehardouin, it passed to the Byzantines in 1262 and became the capital of the Despotate of the Morea in 1348 — a semi-autonomous province of the Byzantine Empire. At its height, 20,000 people lived here. In 1449, Constantine XI was crowned here as the last emperor of Byzantium before dying on the walls of Constantinople in 1453. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1989.

The Peribleptos Monastery is literally clinging to the rock face — the church is carved into the cliff itself. Inside, the 14th-century frescoes are among the finest examples of late Byzantine art: Christ Pantocrator in the dome, vivid narrative scenes from the life of Christ. The colors are still remarkably bright. Dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered) and wear sturdy shoes for the steep cobbled paths.

The Pantanassa Monastery is the only one in Mystras still inhabited: a small community of Orthodox nuns maintains the grounds. Its ornate stone façade and 15th-century frescoes, blazing with color, already betray the influence of the Italian Renaissance — Mystras and Florence were exchanging artists and intellectuals during this period. The flower-filled courtyard is an oasis of calm amid the ruins. The nuns sometimes sell aromatic herbs and homemade preserves.

For the brave, climb all the way up to the fortress at the hilltop. The ascent is steep (roughly 40 minutes from the lower town) but the reward matches the effort: a 360° panorama over the plain of Laconia, the Taygetus massif — sometimes capped with snow — and, on clear days, the Mediterranean Sea in the distance. This is where you truly grasp why the Byzantines chose this impregnable position.

activities.text: Visiting Mystras works in three stages: the lower town (monasteries and churches), the upper town (Palace of the Despots) and the fortress (panorama). Allow at least 3 hours. In summer, start at opening time (8 AM) to avoid the midday heat on the exposed hillside. Bring water — there are no refreshment points anywhere on the site.

In the evening, modern Sparta is a functional town without much charm — but the handful of tavernas around the main square serve solid roast lamb and Kalamata olives (the best in Greece come from this very region). The Archaeological Museum of Sparta, a 10-minute walk away, is also worth a visit for its votive Laconian terracotta masks from the 7th century BC.

visits.text: The road from Sparta crosses Kalamata, capital of Messenia and world-famous for its olives. If you pass through mid-morning, stop at the central covered market: spices, olive oils, marinated olives of every kind, local cheeses. It's the best spot in the Peloponnese for gourmet shopping before heading on toward the Messenian coast.

The Archaeological Museum of Olympia is one of the most important in Greece. Don't skip it in favor of the ruins — do both. Its star pieces include the Hermes of Praxiteles (one of the finest marble statues from antiquity, discovered in 1877, standing 2.10 meters tall), the winged Victory of Paionios, and the monumental pediments of the Temple of Zeus depicting the chariot race between Pelops and Oenomaus. The collection of ancient bronzes is the largest in the world. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

The ancient site of Olympia has been inhabited since the 3rd millennium BC, and the Games were held here without interruption from 776 BC to 393 AD — over 1,100 years. The Altis, Zeus' sacred grove, forms the heart of the sanctuary. The Temple of Zeus, built between 470 and 456 BC, once housed Phidias' chryselephantine statue of Zeus — 12 meters tall and one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Only toppled columns remain, brought down by earthquakes. The Olympic flame is still lit here every four years in front of the Temple of Hera during the official ceremony.

The ancient stadium of Olympia measures 212.54 meters long by 28.50 meters wide. Built in the 5th century BC, it could hold 40,000 to 45,000 spectators seated on the natural earthen banks. At the entrance, you pass through the same vaulted tunnel that athletes used in antiquity. Run the length of the track — it's the "stadion," the original Olympic event: 192 meters, one Greek stade. You won't be the first to try.

activities.text: The modern village of Olympia, a few hundred meters from the site, lives entirely off tourism — but its tavernas are honest and convenient after a long day of sightseeing. Try the kokoretsi (spit-roasted lamb offal), a Peloponnesian specialty, and a glass of Mantinia wine, a fruity white Moschofilero produced in the Arcadia region.

If you arrive early in the afternoon, also explore the Museum of the History of the Ancient Olympic Games, housed in the former archaeological museum building. It traces the history of the events, the ceremonies and the sacred truce (the Ekecheiria) that suspended all wars during the Games. More accessible than the main museum, it's particularly well-suited for children.

visits.text: The road from Olympia to Arachova crosses the Rio-Antirrio Bridge, inaugurated in 2004 for the Athens Olympic Games. Spanning 2.9 km, this cable-stayed bridge crosses the Gulf of Corinth at its narrowest point — it links the Peloponnese to mainland Greece and replaced a ferry crossing that took 15 minutes but could mean hours of waiting in season. Pull over at the rest area on the Antirrio side for a close-up view of the structure.

From Antirrio, the road climbs toward Parnassus through a landscape of limestone hills, pine forests and hilltop villages. Arachova appears suddenly at 960 meters altitude, its stone houses stacked along the cliff above a breathtaking drop. The village is nicknamed the "winter Mykonos" by Athenians who come to ski on the slopes of Parnassus, 20 km away. In summer, it's far more tranquil — and the late-afternoon light on the stone façades is extraordinary.

The Arachova clock tower, perched on a rocky spur overlooking the village, is the town's landmark. The small square below, ringed by open-air cafés, is where locals gather in the evening. From here you get the best view of the village tumbling down toward the Pleistos valley 600 meters below. The Church of Saint George, reached via 264 steps from the center, offers an even wider panorama of the region.

activities.text: Arachova's main street is one long corridor of shops selling local specialties. Formaela is the region's PDO cheese: semi-hard, made from sheep's milk, with a sharp, slightly tangy flavor. It's eaten grilled over charcoal or pan-fried in olive oil and drizzled with local honey — order it as a starter at any taverna. Hand-woven rugs and textiles are the village's other artisan specialty.

After dinner, stroll through the cobbled lanes of Arachova after dark: the stone houses are softly lit, the tavernas hum with life, and the view of the lights in the valley below is striking. It's one of the most photogenic villages in central Greece, especially outside the peak tourist season.

visits.text: The Sanctuary of Delphi, 10 km from Arachova, was considered by the ancient Greeks to be the navel of the world — the omphalos, an ogive-shaped sacred stone, was believed to mark its exact center. This pan-Hellenic sanctuary dedicated to Apollo was, from the 6th to the 4th century BC, the most influential in Greece: kings, tyrants and city-states came to consult the Pythia before every major decision — war, founding a colony, a dynastic marriage. The Sacred Way winds uphill from the entrance, lined with treasuries offered by the various cities (including the finely reconstructed Treasury of the Athenians) up to the Temple of Apollo, of which 6 columns still stand. The maxims of the Sages — including the famous "Know thyself" — were inscribed on the pediment. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

Before entering the main sanctuary, make the detour to Marmaria, the Sanctuary of Athena Pronaia below the road. This is where you'll find the Tholos of Delphi, built around 380–360 BC by Theodoros of Phocaea: a circular structure of white marble and blue Eleusis limestone, with 20 Doric columns on the outside and 10 Corinthian columns within. Three columns have been re-erected, and it's the most photographed image of Delphi — deservedly so. Its function remains a mystery to archaeologists.

The Archaeological Museum of Delphi, next to the site, is essential to complete the visit. Its star piece is the Charioteer of Delphi, a bronze sculpture from 478 BC discovered in 1896 — 1.80 meters tall, with inlaid glass paste eyes still in place and drapery of breathtaking finesse. It is one of the very few classical Greek bronzes to have survived in its entirety. The museum also houses the original omphalos, the bronze bull from the island of Corcyra, and gold and silver votive offerings from every corner of the Greek world.

activities.text: If you still have energy after Delphi, climb up to the theatre and the stadium above the sanctuary. The 4th-century BC theatre (4,500 seats) offers the best overall view of the site from its upper rows — with the columns of the Temple of Apollo in the foreground and the Pleistos valley stretching to infinity behind. The stadium, even higher up (7,000 seats, built for the Pythian Games held every four years), is often deserted, leaving you free to run the ancient track in peace.

The drive back to Athens via Livadia and Thiva (Thebes) crosses the Boeotian plains and the Parnassus passes. Allow 2.5 to 3 hours of driving. Arriving in Athens in the evening, head to the tavernas of Monastiraki or Exarcheia for a final Greek meal before departure — the souvlaki joints in Monastiraki stay open until 3 AM.

seo.h1_title: Classical Greece Road Trip: Athens To Olympia
seo.intro_paragraph: From the Acropolis of Athens to the stadiums of Olympia, this road trip crosses the most foundational sites of Western civilization. The Cyclopean citadels of Mycenae, the theatre of Epidaurus with its unexplained acoustics, the ghostly Byzantine city of Mystras, the ruins of Olympia where the Games were celebrated for eleven centuries, and Delphi where Apollo's oracle advised kings and city-states — seven days of open air, marble and history.
seo.seo_hook: Athens' Acropolis and Parthenon, Mycenaean citadels, the Theatre of Epidaurus, Byzantine Mystras, the stadiums of Olympia, the Oracle of Delphi — the foundations of the West in a single road trip.
featured_list: Athens: Pericles' Acropolis and the museum that brings it to life
Epidaurus: the theatre with unexplained acoustic properties
Mycenae: the city of Agamemnon and the Treasury of Atreus
Delphi: the navel of the world and the bronze Charioteer

places_taglines.GR::athenes: Where Pericles' Acropolis stands watch over 3,000 years of history
places_taglines.GR::tolo: A sun-soaked base in Argolis, between Mycenae, Epidaurus and Nafplio
places_taglines.GR::sparte: Gateway to Mystras, the UNESCO-listed ghost city of Byzantium
places_taglines.GR::olympie: Birthplace of the Olympic Games, celebrated here for eleven centuries
places_taglines.GR::arachova: A stone-built village perched on Parnassus, at the doorstep of Delphi

highlights: Athens Acropolis: book online, daily cap of 20,000 visitors
Theatre of Epidaurus: test the acoustics from the top rows
Mystras: allow at least 3 hours, bring water, modest dress required
Olympia: site + archaeological museum in half a day, run the stadium

itineraries.title: Peloponnese Road Trip: Mycenae, Epidaurus & Olympia
itineraries.subtitle: From the Cyclopean walls of Mycenae to the stone tiers of Epidaurus — where acoustics still baffle engineers — and on to the stadium where Phidias carved the statue of Zeus: the classical Peloponnese in six days.
itineraries.notes: The route ends in Olympia, roughly 90 km from Patras (an international port with ferries to Italy and rail connections). Plan your exit via Patras or Pyrgos.

visits.text: The Theatre of Epidaurus is arguably the most spectacular ancient theatre still standing anywhere in the world. Built in the 4th or early 3rd century BC on the slopes of Mount Kynortion, it originally had 34 rows of seats holding 6,200 spectators — the Romans later added 21 more rows, bringing capacity to nearly 14,000. Walk to the centre of the orchestra (the circular stone slab on the ground) and drop a coin: you'll hear it ring from the very last row, 60 metres away. Researchers at the Georgia Institute of Technology found that the limestone seats act as a natural acoustic filter, dampening low-frequency background noise while letting voices travel unamplified. Arrive in the morning to beat the tour groups that flood in after 10 am.

Five hundred metres from the theatre, the Sanctuary of Asklepios served for six centuries as the foremost healing centre in the Greek world. Pilgrims journeyed from across the Mediterranean to practise incubation — sleeping in the abaton (a sacred dormitory) in the hope that the god of medicine would appear in their dreams and cure them. Excavations uncovered votive stelae describing healings — hernias, blindness, paralysis — that would make a modern physician smile, yet they attest to a belief system that endured for a millennium. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988.

The Tholos of Epidaurus, dated to around 360 BC, is the most enigmatic structure in the sanctuary. This circular building featured a triple ring of columns — 6 Doric on the outside, 14 Corinthian on the inside — and its purpose remains hotly debated: healing rituals, a home for the sacred serpents of Asklepios, or an initiation chamber? Don't bother looking for the answer in the museum — it's not there either. Take the time instead to admire the beautifully preserved Corinthian capitals.

The Epidaurus Museum, at the foot of the site, displays sculptures and architectural fragments unearthed during the excavations. Don't miss the scale models that reconstruct the sanctuary — they give a vivid sense of how vast this complex was at its Hellenistic peak, far larger than what you've just walked through. Allow about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Test the theatre's acoustics for yourself: stand on the central slab of the orchestra and whisper a sentence. Ask a companion to climb to the top row — they'll hear you clearly. If you're on your own, drop a coin on the stone. Guides have been doing this demonstration for centuries, and it never fails to astonish. The Epidaurus Festival stages ancient tragedies every Friday and Saturday evening from June through September — if you're visiting in season, book ahead.

practical_info.booking: Epidaurus Festival: advance booking recommended in summer

activities.text: Swimming in the coves between Paleá Epídavros and Nea Epidavros, just a few kilometres from the site: small pebble beaches hemmed in by pine trees, with crystal-clear water. The village of Paleá Epídavros has a fishing harbour lined with waterfront seafood tavernas — perfect for dinner.

Try gemista: tomatoes and peppers stuffed with rice, fresh herbs and sometimes minced meat, slow-baked in the oven. It's a summer staple in Peloponnese tavernas and far more flavourful than the name suggests. Pair it with a glass of assyrtiko — a dry, mineral white with a crisp acidity that cuts beautifully through the stuffed vegetables.

visits.text: The Palamidi Fortress towers over Nafplio from 216 metres above sea level. The Venetians built it between 1711 and 1714 under the direction of two French engineers, Lasalle and Levasseur — but the Ottomans under Ali Douad Pasha captured it as early as 1715, before it was even finished, slaughtering the entire garrison. Climb the stairs from the old town: legend says 999 steps, though actual counts range from 857 to 913. Bring water and avoid midday in summer. The view from the ramparts over the Argolic Gulf, the rooftops below and the tiny Bourtzi island is the finest panorama in the eastern Peloponnese.

The Bourtzi is the small fortress perched on the islet at the harbour mouth, built by the Venetians in 1473 under the name Castello dello Soglio. Its history is worth a closer look: a chain stretching from Akronafplia to the islet once blocked enemy ships from entering the port — hence the Venetian nickname Porto Cadena. The Ottomans renamed it Bourtzi ("fortress"). Later, it served as the residence of the executioner from the Palamidi prison — a character the town wisely kept at arm's length. Small boats leave from the harbour roughly every 30 minutes in season.

Nafplio's old town may be compact, but its Venetian alleyways, stone staircases and cascading bougainvillea make it one of the loveliest towns in the Peloponnese. Nafplio was the first capital of independent Greece in 1828 — it was here that Ioannis Kapodistrias, the country's first governor, was assassinated on the steps of the Church of Agios Spyridon in 1831. The commemorative plaque is still visible. Don't miss Syntagma Square with its cafés tucked under arcaded buildings: this is where the first legislators of modern Greece held their sessions.

The Nafplio Archaeological Museum, housed in an 18th-century Venetian building on Syntagma Square, holds Mycenaean, Geometric and Classical collections from across the Argolid. Its star exhibit is a nearly complete Mycenaean bronze suit of armour dated to the 14th century BC, discovered at Dendra near Midea — one of the rarest surviving Bronze Age suits of armour in the world. Allow about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Climb to the Palamidi Fortress on foot from the gate on Arvanitia Street in the old town. Start early in the morning (before 9 am in summer) to avoid the heat and catch the soft morning light over the gulf. If you'd rather spare your knees, the eastern access road (3.6 km by car) drops you right at the entrance.

practical_info.difficulty: Strenuous — bring water and closed-toe shoes

activities.text: Stroll along Arvanitia Beach, a 10-minute walk from the old town along the coastal path beneath the Akronafplia fortress. It's a small pebble beach popular with locals, with transparent water and a direct view up to the cliffs and ramparts. Ideal for a late-afternoon swim after a day of sightseeing.

Dinner in the old town — order the youvetsi: lamb braised slowly with orzo pasta in a thick tomato sauce, baked in a clay pot. It's the classic celebratory dish of the Argolid. Pair it with a red agiorgitiko from Nemea — the signature grape of the Peloponnese, velvety and fruity, grown just 40 km away.

visits.text: Mycenae lies 25 km from Nafplio — an easy half-day trip from your base. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1999, it sits at the heart of the Mycenaean civilisation that dominated the eastern Mediterranean from the 15th to the 12th century BC. Right at the entrance, the Lion Gate greets you: built around 1250 BC, this monumental doorway is topped by an 18-tonne lintel and a relief of two lionesses in heraldic pose flanking a column. It is the oldest monumental sculpture in Europe, and the original bronze heads vanished in antiquity. Wear sturdy shoes — the site is steep and the stones are polished slippery by the centuries.

Inside the Cyclopean walls, Grave Circle A is where the self-taught archaeologist Heinrich Schliemann unearthed royal tombs in 1876, finding gold death masks, inlaid swords and jewellery of staggering richness. It was in Shaft Grave V that he found the famous so-called "Mask of Agamemnon" — actually predating the Homeric king by three centuries, as we now know, but no matter: the original is in the National Archaeological Museum in Athens, and the replica in the Mycenae museum is well worth seeing.

The Treasury of Atreus, a few hundred metres from the citadel, is one of the most audacious structures of the ancient world. This tholos (beehive) tomb dates to around 1250 BC — its dromos (entrance corridor) stretches 36 metres long and 6 metres wide. Once past the threshold (the lower lintel alone weighs 120 tonnes), you step into a circular chamber 14.6 metres across and 13.5 metres high, built entirely by corbelling. This dome remained the largest in the world until the Pantheon was raised in Rome in the 2nd century AD. It had already been looted in antiquity — but the sheer scale of the space is breathtaking.

The Mycenae Museum, opened in 2007, stands at the entrance to the site. Modern and well-documented, it displays painted Mycenaean pottery, bronze weapons, jewellery and terracotta figurines recovered from local excavations. Look out in particular for the Linear B tablets — this syllabic script, the earliest form of written Greek, was only deciphered in 1952 by the British architect Michael Ventris.

activities.text: Explore the Mycenaean acropolis: climb to the royal palace at the top of the hill. The megaron (great hall) is recognisable by its four column bases. From up here, the view sweeps across the Plain of Argos and, on a clear day, all the way to the Argolic Gulf — a reminder of why this spot was chosen: absolute strategic command over the entire plain. The Cyclopean walls, up to 8 metres thick in places, are built of blocks so massive that the Classical Greeks attributed them to the Cyclopes — the only beings strong enough to lift them.

On the way back to Nafplio, stop at Tiryns, the other Mycenaean citadel inscribed by UNESCO (just 5 km from the centre of Nafplio). Less dramatic than Mycenae but boasting even better-preserved Cyclopean walls up to 8 metres thick. The interior corbelled galleries — carved entirely from unmortared blocks — rank among the most astonishing feats of prehistoric Greek architecture.

Try the real thing: homemade tzatziki in an old-town taverna in Nafplio — thick Greek yoghurt, grated and drained cucumber, garlic and dill — fresher and lighter than the tourist versions. Served as a mezze with warm pita bread and Kalamata olives, it's the opening ritual that signals a good evening ahead.

visits.text: Tripoli, the capital of Arcadia at 650 metres above sea level, deserves a brief stop for its Plateia Areos: a grand neoclassical square with fountains, open-air cafés and the archaeological museum building designed by architect Ernst Ziller — the same man who drew up the Royal Palace in Athens. Tripoli may not look like much at first glance, but it makes an ideal base for exploring the mountain heartland of Arcadia.

The Tripoli Archaeological Museum houses collections from ancient Arcadia: sculptures, inscriptions, ceramics and mosaics from local sites such as Tegea and Mantinea. The Mycenaean finds from Arcadia are displayed here too, offering a glimpse into a region that most classical itineraries overlook entirely. Allow 40 to 50 minutes.

Dimitsana, a mountain village 50 km from Tripoli, clings to a rocky spur high above the Lousios Gorge. Its cobbled lanes, stone tower-houses and abundance of churches make it one of the most beautiful villages in Arcadia. The library founded in 1764 once held 5,000 volumes before the 1821 revolution — when, according to the memoirs of Kolokotronis, the pages were torn out and used to make cartridges with locally produced gunpowder.

The Open-Air Water Power Museum of Dimitsana, opened in 1997, is the only one of its kind in Greece. It showcases restored buildings housing a flour mill, a tannery, a gunpowder mill (which supplied the 1821 revolutionaries with ammunition) and a distillery. The working hydraulic mechanisms, explained with scale models, paint a vivid picture of pre-industrial life in Arcadia.

activities.text: Hike through the Lousios Gorge between the monasteries of Prodromos and Philosophos: a 45-minute to one-hour trail squeezed between blackened limestone cliffs, above a river whose waters Pausanias once described as the coldest in Greece. The Monastery of Prodromos (16th century), still home to monks, is literally glued to the cliff face — its wooden balconies jut out over a sheer drop of several dozen metres. The Monastery of Philosophos (rebuilt in 1691) is the easier starting point.

Tasting session in Dimitsana or Stemnitsa: hilopites — small square egg noodles dried in the sun — and trachanas, a preparation of flour and fermented dried milk. These are the two quintessential mountain dishes of Arcadia, most often cooked into hearty winter soups. In summer, tavernas serve them lighter, topped with grated cheese. Arcadian fir honey, dark and thick, is among the finest in Greece.

visits.text: The archaeological site of Olympia, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1989, is the birthplace of the Olympic Games — held here without interruption from 776 BC to 393 AD, a span of 1,169 years. The River Alpheios flows alongside, and the pines and olive trees lend the site an atmosphere unlike any other major Greek sanctuary. Set aside at least two hours for a full visit. Enter from the east and walk through the Altis (the sacred grove), heading uphill towards the Temple of Zeus.

The Temple of Zeus, built around 460 BC by the architect Libon of Elis, measured 64 metres long by 28 metres wide — one of the largest Doric temples in Greece. All that remains are the columns lying where they fell, toppled by earthquakes in the 6th century AD. Inside once stood the chryselephantine statue of Zeus by Pheidias (c. 435 BC): 12 to 13 metres tall, made of gold and ivory over a wooden frame, and ranked as the third Wonder of the Ancient World. It was taken to Constantinople in the 5th century and destroyed in a fire.

The ancient Olympic Stadium is reached through the vaulted tunnel that athletes once used to make their entrance — slip through this passage and picture the 40,000 spectators waiting on the other side. The track measures 212 metres long by 28 metres wide. The marble starting line is still visible. Give the stadion a go — sprint the full length: this was the blue-ribbon event of the earliest Games.

The Workshop of Pheidias, west of the site, was definitively identified in 1958 thanks to a wine jug inscribed in Greek: "I belong to Pheidias." The workshop's dimensions match the cella of the Temple of Zeus exactly — the statue was first assembled here in separate pieces before being installed inside the temple. A Byzantine church was built over the workshop in the 5th century, then abandoned after the 6th-century earthquakes.

The Archaeological Museum of Olympia ranks among the finest in Greece — some place it on a par with the National Archaeological Museum in Athens for the quality of its collection. It holds the world's largest collection of ancient bronzes. Its star exhibits: the Hermes Carrying the Infant Dionysos by Praxiteles (4th century BC), the Nike of Paionios in Parian marble (2.11 metres tall, the first monumental winged Victory ever sculpted, dated to 421 BC), and the pediments from the Temple of Zeus. Allow at least 1 hour 15 minutes.

activities.text: Run the full length of the Olympic Stadium (212 metres) on the ancient track from the marble starting line: it's the ritual gesture every visitor makes, and it's all the more moving knowing it's exactly the same one athletes performed in the 8th century BC.

An evening stifado in a taverna in the modern village of Olympia: a stew of rabbit (or beef) simmered in red wine sauce with pearl onions, cinnamon and cloves. It's one of the finest slow-cooked dishes in Greek cuisine, and the Elis region has a particularly fragrant version. Pair it with a glass of mavrodaphne from Patras — this sweet red wine, produced just 90 km away, is reminiscent of a light port.

Evening walk along the banks of the Alpheios downstream from the site: the river winds between plane trees and reeds through a landscape free of cars and tourists. This is where ancient athletes came to purify themselves before the Games.

seo.h1_title: Peloponnese Road Trip: Mycenae, Epidaurus & Olympia
seo.intro_paragraph: Start at Epidaurus and its 4th-century-BC theatre whose limestone acoustics still defy explanation, then settle into Nafplio as your base for excursions to Mycenae — Agamemnon's citadel with its 8-metre Cyclopean walls and the Treasury of Atreus, the largest dome on earth before the Pantheon in Rome.
