practical_info.tip: Bring minimum 2L of water. No mobile signal in the canyon. Arrange return from Vikos (transfer or taxi).

visits.text: Megalo Papigo and Mikro Papigo are the two most photographed villages in Zagori, separated by 3 km via a hiking trail. Their cobbled lanes, stone-and-slate houses, and centuries-old plane trees on the central square are perfect examples of the Epirote architecture classified as a UNESCO World Heritage site. Look up and you'll see the Astraka Towers dominating the landscape—vertical limestone columns connecting the summit of Mount Tymfi (2,497 m) to the Vikos Gorge.

Near Kipi, the Kalogeriko (or Plakidas) bridge is the iconic three-arched bridge of Zagori. Built in 1814 by Abbot Seraphim from the local monastery to replace a wooden bridge, it spans the Voidomatis River at 56 meters long and 6.7 meters high. Viewed from the surrounding hills, its three successive arches give it the appearance of a caterpillar in motion—a nickname that stuck. A few minutes away, the Kokkoris bridge, built in 1750, is framed by two sheer rock walls: the most spectacular setting in the region.

activities.text: Between Megalo and Mikro Papigo, the trail connecting the two villages passes by the Ovires (or Kolymbithres)—natural pools carved into the limestone rock by the Rogovo River. Legend has it that Ali Pasha himself came here to bathe. In summer, the water is cool and the cascading pools offer a unique swim. The trail easily loops the two villages in 2 hours.

practical_info.tip: The pools are well-filled in spring and early June. In summer, water levels can be low—morning is best.

activities.text: On the return route, do the Kipi bridges loop on foot (10 km, 3 hours) to discover six single-arch stone bridges. The Kipi and Koukouli area has the densest concentration of stone bridges in all of Zagori—every configuration is represented: one, two, and three arches. These bridges were the only link between villages until the first paved roads arrived in the 1950s.

practical_info.tip: Easy and well-marked. Bring water, limited shade on some sections.

visits.text: Konitsa is built amphitheater-style on a Pindus slope, overlooking the confluence of the Aoos and Voidomatis rivers. Its stone bridge is one of the largest single-arch bridges in the Balkans: 20 meters high, nearly 40 meters span, built between 1823 and 1871 by master mason Zioga Frontzo from the village of Pyrsoyianni. Cross it on foot—the wind blows hard at the arch's summit, and the masons hung a small bell underneath to keep rhythm during construction.

The Panagia Stomiou monastery sits 5 km downstream from Konitsa bridge, clinging to the rock wall of the Aoos Gorge at 650 meters altitude. Built in 1774, it was inhabited for several decades by monk Paisios, who lived there as a hermit before joining Mount Athos. The trail leading to it follows the turquoise river in a grandiose setting—swimmers bathe there in summer, though the water is ice-cold and the current strong.

activities.text: The Aoos River below Konitsa is one of Europe's last wild rivers: clear waters, pebble beds, steep gorges. In summer, local operators offer rafting on the rapids downstream from the bridge—a 2 to 3-hour descent suitable for beginners. If you prefer a calmer approach, kayaking on the peaceful stretches lets you observe the cliffs and riverside vegetation from the water.

practical_info.tip: Booking recommended in July-August.

visits.text: The Acheron is one of the five rivers of Hades in Greek mythology—and the only one that actually rises in the world of the living, in Epirus. For the ancient Greeks, this river that plunged into karst chasms before reappearing near the Ionian Sea could only be the boundary between the world of the living and the dead. Homer sends Odysseus to consult it in the Odyssey to question the seer Tiresias. The village of Glyki is the starting point for activities on the river.

A few kilometers from Mesopotamos, a hill bears the ruins of the Necromanteion of Ephyra—identified since 1958 as the oracle of the dead described by Homer in the Odyssey, though this identification remains debated among archaeologists. According to the site's interpretation, pilgrims descended into an underground labyrinth after purification and sacrifice rituals to 'receive' the souls of their dead. The 4th-century BC ruins are worth the visit, whatever interpretation you give them.

activities.text: The hike through the Acheron Gorge from Glyki is one of the most beautiful water hikes in Greece. The trail follows the river for 8 km round trip between limestone walls covered with plane trees and oleander. The water is transparent and cool—swimming possible in the natural pools along the way. Operators also offer canoe descent from Glyki, physically easier and equally spectacular.

practical_info.tip: Bring a swimsuit—the pools in the gorge are perfect for swimming. Hiking shoes essential, the trail is wet in places.

visits.text: Nikopolis—'the city of victory'—was founded in 31 BC by Octavian Augustus on the very hill where he had pitched his camp during the naval Battle of Actium against Mark Antony and Cleopatra. It's the largest ancient city in Greece at 900 hectares. Its Victory Monument still bears anchor-shaped notches where bronze rams from ships captured from the enemy were sealed—it had 36 in all. This is also where Epictetus, the slave-philosopher founder of Stoicism, taught until 125 AD.

The Archaeological Museum of Nikopolis, inaugurated in 2009, is an essential introduction before exploring the site. It presents collections chronologically: from the Battle of Actium to the city's abandonment. Don't miss the early Christian mosaics from the basilicas—some are of exceptional quality and preservation—and fragments of Augustus's votive inscription, 54 meters long.

activities.text: Preveza, 7 km south of Nikopolis, is a quietly charming Ionian port town. In the evening, the pedestrian Spiliadou street comes alive around its fish and seafood tavernas—the Ambracian Gulf before you is the same one where the fate of the Roman world was decided on September 2, 31 BC. Aktion airport is 20 km away, across the strait.

practical_info.tip: Aktion airport (PVK) serves several European cities in summer. Check schedules in advance.

seo.h1_title: Epirus & Zagori: Gorges, Stone Villages and the River of the Underworld
seo.intro_paragraph: Epirus is the Greece nobody knows: no islands, no postcard beaches, but breathtaking gorges, UNESCO-listed stone villages, a lake haunted by Ali Pasha, and the real River of the Underworld from Greek mythology. This 8-day route connects Ioannina to Preveza via Zagori and the Vikos Gorge—listed in the Guinness Book as the world's deepest canyon relative to its width—before ending at the ruins of Nikopolis, the city founded by Augustus after his victory over Mark Antony.
seo.seo_hook: Ioannina, Vikos Gorge, Acheron River and Nikopolis: 8 days in the Greece nobody knows.
featured_list: Vikos Gorge: listed in the Guinness Book as the world's deepest canyon relative to its width
Ioannina: inhabited Byzantine citadel, lake island and the legend of Ali Pasha
Acheron: the mythological river of Hades that actually rises in Epirus
Nikopolis: founded by Augustus in 31 BC after his victory over Mark Antony, Greece's largest ancient city

places_taglines.GR::ioannina: Where Byzantine ramparts meet Ottoman minarets on the shores of a lake haunted by Ali Pasha's ghost
places_taglines.GR::monodendri: A stone village perched at the precipice of the world's deepest canyon
places_taglines.GR::papigo: Twin hamlets in the shadow of limestone towers, where mountain rivers carve turquoise pools
places_taglines.GR::konitsa: Where the wildest rivers in Europe converge beneath a single-arch bridge built to defy gravity
places_taglines.GR::preveza: An Ionian harbor town seven kilometers from the ruins of Augustus's greatest triumph

highlights: Vikos Gorge: listed in the Guinness Book as the world's deepest canyon relative to its width, with walls reaching 1,000 m
Ioannina: the only city in Greece with three mosques, tied to Ali Pasha who was assassinated on the lake island in 1822
Zagori stone bridges: 60 surviving arched bridges out of 160 built in the 18th-19th centuries by Epirote master masons
Acheron River: mythological river identified in antiquity as the entrance to Hades, which actually rises in Epirus
Nikopolis: founded in 31 BC by Augustus after his victory at Actium over Mark Antony and Cleopatra, Greece's largest ancient city
